""" md""" The coefficients of the corresponding filter \begin array rcl R 3 Z & & r 3 Z^3 r 2 Z^2 r 1 Z \\ & & r 0 r 1\,Z r 2\,Z^2 r 3\,Z^3\end array are given by """ md""" \begin array rcl r 0 & & \displaystyle \int\limits 2 ^ 1 \left x 2 ^2 2\right ^2\,dx \int\limits 1 ^ 0 \left 3 2\, x^2 2\,x\right ^2\,dx \\ & & \displaystyle \int\limits 0 ^ 1 \left 3 2\, x^2 2\, x\right ^2\,dx \int\limits 1 ^ 2 \left x 2 ^2 2\right ^2\,dx \\ & \displaystyle \frac 2 3 \end array """ md""" \begin array rcl r 1 & & \displaystyle \int\limits 2 ^ 1 \left x 2 ^2 2\right \,\left 3 2\, x 1 ^2 2\, x 1 \right \,dx \\ \nonumber & & \displaystyle \int\limits 1 ^ 0 \left 3 2\, x^2 2\,x\right \,\left 3 2\, x 1 ^2 2\, x 1 \right \,dx \\ & & \displaystyle \int\limits 0 ^ 1 \left 3 2\, x^2 2\, x\right \,\left x 1 2 ^2 2\right \,dx \\ & & \displaystyle \frac 1 8 \end array """ md""" \begin array rcl r 2 & & \displaystyle \int\limits 2 ^ 1 \left x 2 ^2 2\right \,\left 3 2\, x 2 ^2 2\, x 2 \right \,dx \\ & & \displaystyle \int\limits 1 ^ 0 \left 3 2\, x^2 2\,x\right \,\left x 2 2 ^2 2\right \,dx \\ & & \displaystyle \frac 1 5 \end array """ md""" \begin array rcl r 3 & & \displaystyle \int\limits 2 ^ 1 \left x 2 ^2 2\right \,\left x 3 2 ^2 2\right \,dx \frac 1 120 \end array """ md""" The filter can be multiplied by \epsilon^2 and added, in matrix form, to the inverted matrix without disrupting its seven diagonal structure. """ spline3 σ 1 σ 3 σ σ 3 6, 4 3 σ 2 σ^2 6, 1 3 σ 1 1 σ σ 6, σ^3 6 Cubic spline function regularization3 n, ϵ "derivative regularization for cubic splines" A BandedMatrix Float64 undef, n, n , 3, 3 A band 0 . 2ϵ 3 A band 1 . A band 1 . ϵ 8 A band 2 . A band 2 . ϵ 5 A band 3 . A band 3 . ϵ 120 return A end md""" Multidimensional inverse interpolation A multidimensional interpolation problem can be converted into a series of one dimensional problems in certain cases. For example, consider interpolating a 2 D function f x,y sampled regularly in x and y , and then sampling it again in new coordinates \hat x and \hat y , which are defined by \hat x h x x,y and \hat y h y x,y . """ md""" This problem can be solved in three steps 1. For each y , interpolate from h y x,y to h y \hat x ,y using the one dimensional mapping provided by h x x,y . 2. For each y , interpolate from f x,y to f \hat x ,y , using the one dimensional mapping provided by h x x,y . 3. For each \hat x , interpolate f \hat x ,y to f \hat x ,\hat y using the one dimensional mapping provided by h y \hat x ,y . """ md""" The inverse interpolation can proceed in the reverse order 1. For each y , interpolate h y x,y to h y \hat x ,y using the one dimensional mapping provided by h x x,y . 2. For each \hat x , inverse interpolate from f \hat x ,\hat y to f \hat x ,y using the one dimensional mapping provided by h y \hat x ,y . 3. For each y , inverse interpolate from f \hat x ,y to f x,y , using the one dimensional mapping provided by h x x,y . A similar process can be applied in 3 D or higher dimensions. """ function inverse spline3 irreg Array, x1 Array, x2 Array, n Array Int d 1,1 , o 0,0 n1, n2 n m1, m2 size x1 xhat Array eltype x1 undef, m2, n1 for i2 in 1 m2 xhat i2, inverse spline3 x1 ,i2 , x2 ,i2 , n1, d d 1 , o o 1 end yhat Array eltype irreg undef, m2, n1 for i2 in 1 m2 yhat i2, inverse spline3 irreg ,i2 , x1 ,i2 , n1, d d 1 , o o 1 end reg Array eltype irreg undef, n1, n2 for i1 in 1 n1 reg i1, inverse spline3 yhat ,i1 , xhat ,i1 , n2, d d 2 , o o 2 end return reg end download "https ahay.org data ctscan circle.rsf ","circle.bin" begin slice Array Float32 undef, 512, 512 read "circle.bin", slice end download "https ahay.org data ctscan mask.rsf ","mask.bin" begin mask Array Float32 undef, 512, 512 read "mask.bin", mask end plot scan scan, title heatmap mask . scan, title title, c grays, aspect ratio equal, legend none, clim 0, 244 , border none plot scan slice, "CT scan" function rotate angle, n1, n2 degrees to radians cosa, sina cos π angle 180 , sin π angle 180 central point c1, c2 n1 1 2, n2 1 2 x1 Array Float64 undef, n1, n2 x2 Array Float64 undef, n1, n2 for i2 in 1 n2, i1 in 1 n1 x1 i1, i2 c1 i1 c1 cosa i2 c2 sina x2 i1, i2 c2 i1 c1 sina i2 c2 cosa end return x1, x2 end angle 45 x1, x2 rotate angle, 512, 512 md""" assignment Task 2 Repeat the experiment of rotating the image back and forth using `forward linear` and `inverse linear` applied to two dimensional coordinate mapping. """ md""" General inverse interpolation In the general case of irregular space locations, the basic estimation equation remains the same. Inversion typically requires multiple iterations of the conjugate gradient method. Variations of this equation, such as the preconditioning formulation \widehat \mathbf m \mathbf C m \,\mathbf F ^T\,\left \mathbf F \,\mathbf C m \,\mathbf F ^T \epsilon^2\,\mathbf I \right ^ 1 \,\mathbf d \ , offer additional possibilities for constraining the estimating model and for accelerating convergence. """ md""" Interpolation after binning To explore inverse interpolation in multiple dimensions, we will begin by analyzing the situation where the data are already on a regularly sampled grid, but some bins are empty. """ download "https ahay.org data hall horizon.asc","horizon.asc" xyz readdlm "horizon.asc" read data from a text file md""" In this case, the forward operator \mathbf F is a simple mask that identifies data at known locations. We can specify \mathbf D m as the gradient or Laplacian operator to achieve a smooth interpolation. """ function laplacian m Array, adjoint Bool n1, n2 size m l zeros eltype m , n1, n2 for i2 in 1 n2, i1 in 1 n1 if i1 1 if adjoint l i1 1, i2 m i1, i2 l i1, i2 m i1, i2 else l i1, i2 m i1, i2 m i1 1, i2 end end if i1 n1 if adjoint l i1 1, i2 m i1, i2 l i1, i2 m i1, i2 else l i1, i2 m i1, i2 m i1 1, i2 end end if i2 1 if adjoint l i1, i2 1 m i1, i2 l i1, i2 m i1, i2 else l i1, i2 m i1, i2 m i1, i2 1 end end if i2 n2 if adjoint l i1, i2 1 m i1, i2 l i1, i2 m i1, i2 else l i1, i2 m i1, i2 m i1, i2 1 end end end return l end md""" We will use the conjugate gradient method to minimize \left|\mathbf D m\,\mathbf m \right|^2 iteratively while ensuring that \mathbf m remains unchanged at the locations of the known data. Briggs, I.C., 1974. Machine contouring using minimum curvature. Geophysics, 39 1 , pp.39 48. """ function dottest forward Function, adjoint Function, m Array, d Array "Dot product test" mod similar m rand mod dat similar d rand dat println " L m ⋅d forward mod ⋅ dat " println "L' d ⋅m adjoint dat ⋅ mod " end function conjgrad forward Function, known Array Bool , x0 Array, niter Int "Conjugate gradients for minimizing |forward x |^2 under the constraint known x x0" x deepcopy x0 R forward x, false s, S similar x , similar R gnp z zero eltype x for iter in 1 niter g forward R, true apply adjoint g known . z avoid changing x at known locations G forward g, false gn g ⋅ g if iter 1 steepest descent s, S g, G else β gn gnp s g β s S G β S end gnp gn α gn S ⋅ S x α s R α S end return x end niter 200 number of iterations md""" assignment Task 3 Modify the `conjgrad` function above to output, in addition to `x`, the residual size ` R ⋅ R ` at each iteration. To visualize convergence, plot the residual size versus the number of iterations. """ md""" Interpolation without binning To test inverse interpolation without binning, we will return to the familiar example of rainfall interpolation in Switzerland. """ begin download data files download "https ahay.org data rain border.rsf ","border.bin" download "https ahay.org data rain alldata.rsf ","alldata.bin" download "https ahay.org data rain obsdata.rsf ","obsdata.bin" end begin read data border Array Float32 undef, 2, 1289 single precision array alldata Array Float32 undef, 3, 467 single precision array obsdata Array Float32 undef, 3, 100 single precision array read "border.bin", border read "alldata.bin", alldata read "obsdata.bin", obsdata end begin plot border 1, , border 2, , linewidth 2, label none scatter alldata 1, , alldata 2, , ms 2, ma 0.5, label "all stations" scatter obsdata 1, , obsdata 2, , markershape utriangle, ms 4, label "test stations", title "Switzerland Weather Stations" end md""" We will be minimizing \min\left |\mathbf F \,\mathbf m \mathbf d |^2 \epsilon^2 |\mathbf R \,\mathbf m |^2\right \ , where \mathbf d is irregular data, \mathbf m is model estimated on a regular grid, \mathbf F is forward interpolation from the regular grid to irregular locations, \epsilon is a scaling parameter, and \mathbf R is the regularization operator related to the inverse of the assumed model covariance. """ function nnint regul Array, coord d 1,1 , o 0,0 "nearest neighbor interpolation" n size regul nd size coord, 2 irreg Array eltype regul undef, nd for id in 1 nd find nearest neighbor i1 round Int, 1 coord 1,id o 1 d 1 i2 round Int, 1 coord 2,id o 2 d 2 if 0 i1 && i1 n 1 && 0 i2 && i2 n 2 irreg id regul i1,i2 end end return irreg end function nnint adjoint irreg Vector T , coord, n Vector Int d 1,1 , o 0,0 where T Real "adjoint of nearest neighbor interpolation" nd size coord, 2 regul zeros T, n 1 , n 2 for id in 1 nd find nearest neighbor i1 round Int, 1 coord 1,id o 1 d 1 i2 round Int, 1 coord 2,id o 2 d 2 if 0 i1 && i1 n 1 && 0 i2 && i2 n 2 regul i1,i2 irreg id end end return regul end rain0 zeros Float32, 371, 255 begin ϵ 0.01 regularization parameter iters 50 number of iterations end begin lat 185 185 latitude lon 127 127 longitude end exact alldata 3, md""" assignment Task 4 Increase the number of iterations in the experiment above to achieve convergence. Then, try the following modifications to see if they improve the results 1. Replace nearest neighbor interpolation with linear interpolation. 2. Replace Laplacian minimization with gradient minimization. """ function lint regul Array, coord d 1,1 , o 0,0 "bilinear interpolation" n size regul nd size coord, 2 irreg Array eltype regul undef, nd for id in 1 nd find nearest neighbor x1 1 coord 1,id o 1 d 1 x2 1 coord 2,id o 2 d 2 i1, i2 floor Int, x1 , floor Int, x2 a1, a2 x1 i1, x2 i2 b1, b2 1 a1, 1 a2 if 0 i1 && i1 n 1 && 0 i2 && i2 n 2 irreg id regul i1,i2 b1 b2 regul i1 1,i2 a1 b2 regul i1,i2 1 b1 a2 regul i1 1,i2 1 a1 a2 end end return irreg end function lint adjoint irreg Vector T , coord, n Vector Int d 1,1 , o 0,0 where T Real "adjoint of bilinear interpolation" nd size coord, 2 regul zeros T, n 1 , n 2 for id in 1 nd ADD MISSING LINES end return regul end begin forw2 x lint x, obsdata, o 185, 127 adjt2 y lint adjoint y, obsdata, 371, 255 , o 185, 127 end dottest forw2, adjt2, rain0, obsdata 3, function gradient x Array finite difference gradient operator n1, n2 size x g zeros eltype x , n1, n2, 2 for i1 in 1 n1 1, i2 in 1 n2 1 g i1, i2, 1 x i1 1, i2 x i1, i2 g i1, i2, 2 x i1, i2 1 x i1, i2 end return g end function gradient adjoint g Array finite difference gradient operator n1, n2 size g, 1 , size g, 2 x zeros eltype g , n1, n2 for i1 in 1 n1 1, i2 in 1 n2 1 x i1 1, i2 g i1, i2, 1 ADD MISSING LINES end return x end one line function with one line conditional gradient x Array, adj Bool adj ? gradient adjoint x gradient x grad0 zeros Float32, 371, 255, 2 dottest gradient, gradient adjoint, rain0, grad0 md""" assignment Task 5 Use your `binning` function from Task 1 to put the rainfall data into a regular grid. Then, apply the Laplacian minimization method from Task 3 to fill in the empty bins. Compare the output with previous results. """ md""" assignment Bonus Task Let's try an alternative approach to linear estimation, where we use preconditioning operator \mathbf P such that \mathbf P\,P ^T \mathbf C m instead of regularization operator \mathbf D such as \mathbf D ^T\,\mathbf D \mathbf C m^ 1 . We can use the helix transform to implement \mathbf P as recursive filtering on a helix. """ mutable struct HelixFilter lag Vector CartesianIndex flt Vector HelixFilter lag,flt new map CartesianIndex,lag ,flt end Base.length a HelixFilter Base.length a.lag function plot sketch coord plt plot 0, 4 , 0, 0 , label none, color blue for k in 1 5 plot plt, k 1, k 1 , 0.1, 0.1 , label none, color blue annotate plt, k 1, 0.2, L"x % k" end for k in 1 length coord plot plt, coord k , coord k , 0.2, 0 , arrow closed, linewidth 2, label none, color red, aspect ratio equal, ylim 0.2, 0.4 , border none annotate plt, coord k , 0.3, L"y % k" end return plt end plot sketch coord function nnint regul Vector T , coord, d1 1, o1 0 where T Real "Nearest neighbor interpolation" nm, nd length regul , length coord irreg similar coord for id in 1 nd f 1 coord id o1 d1 im round Int, f nearest integer irreg id 0 im && im nm ? regul im zero T end return irreg end y nnint x, coord begin forw x nnint x, obsdata, o 185, 127 adjt y nnint adjoint y, obsdata, 371, 255 , o 185, 127 end dottest forw, adjt, rain0, obsdata 3, function binning irreg Vector T , coord, n Int, d 1, o 0 where T Real nd length coord reg zeros T, n count zeros Int, n for id in 1 nd x 1 coord id o d i round Int, x , 2ϵ 3 return reg end function forward spline3 reg Array, x1 Array, x2 Array d 1,1 , o 0,0 n1, n2 size reg m1, m2 size x1 xhat Array eltype x1 undef, m2, n1 for i2 in 1 m2 xhat i2, inverse spline3 x1 ,i2 , x2 ,i2 , n1, d d 1 , o o 1 end yhat Array eltype reg undef, m2, n1 for i1 in 1 n1 yhat ,i1 forward spline3 reg i1, , xhat ,i1 , d d 2 , o o 2 end irreg similar x1 for i2 in 1 m2 irreg ,i2 forward spline3 yhat i2, , x1 ,i2 , d d 1 , o o 1 end return irreg end begin ins1 mapslices x forward spline3 x, xdense, d dx, o x0 , spars dims 1 ins2 mapslices x forward spline3 x, xdense, d dx, o x0 , in1 dims 2 serror ins2 dense end plot xdense, serror ,250 , ylim 0.15,0.15 , linewidth 2, title "Cubic Spline Interpolation Error", label none begin ps1 heatmap xdense, xdense, ins2, color grays, clim 0.5, 0.5 , title "Spline Interpolation" ps2 heatmap xdense, xdense, serror, color grays, clim 0.5, 0.5 , title "Interpolation Error" plot ps1, ps2, layout 1, 2 end rotated forward spline3 slice, x1, x2 plot scan rotated, "Rotated by angle °" back inverse spline3 rotated, x1, x2, 512, 512 plot scan back, "Rotated Back" plot scan back slice, "Error" begin n1, n2 196, 291 iline xyz 1 n1 end, 1 xline xyz 1 n1, 2 horizon reshape xyz , 3 , n1, n2 subtract mean mean sum horizon length horizon horizon . mean end plot horizon map, title heatmap iline, xline, map, title title, xlabel L" x m ", ylabel L" y m ", clim 14,14 plot horizon horizon, "Seismic Horizon" begin cut holes horizon2 deepcopy horizon horizon2 126 147, 151 172 . 0 horizon2 151 172, 51 72 . 0 horizon2 76 97, 126 147 . 0 end plot horizon horizon2, "Input" begin xmask ones Bool, size horizon xmask 126 147, 151 172 . false xmask 151 172, 51 72 . false xmask 76 97, 126 147 . false end plot horizon xmask 14, "Space Mask" dottest x laplacian x, false , x laplacian x, true , horizon, horizon function conjgrad forward Function, adjoint Function, regularization Function, d Array, x0 Array, ϵ Real, niter Int "Conjugate gradients for minimizing |forward x d|^2 ϵ^2 |regul x |^2" x deepcopy x0 R1 forward x d R2 ϵ regularization x, false nd length d s, S1, S2 similar x , similar R1 , similar R2 gnp zero eltype x for iter in 1 niter g adjoint R1 ϵ regularization R2, true block adjoint G1 forward g G2 ϵ regularization g, false gn g ⋅ g if iter 1 steepest descent s, S1, S2 g, G1, G2 else β gn gnp s g β s S1 G1 β S1 S2 G2 β S2 end gnp gn α gn S1 ⋅ S1 S2 ⋅ S2 x α s R1 α S1 R2 α S2 Rn R1 ⋅ R1 R2 ⋅ R2 length of the residual show iter, Rn end return x end filled conjgrad laplacian, xmask, horizon2, niter plot horizon filled, "Filled" map1 conjgrad forw, adjt, laplacian, obsdata 3, , rain0, ϵ, iters heatmap lat, lon, map1', title "Laplacian Regularization iters iterations ", cmap viridis predict nnint map1, alldata, o 185, 127 begin lim 10,600 correlation coefficient cc exact ⋅ predict sqrt exact ⋅ exact sqrt predict ⋅ predict scatter exact, predict, xlabel "True", ylabel "Predicted", title "Gradient Regularization, cc Float16 cc ", aspect ratio equal, xlim lim, ylim lim, label none plot lim, lim, label none end conjugate gradients with preconditioning function conjgrad precon forward Function, adjoint Function, precon Function, d Array, x0 Array, ϵ Real, niter Int "Conjugate gradients for minimizing |forward precon x d|^2 ϵ^2 |x|^2" x deepcopy x0 R1 forward precon x, false d R2 ϵ x nd length d s, S1, S2 similar x , similar R1 , similar R2 gnp zero eltype x for iter in 1 niter g precon adjoint R1 , true ϵ R2 block adjoint G1 forward precon g, false G2 ϵ g gn g ⋅ g if iter 1 steepest descent s, S1, S2 g, G1, G2 else β gn gnp s g β s S1 G1 β S1 S2 G2 β S2 end gnp gn α gn S1 ⋅ S1 S2 ⋅ S2 x α s R1 α S1 R2 α S2 Rn real R1 ⋅ R1 R2 ⋅ R2 length of the residual show iter, Rn end return precon x, false end function helix a HelixFilter, ci CartesianIndices "convert helix lags to 1 D for a given grid" middle of the grid mid CartesianIndex Tuple last ci .÷ 2 helix index of middle hmid LinearIndices ci mid from Cartesian shift to helix shift return LinearIndices ci map x x mid, a.lag . hmid end function hconvolve x, a HelixFilter, adjoint false cx, nx, na CartesianIndices x , length x , length a lag helix a, cx y deepcopy x accounting for the zero coefficient for ia in 1 na for iy in 1 lag ia nx ix iy lag ia if adjoint correlation y ix x iy a.flt ia else convolution y iy x ix a.flt ia end end end return y end function hrecursive x, a HelixFilter, adjoint false cx, nx, na CartesianIndices x , length x , length a lag helix a, cx y similar x if adjoint for ix in nx 1 1 t x ix for ia in 1 na iy ix lag ia if iy nx t a.flt ia y iy end end y ix t end else for ix in 1 nx t x ix for ia in 1 na iy ix lag ia if iy 1 t a.flt ia y iy end end y ix t end end return y end function hwilson au0 Real, auto HelixFilter, lag, niter 10, pad 5 "Wilson Burg spectral factorization on a helix" initialize filter na length lag T eltype auto.flt a HelixFilter lag,zeros T,na grid Tuple pad maximum a.lag minimum a.lag au zeros T,grid na2 length auto nc CartesianIndex grid .÷ 2 initialize autocorrelation au nc au0 for i in 1 na2 au nc auto.lag i auto.flt i au nc auto.lag i auto.flt i end b zeros T,grid b nc one T ic LinearIndices au nc for iter in 1 niter bb hrecursive au, a, false S A cc hrecursive bb, a, true S AA' ϵ 0 for i in ic 1 2 ic 1 b Causal 1 cc bi 0.5 cc i cc 2 ic i cc ic if ϵ abs bi ϵ abs bi end b i bi end show iter, ϵ b hconvolve b, a c A b for i in 1 na a.flt i b nc a.lag i end end return a end begin define helical derivative hlaplacian HelixFilter 1,0 , 0,1 , 1.0, 1.0 hlag vcat x,0 for x in 1 10 , x,1 for x in 10 0 helder hwilson 4.0, hlaplacian, hlag make sure the filter removes DC helder.flt sum helder.flt end precon x, adj hrecursive x, helder, adj

title: Triangulation
text: A Pluto.jl notebook v0.20.27 frontmatter chapter 4 section 15 order 15 image "https raw.githubusercontent.com fonsp Pluto.jl 580ab811f13d565cc81ebfa70ed36c84b125f55d demo plutodemo.gif" title "Triangulation" tags "module4", "track julia", "track material", "Pluto", "PlutoUI" layout "layout.jlhtml" using Markdown using InteractiveUtils using Plots, LaTeXStrings using DelaunayTriangulation, Random using BenchmarkTools using LinearAlgebra using IterativeSolvers for gmres using LinearMaps for LinearMap import HTTP let notebook dir DIR toc path joinpath notebook dir, "TOC Notebook.jl" Markdown.parse " ⬅️ Back to Table of Contents . open?path HTTP.escapeuri toc path " end md""" Triangulation A simple extension of linear interpolation to irregular so called unstructured grids involves intervals in 1 D, triangles in 2 D, and tetrahedra in 3 D. The problem of arranging irregularly sampled points into reasonably behaved triangles or tetrahedra is a classic computational geometry problem. """ md""" Linear interpolation on unstructured grids In the 1 D case, interpolating function f x linearly between two points a and b amounts to computing f x \approx \displaystyle \frac f a \, b x f b \, x a b a for a point x between a and b . """ function linear1 f x 1 x^2 fi x f 2 x 1 f 1 2 x plt plot f, 0, 3, label none plot plt, fi, 1, 2, label none xs 1, 1.3, 2 fs f 1 , fi 1.3 , f 2 for k in 1 3 p, fp xs k , fs k plot plt, p, p , 0, fp , color black, label none, linestyle dash plot plt, 0, p , fp, fp , color black, label none, linestyle dash scatter plt, p , fp , color black, label none, ylim 1, 7 , xlim 0.75, 2.25 , xticks xs, L"a", L"x", L"b" , yticks fs, L"f a " L"y" L"f b " , tickfontsize 16 end return plt end linear1 md""" The principle of linear interpolation easily extends to higher dimensions. In 2 D, we consider a point X falling inside a triangle with vertexes A , B , and C . The linear approximation for f X is \begin array c f X \approx \\ \displaystyle \frac f A \,T X,B,C f B \,T A,X,C f C \,T A,B,X T A,B,C \ ,\end array where T X,Y,Z denotes the area of the corresponding triangle. """ function linear2 a 0,0 b 1,1 c 1 2,3 2 x 1 4,3 4 plt plot Shape a 1 , b 1 , c 1 , a 2 , b 2 , c 2 , fillcolor white, linewidth 2, linecolor green, label none for p in a, b, c plot plt, p 1 , x 1 , p 2 , x 2 , linestyle dash, color blue, label none end scatter plt, a 1 , b 1 , c 1 , a 2 , b 2 , c 2 , color green, label none scatter plt, x 1 , x 2 , color blue, label none, box none annotate plt, a 1 0.025, a 2 0.025, text "A", green, right, 16 annotate plt, b 1 0.025, b 2 , text "B", green, left, 16 annotate plt, c 1 0.025, c 2 , text "C", green, right, 16 annotate plt, x 1 , x 2 0.08, text "X", blue, center, 16 return plt end linear2 md""" In 3 D, triangles get replaced with tetrahedra, and areas are replaced with volumes. """ md""" An efficient way to compute the area of a triangle is by considering the vector product \begin array rcl T A,B,C & & \displaystyle \frac 1 2 \,|\vec AC |\,|\vec BC |\,\sin \gamma \frac 1 2 \, \vec AC \times \vec BC 3 \\ & & \displaystyle \frac 1 2 \,\left \vec AC 1\,\vec BC 2 \vec AC 2\,\vec BC 1\right \ .\end array Defined that way, T A,B,C has a sign it is positive if the path ABC goes counter clockwise and negative if it goes clockwise. """ md""" This definition makes T A,B,C a valuable measure for testing if a given point X is inside a given triangle we simply need to compute T X,B,C , T A,X,C , and T A,B,X and check whether they have the same sign. If one of these areas is zero, the point X falls on the edge of the triangle. """ T A, B, C C 1 A 1 C 2 B 2 C 2 A 2 C 1 B 1 2 md""" assignment Task 1 theoretical If A , B , C , and D are points in a three dimensional space, how can we compute the volume of a tetrahedron with these points as vertices? How can we interpolate the value of a function at a point X inside the tetrahedron from its values at the vertices? """ md""" Linear interpolation requires several additions and multiplications per point. If a plane region contains a set of N triangulated points, how much computational effort does it take to locate a point inside one of the triangles? Imagine that we insert points into the set incrementally one by one . Every time we insert a new point, it can split one of the triangles into three or fall on an edge, splitting two neighboring triangles into four. This observation suggests a tree structure, where each triangle appearing during incremental triangulation has pointers to its two or three "children." Locating a point requires traversing the tree until we come to a branch without children. """ md""" Thus, the triangle location algorithm is 1. Let \mathcal T 0 be a big triangle that contains all the points. Set \mathcal T \mathcal T 0 . 2. If \mathcal T has no children, return \mathcal T . 3. Test all two or three children of \mathcal T to see if X is contained inside one of them. The test involves computing signed areas and comparing their signs. If \mathcal C is the child triangle that contains X , set \mathcal T \mathcal C and go to the previous step. """ md""" With randomly distributed points, we can expect \log N levels in the tree and, therefore, O \log N cost of locating a triangle. The cost of interpolating M points for a given triangulation is O M\,\log N . Similar logic works in 1 D and 3 D. """ md""" Delaunay triangulation Not all triangulations are the same. One has unique properties among many possible triangulations of N points in a plane. It is known as Delaunay triangulation. Delaunay triangulation maximizes the minimum angle among all the internal triangle angles in the triangulation. """ begin Random.seed 2025 points rand 2 for i in 1 15 end tri triangulate points DelaunayTriangulation.get edges tri function plot tri tri Triangulation, title plt Plots.plot points DelaunayTriangulation.get points tri for edge in DelaunayTriangulation.get edges tri if edge 1 1 && edge 2 1 a, b points edge 1 , points edge 2 plot plt, a 1 , b 1 , a 2 , b 2 , label none, color blue end end x, y p 1 for p in points , p 2 for p in points scatter plt, x, y, aspect ratio equal, color red, label none, box none, title title return plt end plot tri tri, "Delaunay Triangulation" md""" Delaunay triangulation is the topological dual of Voronoi tessellation division of the plane into regions polygons that correspond to the nearest points. """ vorn voronoi tri clip true function plot voronoi vorn VoronoiTessellation, tri Triangulation, points vertices DelaunayTriangulation.get polygon points vorn polygons DelaunayTriangulation.get polygons vorn x, y p 1 for p in points , p 2 for p in points plt scatter x, y, label none for polygon in polygons x1 vertices p 1 for p in polygon 2 x2 vertices p 2 for p in polygon 2 plot plt, Shape x1, x2 , label none end for edge in DelaunayTriangulation.get edges tri if edge 1 1 && edge 2 1 a, b points edge 1 , points edge 2 plot plt, a 1 , b 1 , a 2 , b 2 , label none, color black, linestyle dash end end scatter plt, x, y, aspect ratio equal, color white, label none, box none return plt end plot voronoi vorn, tri, points md""" A defining property of Delaunay triangulation is that a circle that goes through the vertexes of any triangle in the triangulation does not contain any other points from the triangulation. How can we test computationally if a given point is inside the circle drawn through three other points? """ md""" A clever computational trick is to map the 2 D space to 3 D using the surface of a paraboloid x 3 x 1^2 x 2^2 . Imagine an intersection of the paraboloid with a plane x 3 a\,x 1 b\,x 2 c for c 0 . The following equation gives the projection of the intersecting curve to a plane x 1^2 x 2^2 a\,x 1 b\,x 2 c\ , which is the equation of the circle \left x 1 \frac a 2 \right ^2 \left x 2 \frac b 2 \right ^2 r^2 with r^2 c a^2 4 b^2 4 . """ md""" The converse is true if three points A , B , and C belong to a circle in 2 D, their projection to the paraboloid surface will belong to a common plane. The fourth point, D , will project above the plane if the point is outside the circle and below the plane if the point is inside the circle. """ md""" The orientation below or above the plane is easy to compute using the signed volume of the projected ABCD tetrahedron, which is given by the 4 \times 4 determinant V \left|\begin array llll A 1 & A 2 & A 1^2 A 2^2 & 1 \\ B 1 & B 2 & B 1^2 B 2^2 & 1 \\ C 1 & C 2 & C 1^2 C 2^2 & 1 \\ D 1 & D 2 & D 1^2 D 2^2 & 1 \end array \right|\ , and can be computed using only elementary operations additions and multiplications . """ md""" The determinant equation gives us a method for determining if a counter clockwise polygon ABCD belongs to Delaunay triangulation when divided into two triangles by the edge AC , which creates triangles ABC and ACD . If that is not the case, replacing AC with BD to create Delaunay conforming triangles ABD and DBC is sufficient for fixing the triangulation. """ function plot flip A cos 4 π 3 ,sin 4 π 3 C cos π 4 ,sin π 4 B cos π 6 , sin π 6 D 0.6 cos 0.6 π ,0.6 sin 0.6 π z1 x,y x^2 y^2 1 x range 1.2, 1.2, length 100 z . z1 x', x p1 plot Shape A 1 , C 1 , D 1 , A 2 , C 2 , D 2 , fillcolor white, linewidth 2, label none plot p1, Shape A 1 , B 1 , C 1 , A 2 , B 2 , C 2 , fillcolor white, linewidth 2, label none contour p1, x, x, z, linestyle dash, levels 0 , cbar false scatter p1, A 1 , B 1 , C 1 , D 1 , A 2 , B 2 , C 2 , D 2 , label none, title "Not Delaunay" annotate p1, A 1 0.2, A 2 , "A" annotate p1, B 1 0.2, B 2 , "B" annotate p1, C 1 0.2, C 2 , "C" annotate p1, D 1 0.2, D 2 , "D" z2 x,y x 0.0634027995459717 ^2 y 0.236622469247671 ^2 0.713570768539733 z . z2 x', x p2 plot Shape A 1 , B 1 , D 1 , A 2 , B 2 , D 2 , fillcolor white, alpha 1, linewidth 2, label none plot p2, Shape D 1 , B 1 , C 1 , D 2 , B 2 , C 2 , fillcolor white, alpha 1, linewidth 2, label none contour p2, x, x, z, linestyle dash, levels 0 , cbar false scatter p2, A 1 , B 1 , C 1 , D 1 , A 2 , B 2 , C 2 , D 2 , label none, title "Delaunay" annotate p2, A 1 0.2, A 2 , "A" annotate p2, B 1 0.2, B 2 , "B" annotate p2, C 1 0.2, C 2 , "C" annotate p2, D 1 0.2, D 2 , "D" return plot p1, p2, layout 1, 2 , box none, aspect ratio equal end plot flip md""" The determinant approach suggests a constructive algorithm 1. Look through all the edges in the triangulation. 2. For any edge that connects two triangles, check if this is a valid Delaunay edge. If not, flip it. 3. Repeat. The algorithm eventually terminates because every edge flip increases the minimum angle of the two adjacent triangles, and these angles can increase through the finite number of possible triangulations only until the Delaunay triangulation is reached. However, achieving this state may take many steps. """ md""" Incremental algorithm To make the algorithm more systematic, we can insert points one by one, each time locating the point inside the current triangulation using O \log N operations thanks to the previously described tree structure. When a point is inserted, it can either a fall inside one of the triangles, thus creating three new edges and three new triangles, or b fall on an edge, creating two new edges and four new triangles. New edges need to be checked as before and flipped as necessary. Next, the adjacent edges are checked, and the process repeats recursively until the Delaunay triangulation is achieved. """ md""" In the worst case scenario, we may need to rebuild the whole triangulation every time we insert a new point, leading to a slow O N^2 algorithm. However, it turns out that if points are inserted in a random order, the expected number of edge flips is constant and does not depend on N . Thus, the randomized incremental algorithm for Delaunay triangulation requires only O N\,\log N operations the \log N factor coming from point location and O N storage. Similar ideas work in 3 D for tetrahedra but, unfortunately, not in higher dimensions. """ begin rtri deepcopy tri refine rtri min angle 25 end begin p1 plot tri tri, "Initial Triangulation" p2 plot tri rtri, "Refinement" plot p1, p2, layout 1, 2 end md""" Applying triangulation to the rainfall data To test linear interpolation by triangulation, we return to the now familiar example of the day of rainfall in Switzerland. """ begin download data files download "https ahay.org data rain alldata.rsf ","alldata.bin" download "https ahay.org data rain obsdata.rsf ","obsdata.bin" end begin read the data alldata Array Float32 undef, 3, 467 single precision array obsdata Array Float32 undef, 3, 100 single precision array read "alldata.bin", alldata read "obsdata.bin", obsdata end begin lat 185 185 latitude lon 127 127 longitude end four corners of the map corners lat 1 ,lon 1 , lat 1 ,lon end , lat end ,lon 1 , lat end ,lon end weather station coordinates corners stations vcat obsdata 1 2,k for k in 1 size obsdata, 2 , corners tri rain triangulate stations plot tri tri rain, "Trianguled Swiss Weather Stations" linear interpolation using triangulation function lint tri Triangulation, points, data, X a, b, c find triangle tri, X A, B, C points a , points b , points c return data a T X,B,C data b T A,X,C data c T A,B,X T A,B,C end md""" To check interpolation accuracy, we can utilize the forward interpolation from the map using the bilinear approach. """ function lint regul, coord d 1,1 , o 0,0 "bilinear interpolation" n1, n2 size regul nd size coord, 2 irreg zeros eltype regul , nd inbounds for id in 1 nd find nearest neighbor x1 1 coord 1,id o 1 d 1 x2 1 coord 2,id o 2 d 2 i1, i2 floor Int, x1 , floor Int, x2 if 0 i1 n1 && 0 i2 n2 a1, a2 x1 i1, x2 i2 b1, b2 1 a1, 1 a2 irreg id regul i1,i2 b1 b2 regul i1 1,i2 a1 b2 regul i1,i2 1 b1 a2 regul i1 1,i2 1 a1 a2 end end return irreg end function linmap tri Triangulation, points, data n1, n2 length lat , length lon map similar data, n1, n2 add four corners pad vcat data, zeros eltype data , 4 for i in 1 n1, j in 1 n2 point lat i , lon j map i, j lint tri, points, pad, point end return map end map linmap tri rain, stations, obsdata 3, heatmap lat, lon, map', title "Triangulation", cmap viridis btime linmap tri rain, stations, obsdata 3, begin exact alldata 3, predict lint map, alldata o lat 1 , lon 1 end begin lim 10, 600 cc exact ⋅ predict sqrt exact ⋅ exact sqrt predict ⋅ predict scatter exact, predict, xlabel "True", ylabel "Predicted", title "Triangulation, cc Float16 cc ", aspect ratio equal, xlim lim, ylim lim, label none plot lim, lim, label none end md""" From triangulation to shaping The triangulation result captures the general behavior of the rainfall map, but it is not smooth. To reconstruct a smooth map, we can apply shaping iterations. \mathbf m n 1 \mathbf S m\left \mathbf B\,d \mathbf I B\,F \,\mathbf m n\right \ , where \mathbf m n is the map at n th iteration, \mathbf d is measured rainfall, \mathbf F is forward interpolation from a map to irregular locations, \mathbf B is backward interpolation by triangulation, and \mathbf S is smoothing. """ import LocalSignalAttributes radius 15, 15 smoothing radius in samples begin forward x lint x, obsdata o lat 1 , lon 1 backward y linmap tri rain, stations, y shaping x LocalSignalAttributes.smooth x, radius end function shape forward Function, backward Function, shaping Function, x0 Array, niter Int x deepcopy x0 for iter in 1 niter x shaping x0 x backward forward x end return x end smap shape forward, backward, shaping, map, 10 heatmap lat, lon, smap', title "Triangulation Shaping", cmap viridis spredict lint smap, alldata, o lat 1 , lon 1 begin ccs exact ⋅ spredict sqrt exact ⋅ exact sqrt spredict ⋅ spredict scatter exact, spredict, xlabel "True", ylabel "Predicted", title "Triangulation Shaping, cc Float16 ccs ", aspect ratio equal, xlim lim, ylim lim, label none plot lim, lim, label none end md""" assignment Task 2 Find a way to measure the convergence of shaping iterations. Determine how many iterations are needed for convergence in this example. """ md""" assignment Task 3 To improve the convergence further, we can notice that the method calls for inverting \mathbf m \infty \left \mathbf I \mathbf S m\, \mathbf B\,F I \right ^ 1 \,\mathbf S m\,\mathbf B\,d \ . The inverted matrix is not symmetric. Therefore, the conjugate gradient algorithm doesn't help. However, we can use a different iterative algorithm, such as GMRES. Saad, Y. and Schultz, M.H., 1986. GMRES A generalized minimal residual algorithm for solving nonsymmetric linear systems https epubs.siam.org doi abs 10.1137 0907058 . SIAM Journal on scientific and statistical computing, 7 3 , pp.856 869. """ begin x 1,2,3,4 A 1 1 1 0 1 2 0 0 1 3 1 0 1 4 0 1 b A x end f x A x assert x ≈ gmres Float32. A , Float32. b size A , size b use a function wrapped in LinearMap instead of a matrix assert x ≈ gmres LinearMap x A x, length x , Float32. b , maxiter 5, verbose true reshape x, 2,2 function shaping operator x AbstractVector wrap I S BF I operator for abstract vectors x2 reshape x, size map y2 x2 shaping backward forward x2 x2 return vec y2 end lin operator LinearMap Float32 shaping operator, length map md""" Your task Implement shaping regularized interpolation with GMRES and compare the result with that from simple iterations. """ md""" References Delaunay triangulation Delaunay, 1934 and related algorithms are described in computational geometry textbooks de Berg et al., 2008 . For an elementary introduction with code examples, see O’Rourke, 1998 . """ md""" de Berg, M., O. Cheong, M. van Kreveld, and M. Overmars, 2008, Computational geometry Algorithms and applications, 3rd ed. Springer. Delaunay, B. N., 1934, la sphère vide Izv. Akad. Nauk SSSR, Otdel. Mat. Est. Nauk, 7, 793–800. O’Rourke, J., 1998, Computational geometry in C, 2nd ed. Cambridge University Press. """ md""" The randomized incremental algorithm for Delaunay triangulation was developed by Guibas et al. 1992 . Alternative methods with similar O N\,\log N complexity include the divide and conquer algorithm Shamos & Hoey, 1975 and the sweepline algorithm Fortune, 1987 . """ md""" Fortune, S., 1987, A sweepline algorithm for Voronoi diagram Algorithmica, 2, 153–174. Guibas, L. J., D. Knuth, and M. Sharir, 1992, Randomized incremental construction of Delaunay and Voronoi diagrams Algorithmica, 7, 381–413. Shamos, M., and D. Hoey, 1975, Closest point problems, in Proceedings 16th Annual IEEE Sympos. Found. Comput. Sci., 151–162. """ md""" Geometrical algorithms such as Delaunay triangulation often require computations with adaptive precision Shewchuk, 1996 . Adaptive precision is needed, for example, to determine the sign of the determinant beyond the precision limits when the determinant is close to zero. Shewchuk, J. R., 1996, Robust adaptive floating point geometric predicates, in 12th Annual Symposium on Computational Geometry Association for Computing Machinery, 141–150. """

title: Shaping Regularization
text: A Pluto.jl notebook v0.20.27 frontmatter chapter 4 section 16 order 16 image "https raw.githubusercontent.com fonsp Pluto.jl 580ab811f13d565cc81ebfa70ed36c84b125f55d demo plutodemo.gif" title "Shaping Regularization" tags "module4", "track julia", "track material", "Pluto", "PlutoUI" layout "layout.jlhtml" using Markdown using InteractiveUtils begin using PlutoUIExtra TableOfContents end using LinearAlgebra defines dot product as "⋅" using Plots using Random using BenchmarkTools using DelaunayTriangulation using IterativeSolvers, LinearMaps for gmres import HTTP let notebook dir DIR toc path joinpath notebook dir, "TOC Notebook.jl" Markdown.parse " ⬅️ Back to Table of Contents . open?path HTTP.escapeuri toc path " end md""" Shaping regularization So far, our discussion of estimation problems has focused on the optimization least squares framework within statistical linear estimation theory. This chapter will examine an alternative framework known as shaping regularization . We will derive a different form of the estimation operator and then uncover connections between the shaping and optimization frameworks. """ md""" Going forward and backward As before, suppose that the data what we measure \mathbf d is connected to the model what we want \mathbf m through the forward operator \mathbf F \mathbf d F\,m \ . Also, suppose that we can construct an efficient backward approximate inverse operator for going back from the data space to the model space \tilde \mathbf m \mathbf B\,d \ . A particular example of \mathbf B is the adjoint operator \mathbf F ^T in the linear case or the adjoint of the derivative of \mathbf F if \mathbf F is nonlinear. However, the concept is more general. The pair of \mathbf F and \mathbf B allows for the following constructive iterative inversion procedure. Putting the forward and backward equations together, we arrive at the equation \tilde \mathbf m \mathbf B\,F\,m \ , which can be solved by simple iteration \mathbf m n 1 \mathbf m n \tilde \mathbf m \mathbf B\,F\,m n \tilde \mathbf m \mathbf I B\,F \,\mathbf m n\ , starting with some \mathbf m 0 . The iteration converges to a solution of the original equation provided that \mathbf B\,F is sufficiently close to the identity operator. Alternatively, we could make a chain \mathbf F\,B and iterate \mathbf d n 1 \mathbf d n \mathbf d \mathbf F\,B\,d n \mathbf d \mathbf I F\,B \,\mathbf d n\ , starting with \mathbf d 0 \mathbf d and completing the iteration by computing \mathbf m N \mathbf B\,d N . """ md""" Introducing shaping Not all information about the model is contained in the data. Additional constraints may come from other sources of information. Let us introduce a model shaping operator \mathbf S m . Shaping is applied to an estimated model to transform it into a model that satisfies additional constraints. Model shaping can be incorporated into the iteration as follows \mathbf m n 1 \mathbf S m\left \tilde \mathbf m \mathbf I B\,F \,\mathbf m n\right \ . If \mathbf F , \mathbf B , and \mathbf S m are linear and if the iteration converges to \mathbf m \infty , then the following condition is satisfied \mathbf m \infty \mathbf S m\left \tilde \mathbf m \mathbf I B\,F \,\mathbf m \infty \right or \mathbf m \infty \left \mathbf I \mathbf S m\, \mathbf B\,F I \right ^ 1 \,\mathbf S m\,\tilde \mathbf m \left \mathbf I \mathbf S m\, \mathbf B\,F I \right ^ 1 \,\mathbf S m\,\mathbf B\,d \ . Alternatively, we can incorporate data domain constraints into the data space iteration via a data shaping operator \mathbf S d . The iteration becomes \mathbf d n 1 \mathbf S d\left \mathbf d n \mathbf d \mathbf F\,B\,d n\right \ . If it converges and if \mathbf F , \mathbf B , and \mathbf S d are linear operators, then, analogously to the model space iteration, \mathbf d \infty \left \mathbf I \mathbf S d\, \mathbf F\,B I \right ^ 1 \, \mathbf S d\,\mathbf d and \mathbf m \infty \mathbf B \,\left \mathbf I \mathbf S d\, \mathbf F\,B I \right ^ 1 \, \mathbf S d\,\mathbf d \ . The model space and data space inversion equations complete the definition of shaping regularization as an alternative to optimization. """ md""" Connection with least squares optimization At first glance, the shaping equations look very different from the estimation equations we used earlier. However, their algebraic equivalence can be established under certain conditions. In particular, the model space equation becomes algebraically equivalent to our previous equation \widehat \mathbf m \left \mathbf F ^T\,\mathbf C n^ 1 \,\mathbf F \mathbf C m ^ 1 \right ^ 1 \,\mathbf F ^T\,\mathbf C n^ 1 \,\mathbf d if we make the following choices \begin array rcl \mathbf B & & \mathbf F ^T\,\mathbf C n^ 1 \ , \\ \mathbf S m & & \left \mathbf I \mathbf C m^ 1 \right ^ 1 \ .\end array """ md""" Analogously, the data space shaping is equivalent to the previous equation \widehat \mathbf m \mathbf C m \,\mathbf F ^T\,\left \mathbf F \,\mathbf C m \,\mathbf F ^T \mathbf C n\right ^ 1 \,\mathbf d if we choose \begin array rcl \mathbf B & & \mathbf C m\,\mathbf F ^T\ , \\ \mathbf S d & & \left \mathbf I \mathbf C n\right ^ 1 \ .\end array The algebraic equivalence is proven by direct substitution. Why would we prefer a particular expression if an alternative produces algebraically equivalent results? To make a practical choice, we need to decide 1. Which operator is easier to specify \mathbf C m , \mathbf C m^ 1 , or \mathbf S m \mathbf C d , \mathbf C d^ 1 , or \mathbf S d ? 2. Which matrix is easier to invert taking fewer iterations with an iterative method such as conjugate gradients ? Different data analysis applications may lead to different choices. """ md""" Proximity operators Is there a deeper meaning to choosing model shaping in the form \left \mathbf I \mathbf C m^ 1 \right ^ 1 ? Notice that this operator resembles the estimation operator for the case \mathbf F \mathbf C n \mathbf I . Using the previously established connection to least squares inversion, we can say that model shaping in the form \mathbf m \left \mathbf I \mathbf C m^ 1 \right ^ 1 \,\mathbf m 0 results from minimizing the least squares objective function \left|\mathbf m \mathbf m 0\right|^2 \left|\mathbf D m\,\mathbf m \right|^2\, with \mathbf D m^T\,\mathbf D m \mathbf C m^ 1 . Analogously, the data shaping operator \mathbf d \left \mathbf I \mathbf C n\right ^ 1 \,\mathbf d 0 can be understood as the result of minimizing |\mathbf d |^2 |\mathbf x |^2 for \mathbf d and \mathbf x under the constraints \mathbf d \mathbf P n\,\mathbf x \mathbf d 0 and \mathbf P n\,\mathbf P n^T \mathbf C n . For a general convex functional R applied to the model, the proximity operator \mathbf P R is defined as the following minimizer \mathbf P R \mathbf m 0 \arg \min \mathbf m \left \left|\mathbf m \mathbf m 0\right|^2 R \mathbf m \right \ . The proximity operator shapes the input model \mathbf m 0 by penalizing unwanted features. Thus, the shaping operator is the proximity operator for R \mathbf m \left|\mathbf D m\,\mathbf m \right|^2 . The general notion of proximity operators extends the connection between the optimization framework and the shaping framework to the case of nonlinear regularizations. """ md""" Shaping conjugate gradients The versions of conjugate gradients that we considered before was concerned with solving the problem of minimizing \left|\mathbf A \,\mathbf x \mathbf b \right|^2 , equivalent to solving \mathbf A ^T\,\mathbf A\,x \mathbf A ^T\,\mathbf b . If we think of conjugate gradients as a method of solving the general linear system \mathbf B\,x \mathbf d with a positive definite matrix \mathbf B , the algorithm takes the form implemented below. """ function conjgrad operator Function, d Array, x0 Array, niter Int "Conjugate gradient algorithm for solving B x d" x deepcopy x0 g operator x d s, S similar x , similar d gnp zero eltype x for iter in 1 niter G operator g gn g ⋅ g show iter, gn if iter 1 steepest descent s, S g, G else β gn gnp s g β s S G β S end gnp gn α gn S ⋅ s x α s g α S end return x end md""" The matrix inverted in the shaping equation is not symmetric. However, if we assume the possibility of the symmetric splitting \mathbf S m \mathbf H \,\mathbf H ^T and the invertability of \mathbf H , we can rewrite the equation in a more symmetric form, as follows \mathbf m \infty \left \mathbf I \mathbf S m\, \mathbf B\,F I \right ^ 1 \,\mathbf S m\,\mathbf B\,d \mathbf H \,\left \mathbf I \mathbf H ^T\, \mathbf B\,F I \,\mathbf H \right ^ 1 \,\mathbf H ^T\,\mathbf B\,d \ . If, additionally, we select the backward operator \mathbf B as \mathbf B \mathbf F ^T\,\mathbf C n^ 1 , the inverted matrix becomes suitable for an application of conjugate gradients. """ md""" assignment Task 1 theoretical Assuming a symmetric splitting for the data shaping operator \mathbf S d \mathbf H d^T\,\mathbf H d , find a symmetric form of the data shaping equation \mathbf m \infty \mathbf B \,\left \mathbf I \mathbf S d\, \mathbf F\,B I \right ^ 1 \, \mathbf S d\,\mathbf d """ function conjgrad forward Function, adjoint Function, shaping Function, d Array, p0 Array, niter 1, ϵ 1.0 "Conjugate gradient algorithm for shaping regularization" p deepcopy p0 x shaping p r forward x . d sp, sx, sr similar p , similar x , similar r gnp, g0 zero Float64 , zero Float64 for iter in 1 niter gx adjoint r ϵ x gp shaping gx ϵ p gx shaping gp gr forward gx gn gp ⋅ gp show iter, gn if iter 1 g0 gn sp gp sx gx sr gr else γ gn g0 β gn gnp sp gp β sp sx gx β sx sr gr β sr end gnp gn α sr ⋅ sr ϵ sp ⋅ sp sx ⋅ sx α gn α p p α sp x x α sx r r α sr end return x end md""" The code implements the conjugate gradient algorithm for finding the solution in case of \mathbf B \displaystyle \mathbf F ^T \epsilon , which corresponds to \mathbf m \infty \mathbf H \,\left \epsilon\,\mathbf I \mathbf H ^T\, \mathbf F ^T\,\mathbf F \epsilon\,\mathbf I \,\mathbf H \right ^ 1 \,\mathbf H ^T\,\mathbf B\,d \ . The program keeps track of three linearly dependent vectors 1. the auxiliary vector \mathbf p , which converges to \mathbf A ^ 1 \,\mathbf H ^T\,\mathbf F ^T\,\mathbf d , where \mathbf A \epsilon\,\mathbf I \mathbf H ^T\, \mathbf F ^T\,\mathbf F \epsilon\,\mathbf I \,\mathbf H \ . 2. the solution vector \mathbf x H\,p , 3. the residual vector \mathbf r F\,x d . The three vectors are updated by adding steps of sizes \mathbf s p , \mathbf s x \mathbf H\,s p , and \mathbf s r \mathbf F\,s p , respectively. To simplify computations, the program also takes note of the fact that the gradient vector is given by \mathbf g p \mathbf A \,\mathbf p \mathbf H ^T\,\mathbf F ^T\,\mathbf d \epsilon\,\mathbf p \mathbf H ^T\, \mathbf F ^T\,\mathbf r \epsilon\,\mathbf x and that the product \mathbf s p^T\mathbf A \,\mathbf s p simplifies to \mathbf s p^T\mathbf A \,\mathbf s p \mathbf s p^T\,\left \epsilon\,\mathbf I \mathbf H ^T\, \mathbf F ^T\,\mathbf F \epsilon\,\mathbf I \,\mathbf H \right \,\mathbf s p \epsilon\,\mathbf s p^T\mathbf s p \mathbf s r^T\mathbf s r \epsilon\,\mathbf s x^T\mathbf s x\ . Note that each iteration of conjugate gradients in this case requires one application of \mathbf F and \mathbf F ^T and one application of \mathbf H and \mathbf H ^T . """ md""" Computational example 1997 Spatial Interpolation Contest """ import GMT GMT.coast proj Mercator, DCW country "CH" , title "Switzerland", show true md""" In 1997, the European Communities organized a Spatial Interpolation Comparison. Many organizations participated with the results published in a special issue of the Journal of Geographic Information and Decision Analysis and a separate report. """ md""" Dubois, G., 1999, Spatial interpolation comparison 97 Foreword and introduction Journal of Geographic Information and Decision Analysis, 2, 1–10. Dubois, G., J. Malczewski, and M. D. Cort, eds., 2003, Mapping radioactivity in the environment. Spatial Interpolation Comparison 1997. Office for Official Publications of the European Communities. """ begin download data files download "https ahay.org data rain border.rsf ","border.bin" download "https ahay.org data rain alldata.rsf ","alldata.bin" download "https ahay.org data rain obsdata.rsf ","obsdata.bin" end begin read data border Array Float32 undef, 2, 1289 single precision array read "border.bin", border end begin alldata Array Float32 undef, 3, 467 single precision array obsdata Array Float32 undef, 3, 100 single precision array read "alldata.bin", alldata read "obsdata.bin", obsdata end begin plot border 1, , border 2, , linewidth 2, label none scatter alldata 1, , alldata 2, , ms 2, ma 0.5, label "all stations" scatter obsdata 1, , obsdata 2, , markershape utriangle, ms 4, label "test stations", title "Switzerland Weather Stations" end md""" The comparison used a dataset from rainfall measurements in Switzerland on the 8th of May 1986, the day of the Chornobyl disaster. A total of 467 rainfall measurements were taken that day. A randomly selected subset of 100 measurements was used as the input data in the 1997 Spatial Interpolation Comparison to interpolate other measurements using different techniques and to compare the results with the known data. """ md""" Gradient regularization Our first approach is to interpolate by solving the regularized least squares optimization problem \min\left |\mathbf F \,\mathbf m \mathbf d |^2 \epsilon^2 |\mathbf R \,\mathbf m |^2\right \ , where \mathbf d is irregular data, \mathbf m is model estimated on a regular grid, \mathbf F is forward interpolation from the regular grid to irregular locations, \epsilon is a scaling parameter, and \mathbf R is the regularization operator related to the inverse of the assumed model covariance. In our experiment, \mathbf R will be the finite difference gradient filter. """ function conjgrad forward Function, adjoint Function, regularization Function, d Array, x0 Array, ϵ Real, niter Int "Conjugate gradients for minimizing |forward x d|^2 ϵ^2 |regul x |^2" x deepcopy x0 R1 forward x d R2 ϵ regularization x, false s, S1, S2 similar x , similar R1 , similar R2 gnp zero eltype x for iter in 1 niter g adjoint R1 ϵ regularization R2, true block adjoint G1 forward g G2 ϵ regularization g, false gn g ⋅ g if iter 1 steepest descent s, S1, S2 g, G1, G2 else β gn gnp s g β s S1 G1 β S1 S2 G2 β S2 end gnp gn α gn S1 ⋅ S1 S2 ⋅ S2 x α s R1 α S1 R2 α S2 Rn R1 ⋅ R1 R2 ⋅ R2 length of the residual show iter, Rn end return x end function dottest forward Function, adjoint Function, m Array, d Array "Dot product test" mod similar m rand mod dat similar d rand dat println " L m ⋅d forward mod ⋅ dat " println "L' d ⋅m adjoint dat ⋅ mod " end function gradient x Array finite difference gradient operator n1, n2 size x g zeros eltype x , n1, n2, 2 inbounds for i1 in 1 n1 1, i2 in 1 n2 1 g i1, i2, 1 x i1 1, i2 x i1, i2 g i1, i2, 2 x i1, i2 1 x i1, i2 end return g end function gradient adjoint g Array finite difference gradient operator n1, n2 size g, 1 , size g, 2 x zeros eltype g , n1, n2 inbounds for i1 in 1 n1 1, i2 in 1 n2 1 x i1 1, i2 g i1, i2, 1 x i1, i2 1 g i1, i2, 2 x i1, i2 g i1, i2, 1 g i1, i2, 2 end return x end rain0 zeros Float32, 371, 255 grad0 zeros Float32, 371, 255, 2 one line function with one line conditional gradient x Array, adj Bool adj ? gradient adjoint x gradient x dottest gradient, gradient adjoint, rain0, grad0 function lint regul Array, coord d 1,1 , o 0,0 "bilinear interpolation" n1, n2 size regul nd size coord, 2 irreg zeros eltype regul , nd for id in 1 nd find nearest neighbor x1 1 coord 1,id o 1 d 1 x2 1 coord 2,id o 2 d 2 i1, i2 floor Int, x1 , floor Int, x2 if 0 i1 n1 && 0 i2 n2 a1, a2 x1 i1, x2 i2 b1, b2 1 a1, 1 a2 irreg id regul i1,i2 b1 b2 regul i1 1,i2 a1 b2 regul i1,i2 1 b1 a2 regul i1 1,i2 1 a1 a2 end end return irreg end function lint adjoint irreg Vector T , coord, n Vector Int d 1,1 , o 0,0 where T Real "adjoint of bilinear interpolation" nd size coord, 2 regul zeros T, n 1 , n 2 for id in 1 nd find nearest neighbor x1 1 coord 1,id o 1 d 1 x2 1 coord 2,id o 2 d 2 i1, i2 floor Int, x1 , floor Int, x2 if 0 i1 n 1 && 0 i2 n 2 a1, a2 x1 i1, x2 i2 b1, b2 1 a1, 1 a2 regul i1,i2 irreg id b1 b2 regul i1 1,i2 irreg id a1 b2 regul i1,i2 1 irreg id b1 a2 regul i1 1,i2 1 irreg id a1 a2 end end return regul end begin lat 185 185 latitude lon 127 127 longitude nlat, nlon length lat , length lon end ϵ 0.01 regularization parameter function plot corr predict Vector, title String lim 10,600 exact alldata 3, correlation coefficient cc exact ⋅ predict sqrt exact ⋅ exact sqrt predict ⋅ predict scatter exact, predict, xlabel "True", ylabel "Predicted", title " title, cc Float16 cc ", aspect ratio equal, xlim lim, ylim lim, label none plot lim, lim, label none end md""" assignment Task 2 Find out experimentally how many iterations are needed for the conjugate gradient algorithm above to converge. """ md""" Shaping regularization Our second approach will use shaping regularization, defining model shaping as smoothing. """ import LocalSignalAttributes radius 15, 15 smoothing radius in samples shaping x LocalSignalAttributes.smooth x, radius smoothing is self adjoint dottest shaping, shaping, rain0, rain0 md""" assignment Task 3 Create an animation showing how the rainfall produced by shaping regularization changes with the number of iterations. To learn how to create animations in Julia, you can check the documentation as follows ```julia ? gif ``` """ myplot k plot sin, 0, k, legend none function definition gif for k in 1 10 create an animated gif using 10 frames myplot k end md""" assignment Task 4 We can use either accuracy or efficiency metrics to compare alternative numerical methods for solving the same problem. The BenchmarkTools https juliaci.github.io BenchmarkTools.jl stable Julia package provides a convenient way of comparing efficiency. Use benchmark or btime functions from BenchmarkTools to compare the efficiency of gradient regularization versus shaping regularization. """ md""" Triangulation Let us now try to be more creative with the backward operator used in shaping regularization. We will experiment with triangulated linear interpolation. """ four corners of the map corners lat 1 ,lon 1 , lat 1 ,lon end , lat end ,lon 1 , lat end ,lon end weather station coordinates corners stations vcat obsdata 1 2,k for k in 1 size obsdata, 2 , corners tri rain triangulate stations function plot tri tri Triangulation, title plt Plots.plot points DelaunayTriangulation.get points tri for edge in DelaunayTriangulation.get edges tri if edge 1 1 && edge 2 1 a, b points edge 1 , points edge 2 plot plt, a 1 , b 1 , a 2 , b 2 , label none, color blue end end x, y p 1 for p in points , p 2 for p in points scatter plt, x, y, aspect ratio equal, color red, label none, box none, title title return plt end plot tri tri rain, "Trianguled Swiss Weather Stations" triangle area T A, B, C C 1 A 1 C 2 B 2 C 2 A 2 C 1 B 1 2 linear interpolation using triangulation function lint tri Triangulation, points, data, X a, b, c find triangle tri, X A, B, C points a , points b , points c return data a T X,B,C data b T A,X,C data c T A,B,X T A,B,C end begin forward x lint x, obsdata, o lat 1 , lon 1 adjoint y lint adjoint y, obsdata, nlat, nlon , o lat 1 , lon 1 end begin x 1,2,3,4 x0 zeros 4 A 1 1 1 0 1 2 0 0 1 3 1 0 1 4 0 1 1 5 1 1 B A' A d B x y A x end assert x ≈ conjgrad x B x, float d , x0, 5 assert x ≈ conjgrad x A x, x A' x, x x, float y , x0, 5 dottest forward, adjoint, rain0, obsdata 3, map10 conjgrad forward, adjoint, gradient, obsdata 3, , rain0, ϵ, 10 heatmap lat, lon, map10', title "Gradient Regularization 10 iterations ", cmap viridis map100 conjgrad forward, adjoint, gradient, obsdata 3, , rain0, ϵ, 100 heatmap lat, lon, map100', title "Gradient Regularization 100 iterations ", cmap viridis maps conjgrad forward, adjoint, shaping, obsdata 3, , rain0, 10, ϵ time shaping maps btime shaping maps heatmap lat, lon, maps', title "Shaping Regularization 10 iterations ", cmap viridis predict lint map100, alldata, o lat 1 , lon 1 plot corr predict, "Gradient Regularization" predicts lint maps, alldata, o lat 1 , lon 1 plot corr predicts, "Shaping Regularization" function linmap tri Triangulation, points, data n1, n2 length lat , length lon T eltype data map Array T undef, n1, n2 add four corners pad vcat data, zeros T, 4 inbounds for i in 1 n1, j in 1 n2 point lat i , lon j map i, j lint tri, points, pad, point end return map end mapt linmap tri rain, stations, obsdata 3, heatmap lat, lon, mapt', title "Triangulation", cmap viridis md""" Triangulation efficiently solves the problem of creating a rainfall map that fits the input data. However, the map looks unrealistic because it is not smooth. To combine the advantages of triangulation with a smoothing constraint, we can use triangulated interpolation as the backward operator in shaping regularization. """ md""" Triangulation as the Backward Operator To reconstruct a smooth map, we can apply shaping iterations. \mathbf m n 1 \mathbf S m\left \mathbf B\,d \mathbf I B\,F \,\mathbf m n\right \ , where \mathbf m n is the map at the n th iteration, \mathbf d is the measured rainfall, \mathbf F is forward interpolation from a map to irregular locations, \mathbf B is backward interpolation by triangulation, and \mathbf S is smoothing. """ backward y linmap tri rain, stations, y function shape forward Function, backward Function, shaping Function, x0 Array, niter Int x deepcopy x0 for iter in 1 niter x shaping x0 x backward forward x end return x end mapts shape forward, backward, shaping, mapt, 10 heatmap lat, lon, mapts', title "Triangulation Shaping", cmap viridis predictts lint mapts, alldata, o lat 1 , lon 1 plot corr predictts, "Triangulation Shaping" md""" assignment Task 5 To improve the convergence of shaping iterations, we observe that the method requires inverting \mathbf m \infty \left \mathbf I \mathbf S m\, \mathbf B\,F I \right ^ 1 \,\mathbf S m\,\mathbf B\,d \ . The inverted matrix is not symmetric. Therefore, the conjugate gradient algorithm is not applicable. However, we can use a different iterative algorithm, such as GMRES. Saad, Y. and Schultz, M.H., 1986. GMRES A generalized minimal residual algorithm for solving nonsymmetric linear systems https epubs.siam.org doi abs 10.1137 0907058 . SIAM Journal on scientific and statistical computing, 7 3 , pp.856 869. """ assert x ≈ gmres Float32. B , Float32. B x assert x ≈ gmres LinearMap x B x, length x , Float32. B x , maxiter 5, verbose true reshape x, 2,2 function shaping operator x AbstractVector wrap I S BF I operator for abstract vectors x2 reshape x, size mapt 1 D to 2 D y2 x2 shaping backward forward x2 x2 return vec y2 2 D to 1 D end lin operator LinearMap Float32 shaping operator, length mapt md""" Your task Implement shaping regularized interpolation with GMRES and compare the result with that from simple iterations. """ md""" assignment Bonus Task Participate in the Spatial Interpolation Contest. Implement a method that would provide a better interpolation of the missing values than either of the methods above. You can change any of the parameters in the existing functions or write your own function but you can use only the 100 original data points as input. """ md""" References Combettes, P. L., and V. R. Wajs, 2005, Signal recovery by proximal forward backward splitting https epubs.siam.org doi abs 10.1137 050626090 Multiscale Modeling and Simulation, 4, 1168–1200. Fomel, S., 2007, Shaping regularization in geophysical estimation problems https library.seg.org doi full 10.1190 1.2433716 Geophysics, 72, R29–R36. Fomel, S., 2008, Nonlinear shaping regularization in geophysical inverse problems https library.seg.org doi abs 10.1190 1.3059294 78th Annual International Meeting, SEG, Expanded Abstracts, 2046–2051. """

title: Sparsity
text:
A Pluto.jl notebook v0.20.27 frontmatter chapter 4 section 17 order 17 image "https raw.githubusercontent.com fonsp Pluto.jl 580ab811f13d565cc81ebfa70ed36c84b125f55d demo plutodemo.gif" title "Sparsity" tags "module4", "track julia", "track material", "Pluto", "PlutoUI" layout "layout.jlhtml" using Markdown using InteractiveUtils begin using PlutoUIExtra TableOfContents end using Plots using FFTW using LinearAlgebra, Random using DelimitedFiles using LaTeXStrings using Statistics import HTTP let notebook dir DIR toc path joinpath notebook dir, "TOC Notebook.jl" Markdown.parse " ⬅️ Back to Table of Contents . open?path HTTP.escapeuri toc path " end md""" Sparsity The principle of sparsity plays a vital role in data analysis. Finding a sparse representation of signals in a transform domain is a convenient way to characterize signal patterns. This chapter examines methods for enforcing sparsity through regularized inversion and explains how to interpret sparsity constraints from both shaping and optimization perspectives. """ md""" Shaping for sparsity A sparse model has only a few non zero values. A simple transformation that makes an input model sparse is thresholding , which sets small values to zero. Hard thresholding does exactly that and can be defined mathematically as a point by point operation h 	au x n \left\  egin array rl x n & \mbox if \quad |x n| \ge 	au\ , \ 0 & \mbox if \quad |x n| 	au\ . \end array ight. Here, 	au represents the threshold value. Soft thresholding , also known as shrinkage , further "shrinks" larger coefficients toward zero, as follows s 	au x n \left\  egin array rl x n 	au & \mbox if \quad x n \ge 	au\ , \ 0 & \mbox if \quad |x n| 	au\ , \ x n 	au & \mbox if \quad x n \le 	au\ . \end array ight. """ md""" We can devise other forms of a point by point sparsifying transformation based on a threshold. For example, we can multiply the input by a nonlinear weight w 	au x n x n\,e^ 	au^2 x n^2 \ . If |x n| is much larger than 	au , w 	au x n approaches x n . On the other hand, if |x n| is much smaller than 	au , w 	au x n approaches zero. """ begin hard x, τ 3 abs x τ ? x zero x soft x, τ 3 abs x τ ? x sign x τ zero x thre x, τ 3 x exp τ^2 x^2 end plot hard soft thre , 15, 15, labels "hard" "soft" "exponential" , title "Thresholding Functions", linewidth 2 md""" Compressive sampling What makes thresholding a useful operation? Classical sampling theory states that a band limited signal can be exactly reconstructed if the sampling interval is less than the reciprocal of the maximum frequency. Let us consider a case where this condition is violated. """ x range start 0,step 0.001,length 10000 example signal with two sinusoids signal . 50 sin 20π x π^2 5 100 sin 80π x π^2 9 subsample signal10 signal 1 10 end begin select random values mask rand Float64, length signal10 known mask . 0.75 unknown . known signal10 unknown . 0 end Fourier transform real to complex fsignal10 rfft signal10 interpolate with padding in the Fourier domain interp irfft vcat fsignal10, zeros 5001 501 , 10000 10 begin plot x, signal interp , xlim 0, 0.5 , ylim 160, 160 , linewidth 2, labels "ideal" "randomly subsampled and interpolated" scatter x 1 10 end known , signal10 known , label none, color lightblue end frequencies f rfftfreq length signal10 plot f, abs. fsignal10 , linewidth 2, title "Spectrum", xlabel "frequency", color green, label none md""" We can see that the signal is sparse in the Fourier domain, with only two significant coefficients. To reconstruct the signal, we can apply thresholding. """ threshold hard. fsignal10, 4000 plot f, abs. threshold , linewidth 2, title "Spectrum After Thresholding", xlabel "frequency", color green, label none tinterp irfft vcat threshold, zeros 5001 501 , 10000 40 plot x, signal tinterp , xlim 0, 0.5 , ylim 160, 160 , linewidth 2, labels "ideal" "reconstruction" md""" The principle illustrated here is known as compressive sensing or compressive sampling and refers to the ability to reconstruct data from random samples when the transform domain is sparse. """ md""" Bougher, B., 2015, Introduction to compressed sensing The Leading Edge, 34, 1256–1257. """ md""" Connection with optimization Suppose that, given an input data vector \mathbf y , we look for the vector \mathbf x by minimizing the following least squares objective function \|\mathbf x \mathbf y \|^2 \epsilon^2\,\|\mathbf x \|^2 \sum n 1 ^ N \left x n y n ^2 \epsilon^2\,x n^2ight for a fixed scalar \epsilon . By taking the component by component derivative in x n , we can find that the objection function is minimized by solving the equation \mathbf x \mathbf y \epsilon\,\mathbf x 0\ , which leads to \mathbf x  rac 1 1 \epsilon^2 \,\mathbf y \ . In other words, the solution corresponding to the L 2 regularization is to make \mathbf x a bit smaller than \mathbf y . """ md""" What if we change the second term in the objective function to the L 1 norm? The objective function becomes \|\mathbf x \mathbf y \| 2^2 2\,\mu\,\|\mathbf x \| 1 \sum n 1 ^ N \left x n y n ^2 2\,\mu\,|x n|ight for some constant \mu . To minimize the L 1 objective function, we can set to zero derivatives with respect to x n , obtaining x n y n \mu\,\mbox sign \, x n 0\ . To satisfy this equation, we can consider two cases 1. x n 0 The equation becomes x n y n \mu 0 and is satisfied by x n y n \mu provided that y n \mu . 2. x n 0 The equation becomes x n y n \mu 0 and is satisfied by x n y n \mu provided that y n \mu . It is easy to see that the operation applied to the input y n is simply soft thresholding x n s \mu y n . As pointed out in the previous discussion of the difference between the mean and the median, changing the norm from L 2 to L 1 corresponds to changing the assumed probability distribution from normal Gaussian to exponential, thus allowing for a higher probability of outliers. """ md""" Sometimes, using a norm with a hybrid behavior, which behaves like L 2 near minimum and like L 1 at the flanks, makes sense in applications. For example, we can replace the absolute value function |x n| with the hyperbolic function \sqrt |x n|^2 \epsilon^2 or with the Huber norm H x n \left\  egin array rl x n^2 & \mbox if \quad |x n| \le \epsilon\ , \ \epsilon\, 2\,|x n| \epsilon , & \mbox if \quad |x n| \epsilon\ . \end array ight. The L 0 norm refers to the number of non zero elements. In this case, the objective function is \|\mathbf x \mathbf y \| 2^2 \mu^2\,\|\mathbf x \| 0 \sum n 1 ^ N \left x n y n ^2 \mu^2\,\delta 1 x n ight \ , where \delta 1 x \left\  egin array rl 1 & \mbox if \quad x 
e 0\ , \ 0 & \mbox if \quad x 0\ . \end array ight. When x n 0 , the minimum of \left x n y n ^2 \mu^2\,\delta 1 x n ight is y n^2 . When x n 
e 0 , the minimum should be larger we can set it to \mu^2 by setting x n y n . Thus, we uncover the operation x n \left\  egin array rl y n & \mbox if \quad |y n| \ge \mu\ , \ 0 & \mbox if \quad |y n| \mu\ . \end array ight.\ , which is equivalent to hard thresholding x n h \mu y n . The danger of using the L 0 norm in optimization is that the corresponding objective function is not convex. """ md""" Candès, E. J., J. K. Romberg, and T. Tao, 2006, Stable signal recovery from incomplete and inaccurate measurements Communications on Pure and Applied Mathematics, 59, 1207–1223. Candès, E. J., and M. B. Wakin, 2008, An introduction to compressive sampling IEEE Signal Processing Magazine, 25, 21–30. Claerbout, J. F., and F. Muir, 1973, Robust modeling with erratic data Geophysics, 38, 826–844. Huber, P. J., 2009, Robust statistics, 2nd ed. Wiley, New York. """ md""" assignment Task 1 theoretical Derive and plot the thresholding operator that corresponds to the Huber norm \|\mathbf x \mathbf y \| 2^2 \mu^2\,\|\mathbf x \| H \sum n 1 ^ N \left x n y n ^2 \mu^2\,H x n ight \ . """ md""" Iterative thresholding Following the framework of shaping regularization, we can write an iteration of model shaping as \mathbf m n 1 \mathbf S m\left \mathbf m n \mathbf B\,d \mathbf B\,F\,m night \ , where model shaping \mathbf S m corresponds to one of the sparsifying thresholding operations. In the particular case, when the backward operator \mathbf B is the adjoint of the linear forward operator \mathbf F \mathbf B \mathbf F ^T and \mathbf S m corresponds to soft thresholding, the iteration above is known as iterative thresholding algorithm , which converges to the minimum of \|\mathbf F\,m d \| 2^2 2\,\mu\,\|\mathbf m \| 1\ . In this case, the forward operator \mathbf F may include a transform such as the Fourier transform from the domain where we expect the model to be sparse. """ md""" Let us solve our toy data reconstruction problem using iterative thresholding. """ mask for the full signal begin unknown2 zeros Bool, length signal for x1 in x 1 10 end known 1000 unknown2 round Int,x1 1 true end end forward operator function forw x Array pad ifft x pad unknown2 . zero eltype x return pad end backward operator function back y Array pad deepcopy y pad unknown2 . zero eltype y return fft pad length pad end begin cdata complex. signal cdata unknown2 . 0 end dot product test function dottest forward Function, adjoint Function, m Array, d Array "Dot product test" mod similar m rand mod dat similar d rand dat println " L m ⋅d forward mod ⋅ dat " println "L' d ⋅m adjoint dat ⋅ mod " end function shape forward Function, backward Function, shaping Function, λ, x0 Array, niter Int x deepcopy x0 for iter in 1 niter x shaping x0 x λ backward forward x show iter, x ⋅ x end return x end dottest forw, back, cdata, cdata x0 back cdata τ 1 threshold value xhat shape forw, back, x soft. x, τ , length x0 , x0, 5 plot x, signal real ifft xhat length xhat , xlim 0, 0.5 , ylim 160, 160 , linewidth 2, labels "ideal" "iterative thresholding" md""" assignment Task 2 Investigate how the convergence of iterative thresholding depends on the threshold value 	au . """ md""" Iteratively reweighted least squares An alternative to iterative thresholding is a linearization approach, where the L 1 norm behavior is approximated by weighting. This approach is known as iteratively reweighted least squares or IRLS and can be effective in practice. IRLS amounts to the minimization of \|\mathbf F\,m k \mathbf d \| 2^2 2\,\mu\,\|\mathbf W k^ 1 \,\mathbf m k\| 2^2\ \, where \mathbf m k refers to the model at k th iteration, and \mathbf W k is a diagonal weight, which can be defined, for example, as \mathbf W 0 \mathbf I and \mathbf W k \mbox diag \left \sqrt |\mathbf m k 1 | ight for k 0 . Each iteration requires a regular least squares inversion and can be accomplished using the conjugate gradient algorithm. Thus, the iteration consists of cycles of inner conjugate gradient iterations and outer reweighting iterations. If the outer iteration converges, the weighted L 2 norm will approach the L 1 norm. To avoid division by zero and accelerate the convergence, we can also rewrite the objective function using preconditioning as follows \|\mathbf F\,W k\,\mathbf x k \mathbf d \| 2^2 2\,\mu\,\|\mathbf x k\| 2^2\ \, with \mathbf m k \mathbf W k\,\mathbf x k . """ md""" Daubechies, I., R. DeVore, M. Fornasier, and C. S. Güntürk, 2010, Iteratively reweighted least squares minimization for sparse recovery Communications on Pure and Applied Mathematics A Journal Issued by the Courant Institute of Mathematical Sciences, 63, 1–38. Holland, P. W., and R. E. Welsch, 1977, Robust regression using iteratively reweighted least squares Communications in Statistics theory and Methods, 6, 813–827. Scales, J. A., and A. Gersztenkorn, 1988, Robust methods in inverse theory Inverse Problems, 4, 1071. """ md""" TV regularization In the discussion of the smoothing operation, we described it using the diffusion equation  rac \partial u \partial t a^2\,
abla^2 u\ . One step of diffusion using implicit finite differences  rac u \mathbf x ,t \Delta t u \mathbf x ,t \Delta t \,a^2\,
abla^2 u \mathbf x ,t \Delta t corresponds to inversion 4\mathbf u t \Delta t \left \mathbf I \epsilon^2\,\mathbf G ^T\,\mathbf G ight ^ 1 \,\mathbf u t\ , where \mathbf u t represents u \mathbf x ,t , \mathbf G stands for the gradient operator, and \epsilon \Delta t\,a^2 . This equation can be understood as the result of the least squares minimization of \|\mathbf u t \Delta t \mathbf u t\|^2 \epsilon^2\,\|\mathbf G\,u t \Delta t \|^2\ . What happens if we change the L 2 norm in the second term to the L 1 norm known as total variation ? Following the logic of IRLS, this may amount to weighting the gradient by the square root of its absolute value \|\mathbf u t \Delta t \mathbf u t\|^2 \epsilon^2\,\|\mathbf W\,G\,u t \Delta t \|^2\ . Correspondingly, the linear diffusion equation will be transformed to the nonlinear equation  rac \partial u \partial t \displaystyle a^2\,
abla \cdot \left  rac 
abla u \left|
abla uight| ight \ . Scaling the gradient in that manner corresponds to a different form of smoothing, which distinguishes between small and large gradients in the input and preserves edges places of large gradient . """ download "https ahay.org data hall horizon.asc","horizon.asc" xyz readdlm "horizon.asc" read data from a text file begin n1, n2 196, 291 iline xyz 1 n1 end, 1 xline xyz 1 n1, 2 end begin horizon reshape xyz , 3 , n1, n2 subtract mean mean sum horizon length horizon horizon . mean end plot horizon map, title heatmap iline, xline, map, title title, xlabel L" x 1 m ", ylabel L" x 2 m ", clim 14,14 plot horizon horizon, "Seismic Horizon" function smooth x Vector T , nb Int where T Real "triangle smoothing by explicit diffusion" n length x z, y similar x , copy x for ib in 1 nb 1 α 1 4 sin π ib nb ^2 reflecting boundary conditions z 1 1 2 α y 1 α y 1 y 2 for i in 2 n 1 z i 1 2 α y i α y i 1 y i 1 end z n 1 2 α y n α y n 1 y n y, z z, y end return y end smoothed mapslices slice smooth slice, 20 , mapslices slice smooth slice, 20 , horizon dims 1 dims 2 plot horizon smoothed, "Diffusion" md""" Regular smoothing by triangle filtering helps remove the noise but smears the edges of the channel. We can visualize the edges using an edge detection algorithm. https en.wikipedia.org wiki Canny\ edge\ detector https en.wikipedia.org wiki Canny edge detector """ function sobel x Matrix "Sobel gradient filter" n1, n2 size x w1 zeros eltype x , n1, n2 w2 zeros eltype x , n1, n2 inbounds for i1 in 2 n1 1, i2 in 2 n2 1 w1 i1,i2 x i1 1,i2 1 x i1 1,i2 1 2 x i1 1,i2 x i1 1,i2 x i1 1,i2 1 x i1 1,i2 1 w2 i1,i2 x i1 1,i2 1 x i1 1,i2 1 2 x i1,i2 1 x i1,i2 1 x i1 1,i2 1 x i1 1,i2 1 end return w1, w2 end function canny x Matrix pmax 0.95, pmin 0.05 "Canny edge detector" w1, w2 sobel x ww w1 . w1 w2 . w2 pp similar ww n1, n2 size ww edge Array Int8 undef, n1, n2 edge thinning for i1 in 1 n1, i2 in 1 n2 g1 w1 i1,i2 g2 w2 i1,i2 if abs g1 abs g2 j1 1 if g2 g1 0.5 j2 1 elseif g2 g1 0.5 j2 1 else j2 0 end elseif abs g2 abs g1 j2 1 if g1 g2 0.5 j1 1 elseif g1 g2 0.5 j1 1 else j1 0 end else j1 0 j2 0 end k1 i1 j1 if k1 1 || k1 n1 k1 i1 end k2 i2 j2 if k2 1 || k2 n2 k2 i2 end if ww i1,i2 ww k1,k2 pp i1,i2 zero eltype pp continue end k1 i1 j1 if k1 1 || k1 n1 k1 i1 end k2 i2 j2 if k2 1 || k2 n2 k2 i2 end if ww i1,i2 ww k1,k2 pp i1,i2 zero eltype pp continue end pp i1,i2 ww i1,i2 end edge selection wmax quantile ww ,pmax wmin quantile ww ,pmin nedge 0 for i1 in 1 n1, i2 in 1 n2 w pp i1,i2 if w wmax edge i1,i2 1 nedge 1 elseif w wmin edge i1,i2 0 else edge i1,i2 2 end end nold 0 while nedge nold nold nedge for i1 in 1 n1, i2 in 1 n2 if 2 edge i1,i2 if i2 1 if 1 edge i1,i2 1 || i1 1 && 1 edge i1 1,i2 1 || i1 n1 && 1 edge i1 1,i2 1 edge i1,i2 1 nedge 1 continue end end if i2 n2 if 1 edge i1,i2 1 || i1 1 && 1 edge i1 1,i2 1 || i1 n1 && 1 edge i1 1,i2 1 edge i1,i2 1 nedge 1 continue end end if i1 1 && 1 edge i1 1,i2 || i1 n1 && 1 edge i1 1,i2 edge i1,i2 1 nedge 1 end end end end for i1 in 1 n1, i2 in 1 n2 if 2 edge i1,i2 edge i1,i2 0 end end return edge end edges canny horizon mask for edges edge mask x x 0 ? x NaN32 begin plot horizon horizon, "Seismic Horizon with Edges" heatmap iline, xline, edge mask. edges , cmap grays, cbar none end begin plot horizon smoothed, "Smoothed Seismic Horizon with Edges" smooth edges canny smoothed heatmap iline, xline, edge mask. smooth edges , cmap grays, cbar none end md""" Rudin, L. I., S. Osher, and E. Fatemi, 1992, Nonlinear total variation based noise removal algorithms Physica D Nonlinear Phenomena, 60, 259–268. Weickert, J., 1998, Anisotropic diffusion in image processing Teubner. """ md""" Bilateral filtering Another effective approach to edge preserving smoothing is bilateral filtering . Regular smoothing can be described theoretically with the help of a Gaussian weight u s \mathbf x A\,\int u \mathbf y \, \exp \left  rac |\mathbf x y |^2 r^2 ight \,d\mathbf y \ , where u \mathbf y is the input function, u s \mathbf x is its smoothed version, r is the smoothing radius, and A is the normalization A \left \int \exp \left  rac |\mathbf x |^2 r^2 ight \,d\mathbf x ight ^ 1 \ . """ md""" In comparison, bilateral smoothing is a nonlinear operation u b \mathbf x B \mathbf x \,\int u \mathbf y \, \exp \left  rac |\mathbf x y |^2 r^2  rac |u \mathbf x u \mathbf y |^2 ho^2 ight \,d\mathbf y \ , where B \mathbf x \left \int \exp \left  rac |\mathbf x y |^2 r^2  rac |u \mathbf x u \mathbf y |^2 ho^2 ight \,d\mathbf y ight ^ 1 \ . In other words, we determine the neighborhood used for local averaging not only by the distance from the center but also by the difference in data values. Across an edge, the latter is large, and smoothing decreases, as controlled by the parameter ho . """ md""" Tomasi, C., and R. Manduchi, 1998, Bilateral filtering for gray and color images Sixth international conference on computer vision IEEE Cat. No. 98CH36271 , IEEE, 839–846.
