The O N^3 algorithm, on the other hand, would take over 32 billion years, far longer than the age of the known universe. """ md""" note When working with large scale data of size N , we can only afford algorithms with a computational complexity of O N or O N\log N . """ md""" Comparing computational time Let us measure the running time of different functions experimentally. We can measure the computational time using the ` time` macro in Julia. """ x rand 10^3 random array time quicksort x time slowsort x md""" For small sizes of the input data 1,000 in this example , `slowsort ` can be competitive with `quicksort `. But what happens if we increase the size? """ x2 rand 10^5 larger size time quicksort x2 time slowsort x2 md""" A more accurate benchmarking comparison of the running time is provided by Julia's `BenchmarkTools` package. """ btime slowsort x2 btime quicksort x2 Julia standard library function btime sort x2, alg QuickSort md""" How does the speed of Julia's code compare to Python's? It's easy to check since we can call Python functions directly from Julia. """ Python standard library function pysort pybuiltin "sorted" pysort test btime pysort x2 np pyimport "numpy" np.sort test btime np.sort x2 begin Writing our own quicksort in Python py""" def quicksort list if len list 1 return list less, equal, more , , empty lists pivot list 0 for item in list if item pivot less.append item elif item pivot more.append item else equal.append item return quicksort less equal quicksort more """ py quicksort py"quicksort" end py quicksort test btime py quicksort x2 dictionary of functions sorting Dict "Slow own verion " slowsort, "Quick own verion " quicksort, "Julia standard library" sort, "Python standard library" pysort, "Python NumPy library" np.sort, "Python own version " py quicksort begin df DataFrame Implementation String , Time Float64 for name, func in sorting belapsed extracts the runtime in seconds time belapsed func x2 push df, name, time end sort df, Time end extended benchmarking information benchmark quicksort x2 md""" assignment Task 1 To improve the performance of the insertion sort algorithm, notice that it does one unnecessary assignment inside the inner loop. Removing this assignment leads to the following slightly longer code """ function slowsort new list n length list list2 copy list for k in 1 n everything up to k 1 is sorted item list2 k for j in k 1 1 1 k 1,k 2,...,1 prev list2 j if item prev break end out of the loop list2 j 1 , list2 j XXXX, YYYY FIX ME end end return list2 end md""" Fix the code above by replacing `XXXX` and `YYYY` with the correct variable names. You can then test it by sorting test lists of integers and floating point numbers. """ Uncomment below to test slowsort new slowsort new test Uncomment below to test slowsort new slowsort new x md""" Finally, use ` btime` to benchmark the computational time of `slowsort new` and compare it with `slowsort`. """ md""" assignment Task 2 Our implementation of the quick sort algorithm suffers from the need to allocate and deallocate many arrays, which slows down its performance. Below is a version of the code that does sorting in place without allocating new storage. It follows the same logic of dividing the list into two parts but creates those parts by shifting the items in the original list. """ function quicksort list, left 1, right length list if left right return end no need to sort pivot list left l, r left, right separate into two lists while l r item list l 1 if item pivot l 1 else list l 1 list r list r item r 1 end end list left , list l list l , pivot sort each list quicksort list, left, l 1 quicksort list, l 1, ZZZZ FIX ME end md""" Fix the code by replacing `ZZZZ` with the correct variable name. You can then test it by sorting test lists of integers and floating point numbers. """ Uncomment below to test quicksort begin test1 copy test quicksort test1 test1 end Uncomment below to test quicksort begin x1 copy x quicksort x1 x1 end md""" Finally, use btime to benchmark the computational time of `quicksort ` and compare it with `quicksort`. Note that `quicksort ` modifies the input by sorting it in place, while `quicksort` returns a new list. """ btime quicksort x1 setup shuffle x1 md""" assignment Task 3 We can use a simple animation to illustrate the behavior of the two algorithms the insertion sort and the quick sort. """ function plot slowsort list Array n length list list2 copy list anim animate for k in 1 n scatter list2, legend false, title "Slow Sort" everything up to k 1 is sorted for j in k 1 2 k,k 1,...,2 item, prev list2 j , list2 j 1 if item prev break end out of the loop list2 j 1 , list2 j item, prev end end return anim end anim plot slowsort test gif anim, "slowsort.gif", fps 10 function plot quicksort anim, list, left 1, right length list if left right return end no sorting plt scatter list, color green, legend false, title "Quick Sort" frame anim, plt add frame to animation pivot list left l, r left, right separate into two lists while l r item list l 1 if item pivot l 1 else list l 1 list r list r item r 1 end end list left , list l list l , pivot sort each list plot quicksort anim, list, left, l 1 plot quicksort anim, list, l 1, ZZZZ end md""" Replace `ZZZZ` in the code above with the correct variable name, uncomment the code below, and add one line to create an animated GIF file called "quicksort.gif" with an animation rate of 10 frames per second. """ Uncoment below begin anim2 Animation atest copy test plot quicksort anim2, atest Add one line end md""" Quick quantile When calculating a quantile value, such as the median, it is not necessary to fully sort the list. Instead, we can partition the list into two parts, similar to quicksort, and then select the quantile from only one of the two sublists. The code below implements this method. """ function quantile list, n Int pivot list 1 n equal 0 number of items pivot Type typeof pivot less, more Type , Type empty lists for item in list if item pivot push less, item elseif item pivot push more, item else n equal 1 end end n less length less if n n less return quantile less, n end n less n equal if n n less return pivot end return quantile more, n n less end test quantile test, 42 for i 1 100 assert quantile test, i i end md""" The cost of the quick quantile algorithm satisfies the relationship C N C N 2 O N C N 2 \gamma\,N with C 1 0 . It follows that, in this case, C N 2\,\gamma\, N 1 O N . """ function median list n length list if n % 2 0 n is even n÷2 is integer division same as div n,2 return quantile list, n÷2 quantile list, n÷2 1 2 else n is odd return quantile list, n 1 ÷2 end end median test btime median x2 btime Statistics.median x2 md""" assignment Task 4 Our experiment should involve different data sizes to compare the efficiency of various algorithms systematically. """ function benchmark sizes Array Int na length sizes arrays rand. sizes create a data frame bm DataFrame Sizes sizes dictionary of algorithms to compare algorithms Dict "slowsort" slowsort, "quantile" median for name, func in algorithms times Array Float64 undef,na for n in 1 na a arrays n belapsed extracts the runtime in seconds times n belapsed func a end bm ,name times end return bm end sizes 10^n for n in 2 5 bm benchmark sizes plot sizes, bm , "slowsort" bm , "quantile" , linewidth 3, labels "slowsort" "quantile" , legend top, markershape diamond, xscale log10, yscale log10, xlabel "data size", ylabel "computing time seconds ", title "Comparison of Sorting Algorithms" md""" Add your favorite implementation of the quicksort algorithm to the plot. """ md""" Connection with optimization We can frame the averaging attribute as a solution to the following problem find a number a such that a \approx a n for n 1,2,\ldots,N . To solve this problem, we need a way to measure the approximation error. """ md""" For example, we could take the least squares or L 2 measure and minimize F LS a \frac 1 2 \,\sum\limits n 1 ^N \left a n a\right ^2\ . """ md""" F LS a is a quadratic function of a \begin array rcl F LS a & & \displaystyle \frac 1 2 \,\sum\limits n 1 ^N \left a n^2 2\,a n\,a a^2\right \\ & & \displaystyle \frac 1 2 \,\sum\limits n 1 ^N a n^2 a\,\sum\limits n 1 ^N a n \frac a^2 2 \,N\ .\end array """ md""" It reaches the minimum value when \frac d d a F LS a \sum\limits n 1 ^N a n a\,N 0\ . or a \frac 1 N \, \sum\limits n 1 ^ N a n \alpha \ . """ md""" Thus, the mean value corresponds to minimizing the misfit between an average value and data values measured with the least squares misfit function F LS a . """ md""" What if, instead of the L 2 measure, we use the sum of absolute values or the L 1 measure? The L 1 norm is defined as follows F L 1 a \sum\limits n 1 ^N \left|a n a\right|\ . """ md""" The derivative of F L 1 a involves the sum of sign functions \frac d d a F L 1 a \displaystyle \sum\limits n 1 ^N \frac a a n \left|a n a\right| \sum\limits n 1 ^N \mbox sign a a n \ . To set the sum of signs to zero, we need to ensure an equal number of positive and negative signs, or, in other words, the same number of data values greater than a as less than a . """ md""" According to our previous definition, this indicates that the optimal a in the L 1 minimization case is the median. """ begin L1 data, a sum abs. data . a L1test a L1 test, a end begin L2 data, a sum data . a .^2 2 L2test a L2 test, a end begin p1 plot L1test, 1, 100, label L" L 1 objective function" p2 plot L2test, 1, 100, label L" L 2 objective function", color green plot p1, p2, layout 1,2 , linewidth 3, xlabel L"a", legend top end begin pz1 plot L1test, 46, 55, label L" L 1 objective function" pz2 plot L2test, 46, 55, label L" L 2 objective function", color green plot pz1, pz2, layout 1,2 , linewidth 3, xlabel L"a", legend top end md""" We could generalize the optimization approach by considering the L p measure F L p a \frac 1 p \sum\limits n 1 ^N \left|a n a\right|^p with different values of p . """ begin Lp data, a, p sum abs. data . a .^p p L05test a Lp test, a, 0.5 L15test a Lp test, a, 1.5 end begin p3 plot L05test, 46, 55, color red, label L" L 0.5 objective function" p4 plot L15test, 46, 55, color orange, label L" L 1.5 objective function" plot p3, p4, layout 1,2 , linewidth 3, xlabel L"a", legend top end md""" An appropriate value of p should be greater than one because, with smaller values, the objective function shows non convex behavior. """ md""" Why select a specific measure and, consequently, a specific definition of the averaging attribute? The statistical argument links this choice to the probability distribution of data values. """ md""" If the data are distributed according to the normal Gaussian distribution, f a n \frac 1 \sigma\,\sqrt 2\pi \exp\left \frac a n a ^2 2\,\sigma^2 \right \ , and are statistically independent, then their joint probability distribution is the product of exponents """ md""" \begin array l f a 1,a 2,\ldots\,a n \\ \displaystyle \frac 1 \left 2\pi\,\sigma^2\right ^ N 2 \, \exp\left \frac 1 2\,\sigma^2 \,\sum\limits n 1 ^N a n a ^2\right \ .\end array Looking for a that maximizes the probability becomes equivalent to minimizing the least squares function F LS a . """ md""" A key feature of the Gaussian distribution is its rapid decay from the center. Very large or very small values can still occur, but with quickly decreasing probability. When the decay rate is slower, indicating a higher likelihood of outliers, the L 2 measure becomes less suitable than other measures. """ md""" For example, the exponential probability distribution f a n \frac 1 2\,\mu \exp\left \frac |a n a| \mu \right would lead to the L 1 minimization F L 1 a and, correspondingly, to the median. """ md""" note Maximizing the likelihood of a specific parameter creates an optimization problem for estimating that parameter. The choice of an objective function for optimization depends on the statistical assumptions about the data distribution. """ md""" Averaging complex numbers Different ways exist to compute an average of two or more complex numbers. An arithmetic average between c 1 and c 2 is a c 1 c 2 2 . A geometric average g c 1\,c 2 ^ 1 2 may better preserve amplitudes of the signal. The loss of amplitude in stacking complex signals with varying phases can serve as a valuable measure of phase alignment. """ md""" References Bentley, J., 1999, Programming pearls, 2nd ed. Addison Wesley Professional. Cormen, T. H., 2013, Algorithms unlocked The MIT Press. Cormen, T. H., C. E. Leiserson, R. L. Rivest, and C. Stein, 2009, Introduction to algorithms, 3rd ed. The MIT Press. """ md""" Devore, J. L., 2015, Probability and statistics for engineering and the sciences, 9th ed. Cengage Learning. Hoare, C. A. R., 1962, Quicksort Computer Journal, 5, 10–15. Knuth, D. E., 1998, Art of computer programming, Volume 3 Sorting and searching, 2nd ed. Addison Wesley Professional. """ md""" Tarantola, A., 2004, Inverse problem theory and methods for model parameter estimation SIAM. """ md""" Data example In the data example, we will revisit the daily temperature data from Camp Mabry. Let’s load the data from the previously saved CSV file. """ mabry DataFrame CSV.File "mabry.csv" plot mabry.Date, mabry.Temperature, line z mabry.Temperature, color coolwarm, title "Maximum Daily Temperature at Camp Mabry", ylabel "Degrees Celsius", leg false md""" There are many fluctuations in the data. To better observe the trend, it helps to apply local averaging to smooth the data. We will first try median averaging in a sliding window. """ function smoothed data, w, average "smoothing data in windows of size w using average" n length data smooth similar data for i in 1 n k max 0,min n w,i w÷2 1 smooth i average view data k 1 k w end return smooth end mabry.Smoothed smoothed mabry.Temperature, 30, median begin plot mabry.Date, mabry.Temperature, label "original", alpha 0.5 plot mabry.Date, mabry.Smoothed, linewidth 2, title "Maximum Daily Temperature at Camp Mabry", ylabel "Degrees Celsius", label "smoothed" end md""" Smoothing separates signal from noise. We can extract noise and analyze it separately by plotting its histogram. """ noise mabry.Temperature mabry.Smoothed histogram noise, label none, xlabel "Degrees Celsius", title "Noise Distribution" md""" See the StatsPlots https github.com JuliaPlots Plots.jl tree v2 StatsPlots documentation for more ideas on plotting averages. """ md""" assignment Task 5 Does the shape of the noise distribution justify the use of the median for the averaging function? Repeat the analysis, replacing the running median with the running mean, and compare the results. """ md""" assignment Bonus Task Extract an average of the maximum daily summer temperature for different years in the Camp Mabry dataset and plot it. For this assignment, you can define summer as the period between June 1 and August 31. """

title: Using Patterns
text: A Pluto.jl notebook v0.20.27 frontmatter chapter 4 section 21 order 21 image "https raw.githubusercontent.com fonsp Pluto.jl 580ab811f13d565cc81ebfa70ed36c84b125f55d demo plutodemo.gif" title "Using Patterns" tags "module4", "track julia", "track material", "Pluto", "PlutoUI" layout "layout.jlhtml" using Markdown using InteractiveUtils begin using PlutoUIExtra TableOfContents end using LinearAlgebra for dot product using ZipFile using Random using Images using Statistics using Plots import HTTP let notebook dir DIR toc path joinpath notebook dir, "TOC Notebook.jl" Markdown.parse " ⬅️ Back to Table of Contents . open?path HTTP.escapeuri toc path " end md""" Using patterns Having defined prediction error filtering PEF as an efficient method for approximating covariance operators, we will now discuss its application to data reconstruction and noise attenuation. """ mutable struct HelixFilter lag Vector CartesianIndex flt Vector HelixFilter lag,flt new map CartesianIndex,lag ,flt HelixFilter lag HelixFilter lag,zeros Float32, length lag end function helix a HelixFilter, ci CartesianIndices "convert helix lags to 1 D for a given grid" middle of the grid mid CartesianIndex Tuple last ci .÷ 2 helix index of middle hmid LinearIndices ci mid from Cartesian shift to helix shift return LinearIndices ci map x x mid, a.lag . hmid end Base.length a HelixFilter Base.length a.lag function hconvolve x, a HelixFilter, adjoint false cx, nx, na CartesianIndices x , length x , length a lag helix a, cx y deepcopy x accounting for the zero coefficient for ia in 1 na for iy in 1 lag ia nx ix iy lag ia if adjoint correlation y ix x iy a.flt ia else convolution y iy x ix a.flt ia end end end return y end function hrecursive x, a HelixFilter, adjoint false cx, nx, na CartesianIndices x , length x , length a lag helix a, cx y similar x if adjoint for ix in nx 1 1 t x ix for ia in 1 na iy ix lag ia if iy nx t a.flt ia y iy end end y ix t end else for ix in 1 nx t x ix for ia in 1 na iy ix lag ia if iy 1 t a.flt ia y iy end end y ix t end end return y end function pef f, x, a HelixFilter, mask convolution with a prediction error filter nx, na length x , length a lag helix a, CartesianIndices x y zeros eltype x , size x for ia in 1 na for iy in 1 lag ia nx if mask iy skip missing points ix iy lag ia y iy x ix f ia end end end return y end function pef adjoint y, x, a HelixFilter, mask adjoint convolution with a prediction error filter nx, na length x , length a lag helix a, CartesianIndices x f zeros eltype a.flt , na for ia in 1 na for iy in 1 lag ia nx if mask iy skip missing points ix iy lag ia f ia x ix y iy end end end return f end function conjgrad forward Function, adjoint Function, d Array, x0 Array, niter Int "Conjugate gradients for minimizing |forward x d|^2" x deepcopy x0 R forward x . d s, S similar x , similar d gnp zero eltype x for iter in 1 niter g adjoint R G forward g gn g ⋅ g show iter, gn if iter 1 steepest descent s, S g, G else β gn gnp s g β s S G β S end gnp gn α gn S ⋅ S x α s R α S end return x end function find mask known, lag make a filter of all ones a HelixFilter lag a.flt . one Float32 sert ones at unknown locations x ones Float32, size known x known . zero Float32 y hconvolve x, a return y . zero Float32 end function pef lag2 n1, n2 find PEF lags in 2D nc floor Int, n1 2 lag Array Tuple Int, Int undef, n1 nc 1 n1 n2 1 i 1 for i2 in 1 n2 n0 i2 1 ? nc 2 1 for i1 in n0 n1 lag i i1 nc 1, i2 1 i 1 end end return lag end md""" Pattern generation In the first experiment, we will test whether prediction error filters can extract stationary patterns. For each example input, we will estimate the PEF, examine the residual after convolution with the filter, and generate a pattern by applying inverse convolution to a random input. """ md""" Claerbout, J., and M. Brown, 1999, Two dimensional textures and prediction error filters 61st Mtg., Eur. Assn. Geosci. Eng., Session 1009. """ download data from a public server download "https zenodo.org api records 11099632 files archive", "files.zip" begin unzip the archive file r ZipFile.Reader "files.zip" make a dictionary of files for easy access patterns Dict String, IO for file in r.files name splitext file.name 1 patterns name file end "wood" pattern wood Array Float32 undef, 128, 128 single precision array read patterns "wood" , wood wood maximum wood "herring" pattern herr Array Float32 undef, 128, 128 single precision array read patterns "herr" , herr herr maximum herr "seismic" pattern seis Array Float32 undef, 250, 125 single precision array read patterns "seis" , seis seis maximum seis end grayplot data, title heatmap data, title title, yflip true, axis nothing, legend none, color grays md""" assignment Task 1 Test the PEF method for pattern creation with your own data. """ begin an example of loading an image from the Internet image url "https fractalfoundation.org OFCA MarsRivers.jpg" image HTTP.get image url .body | IOBuffer | load end mars convert Array Float32 , Gray. image grayplot mars, "Mars" md""" Data reconstruction Next, we turn to the problem of data reconstruction. If some bins in the original image are missing, can we reconstruct them by extrapolating the pattern from the available data? """ md""" We can formulate the data reconstruction problem as a least squares optimization \left \begin array l \mathbf K \\ \epsilon\,\mathbf A \end array \right \,\mathbf m \approx \left \begin array l \mathbf d \\ \mathbf 0 \end array \right \ , where \mathbf K is the diagonal matrix indicating the locations of known data, \mathbf A denotes convolution with a PEF, and \epsilon is the regularization parameter. """ conjugate gradients with preconditioning function conjgrad forward Function, adjoint Function, precon Function, precon adjoint Function, d Array, x0 Array, ϵ Real, niter Int "Conjugate gradients for minimizing |forward x d|^2 ϵ^2 |regul x |^2" x deepcopy x0 R1 forward precon x d R2 ϵ x nd length d s, S1, S2 similar x , similar R1 , similar R2 gnp zero eltype x for iter in 1 niter g precon adjoint adjoint R1 ϵ R2 block adjoint G1 forward precon g G2 ϵ g gn g ⋅ g if iter 1 steepest descent s, S1, S2 g, G1, G2 else β gn gnp s g β s S1 G1 β S1 S2 G2 β S2 end gnp gn α gn S1 ⋅ S1 S2 ⋅ S2 x α s R1 α S1 R2 α S2 Rn real R1 ⋅ R1 R2 ⋅ R2 length of the residual show iter, Rn end return precon x end function find pef data, known, lag niter length lag f zeros eltype data , length lag a HelixFilter lag, f if known false mask find mask ones Bool,size data , lag else mask find mask known, lag end a.flt conjgrad f pef f, data, a, mask , x pef adjoint x, data, a, mask , data, f, niter return a end function test pef input pef find pef input, false, pef lag2 10, 10 , niter 50 residual hconvolve input, pef random randn eltype input , size input pattern hrecursive random, pef p1 grayplot input, "input" p2 grayplot residual, "input PEF" p3 grayplot pattern, "random PEF" return plot p1, p2, p3, layout 1, 3 end test pef wood test pef herr test pef seis function punch hole data Matrix T where T Real make an elliptical hole n1, n2 size data hole similar data mask zeros Bool, n1, n2 for i2 in 1 n2, i1 in 1 n1 x i1 1 n1 0.5 y i2 1 n2 0.3 u x y v x y 2 if u u v v 0.15 hole i1,i2 zero T else hole i1,i2 data i1,i2 mask i1,i2 true end end return hole, mask end hole, known punch hole wood begin wpef find pef hole, known, pef lag2 10, 10 , niter 50 wresidual hconvolve wood, wpef wrandom randn Float32, size wood std wresidual wpattern hrecursive wrandom, wpef pw1 grayplot wood, "ideal" pw2 grayplot hole, "input" pw3 grayplot wpattern, "pattern" plot pw1, pw2, pw3, layout 1, 3 end function maskop x, known mask operator y zeros eltype x , size x y known . x known return y end wzero zeros eltype hole , size hole wfilled conjgrad x maskop x, known , x maskop x, known , x hrecursive x, wpef, false , x hrecursive x, wpef, true , hole, wzero, 0.01, 100 begin pw4 grayplot wfilled, "filled" plot pw1, pw2, pw4, layout 1, 3 end md""" Handling uncertainty The data reconstruction problem is inherently uncertain. Multiple solutions can exist, all of which fit both the input data and the assumed pattern. """ md""" A practical method for producing multiple realizations involves the following steps 1. Begin with a random realization of the pattern \mathbf m 0 . 2. Instead of estimating \mathbf m such that \mathbf K \,\mathbf m \mathbf d , estimate \mathbf x such that \mathbf K \,\mathbf x \mathbf d \mathbf K \,\mathbf m 0 \ . 3. The estimate for \mathbf m is then \widehat \mathbf m \widehat \mathbf x \mathbf m 0 . """ function realization seed Random.seed seed random randn Float32, size wood std wresidual Step 1 m0 hrecursive random, wpef Step 2 filled conjgrad x maskop x, known , x maskop x, known , x hrecursive x, wpef, false , x hrecursive x, wpef, true , hole m0, wzero, 0.01, 100 Step 3 return filled m0 end pr seed grayplot realization seed , "realization seed " plot pr 1 , pr 2 , pr 3 , layout 1, 3 md""" assignment Task 2 Let's revisit the Seabeam data example. Solve the data reconstruction problem using the PEF approach and produce multiple realizations. """ download a data file download "https ahay.org data seab data.rsf ","data.bin" begin read data seab Array Float32 undef, 3, 368945 single precision array read "data.bin", seab seab 3, 2978 . seab 3, 387 end function binning data Matrix T , n Vector Int d 1,1 , o 0,0 where T Real nd size data, 2 reg, count zeros T, n 1 , n 2 , zeros Int, n 1 , n 2 for id in 1 nd i1 round Int, 1 data 1, id o 1 d 1 i2 round Int, 1 data 2, id o 2 d 2 if i1 1 && i1 n 1 && i2 1 && i2 n 2 reg i1, i2 data 3, id count i1, i2 1 end end for i1 in 1 n 1 , i2 in 1 n 2 if count i1, i2 1 reg i1, i2 count i1, i2 end end return reg, count end begin n 160 o minimum seab 1, , minimum seab 2, m maximum seab 1, , maximum seab 2, d m o n 1 end seabeam, count binning seab, n, n , o o, d d begin lon range start o 1 , stop m 1 , length n lat range start o 2 , stop m 2 , length n end mask for known data locations mask count . 0 begin pbin heatmap lon, lat, seabeam', color grays, legend none, xlabel "longitude", ylabel "latitude", title "Binned" pmsk heatmap lon, lat, mask', color grays, legend none, xlabel "longitude", ylabel "latitude", title "Mask" plot pbin, pmsk, layout 1, 2 end md""" Signal and noise separation When the data vector \mathbf d consists of signal \mathbf s and noise \mathbf n , \mathbf d s n \ , and the signal and noise are characterized by prediction error filters \mathbf S and \mathbf N , we can apply N to both sides of the equation to transform it into \mathbf N\,d \approx \mathbf N\,s and augment it with the equation \mathbf S\,s \approx \mathbf 0 for an overdetermined system of equations \left \begin array l \mathbf N \\ \epsilon\,\mathbf S \end array \right \,\mathbf s \approx \left \begin array l \mathbf N\,d \\ \mathbf 0 \end array \right \ , where \epsilon controls the relative signal to noise ratio. The least squares solution is \widehat \mathbf s \left \mathbf N ^T\,\mathbf N \epsilon^2\,\mathbf S ^T\,\mathbf S \right ^ 1 \, \mathbf N ^T\,\mathbf N\,d \ . The estimated noise is \widehat \mathbf n \mathbf d \widehat \mathbf s \epsilon^2\,\left \mathbf N ^T\,\mathbf N \epsilon^2\,\mathbf S ^T\,\mathbf S \right ^ 1 \, \mathbf S ^T\,\mathbf S\,d \ . The least squares solution is the same as the estimate \begin array rcl \widehat \mathbf s & & \left \mathbf C n^ 1 \mathbf C s ^ 1 \right ^ 1 \,\mathbf C n^ 1 \,\mathbf d \ , \\ \widehat \mathbf n & & \left \mathbf C n^ 1 \mathbf C s ^ 1 \right ^ 1 \,\mathbf C s^ 1 \,\mathbf d \ \end array that we previously derived directly from the Gauss Markov theory. In practice, if we can estimate \mathbf S and \mathbf N from training data, we can use an iterative conjugate gradient method to invert the linear equations and remove additive noise from the data. """ md""" Random noise In the initial toy example, the noise is almost random. By applying inverse recursive filtering to a helix, we use preconditioning to rewrite the system of equations as \left \begin array l \mathbf S ^ 1 \mathbf N \\ \epsilon\,\mathbf I \end array \right \,\mathbf s \approx \left \begin array l \mathbf S ^ 1 \mathbf N\,d \\ \mathbf 0 \end array \right \ . """ function smooth x Vector T , nb Int where T Real "triangle smoothing by explicit diffusion" n length x z similar x for ib in 1 nb 1 α 1 4 sin π ib nb ^2 reflecting boundary conditions z 1 1 2 α x 1 α x 1 x 2 for i in 2 n 1 z i 1 2 α x i α x i 1 x i 1 end z n 1 2 α x n α x n 1 x n x . z end end function make synthetic n1 Int, n2 Int, seed Int Random.seed seed s1 rand Float32, n1 3 . 0.5 .^ 3 s2 rand Float32, n1 3 . 0.5 .^ 3 smooth s1, 2 smooth s2, 4 pp Array Float32 undef, n1, n2 for i2 in 1 n2, i1 in 1 n1 pp i1, i2 s1 i1 n1 i2 1 s2 i1 n1 i2 2 2 end return pp end begin signal make synthetic 75, 25, 2024 noise 2 90 randn size signal data signal noise end function plot two one, title1, two, title2 p1 heatmap one, c grays, cmap 0.08, 0.08 , title title1, legend none p2 heatmap two, c grays, cmap 0.08, 0.08 , title title2, legend none return plot p1, p2, layout 1, 2 end plot two signal, "signal", data, "signal noise" nlag pef lag2 7, 1 slag pef lag2 7, 3 begin npef find pef data, false, nlag, niter 10 spef find pef data, false, slag, niter 10 end function signal noise data, signal pef, noise pef ϵ 1.0, niter 50 PEF based signal and noise separation forward x hconvolve x, noise pef, false adjoint x hconvolve x, noise pef, true precon x hrecursive x, signal pef, false precon adjoint x hrecursive x, signal pef, true x0 zeros eltype data , size data signal conjgrad forward, adjoint, precon, precon adjoint, forward data , x0, ϵ, niter return signal, data signal end signal0, noise0 signal noise data, spef, npef plot two signal0, "estimated signal", noise0, "estimated noise" md""" The separation between signal and noise is controlled by the parameter \epsilon . """ signal1, noise1 signal noise data, spef, npef ϵ 4.0 plot two signal1, "estimated signal ϵ 4 ", noise1, "estimated noise ϵ 4 " signal2, noise2 signal noise data, spef, npef ϵ 0.25 plot two signal2, "estimated signal ϵ 0.25 ", noise2, "estimated noise ϵ 0.25 " md""" Separating signal and noise PEFs When examining coherent noise, it becomes apparent that the prediction error filter estimated from the data does not solely represent the signal pattern but instead reflects a combination of signal and noise patterns. As Simon Spitz initially suggested, this can be described as a product \mathbf D \mathbf N\,S \ . To leverage this observation, we can apply the \mathbf N operator to both equations in the original system \left \begin array l \mathbf N ^2 \\ \epsilon\,\mathbf D \end array \right \,\mathbf s \approx \left \begin array l \mathbf N^2\,d \\ \mathbf 0 \end array \right \ , """ md""" Spitz, S., 1999. Pattern recognition, spatial predictability, and subtraction of multiple events. The Leading Edge, 18 1 , pp.55 58. Claerbout, J., and S. Fomel, 2000, Spitz makes a better assumption for the signal PEF SEP report, 103, 205–213. """ function signal noise2 data, data pef, noise pef ϵ 1.0, niter 50 PEF based signal and noise separation forward x hconvolve hconvolve x, noise pef, false , noise pef, false adjoint x hconvolve hconvolve x, noise pef, true , noise pef, true precon x hrecursive x, data pef, false precon adjoint x hrecursive x, data pef, true x0 zeros eltype data , size data signal conjgrad forward, adjoint, precon, precon adjoint, forward data , x0, ϵ, niter return signal, data signal end md""" We will now treat the clean signal from the previous example as data and try to separate it into two plane wave components. """ begin npef1 find pef signal, false, pef lag2 2, 2 , niter 10 dpef find pef signal, false, pef lag2 5, 3 , niter 20 end sig1, sig2 signal noise2 signal, dpef, npef1, ϵ 4 plot two sig1, "estimated signal", sig2, "estimated noise" npef2 find pef sig2, false, pef lag2 2, 2 , niter 10 sig3, sig4 signal noise2 signal, dpef, npef2, ϵ 4 plot two sig3, "estimated signal", sig4, "estimated noise" md""" assignment Task 3 Apply the method in `signa noise2` to the previous example of removing random noise and compare the results with those from `signal noise`. """ md""" assignment Bonus Task Propose and implement a method to automatically estimate the optimal value of \epsilon . """

title: Local Attributes
text: A Pluto.jl notebook v0.20.27 frontmatter chapter 4 section 22 order 22 image "https raw.githubusercontent.com fonsp Pluto.jl 580ab811f13d565cc81ebfa70ed36c84b125f55d demo plutodemo.gif" title "Local Attributes" tags "module4", "track julia", "track material", "Pluto", "PlutoUI" layout "layout.jlhtml" using Markdown using InteractiveUtils begin using PlutoUIExtra TableOfContents end using Plots, Statistics using LinearAlgebra defines dot product as "⋅" using LocalSignalAttributes using SegyIO for reading data in sgy format using FFTW import HTTP let notebook dir DIR toc path joinpath notebook dir, "TOC Notebook.jl" Markdown.parse " ⬅️ Back to Table of Contents . open?path HTTP.escapeuri toc path " end md""" Local attributes We revisit the definition of simple data attributes to understand how to transform them from global to localized measures using linear estimation tools and shaping regularization. """ md""" From global to local Suppose we have two data vectors, \mathbf a and \mathbf b , and want to find a scale factor \gamma that would make \mathbf b closer to \mathbf a after scaling. The least squares minimization \displaystyle \min \gamma \left|\mathbf a \gamma\,\mathbf b \right|^2 \displaystyle \min \gamma \sum n a n \gamma\,b n ^2 has the solution \widehat \gamma \displaystyle \frac \mathbf b ^T\,\mathbf a \mathbf b ^T\,\mathbf b \displaystyle \frac \sum n a n\,b n \sum n b n^2 \ . What if we want the scaling to be not a constant but a variable vector \mathbf c that would scale different parts of the vector \mathbf b differently for a closer match to the target? One approach would be to split the data into overlapping windows or patches, apply different scaling in each patch, and then stitch the patches back together. While this approach has some advantages, such as the ability to process different patches in parallel, it provides only crude control over localization. To make the problem genuinely localized, we can consider the least squares problem \displaystyle \min \mathbf c \left|\mathbf a \mathbf C \,\mathbf b \right|^2 \min \mathbf c \left|\mathbf a \mathbf B \,\mathbf c \right|^2 \displaystyle \min \mathbf c \sum n a n c n\,b n ^2\ , where \mathbf C \mbox diag \mathbf c and \mathbf B \mbox diag \mathbf b represent diagonal matrices. The problem is poorly defined because it may lead to unstable division when some elements of b n are small or zero. """ md""" We can constrain the unstable division by using regularization for example, by enforcing the estimated vector \mathbf c to be smooth. The solution with enforced smoothness, achieved through shaping regularization, is \widehat \mathbf c \left \lambda^2\,\mathbf I \mathbf S \,\left \mathbf B ^2 \lambda^2\,\mathbf I \right \right ^ 1 \, \mathbf S \,\mathbf B \,\mathbf a \ , where \lambda is a scaling factor, which we can set to the root mean square value of b n \lambda \displaystyle \sqrt \frac 1 N \sum n 1 ^N b n^2 \ , and \mathbf S represents model shaping a smoothing filter with the user specified degree of smoothness. Efficient smoothing by triangle filtering is appropriate for this task. An iterative method, like conjugate gradients, can perform the inversion. The greater the smoothness, the closer the inverted matrix will be to the identity, requiring fewer iterations to reach convergence. With a very large smoothing radius, the solution \widehat \mathbf c will start to approach the constant \widehat \gamma case. Less smoothness enables the desired variability in localized scaling while maintaining stability in the inversion. """ md""" Fomel, S., 2007, Local seismic attributes Geophysics, 72, A29–A33. """ md""" Automatic gain control By setting the vector \mathbf a to a constant, we can determine a weighting factor needed to equalize the values in the input data \mathbf b . This method is commonly known as AGC automatic gain control . The figure above provides an example. A raw input shot gather has amplitudes that decrease over time due to the combined effects of geometrical spreading and attenuation. By setting \mathbf a to one and \mathbf b to the absolute value of the data, we can determine the gain function \mathbf c using the shaping regularization method from the previous section. In this case, the smoothing radius is wide in the horizontal direction, so the necessary variable scaling applies only vertically. The result shows the expected increase over time. Applying the gain to the data results in more balanced amplitudes. """ download "https ahay.org data wz data.rsf ","data.bin" begin shot Array Float32 undef, 2000, 81 single precision array read "data.bin", shot shot 1.0e 8 normalize end begin t range 0, 2, 1001 time axis x range 2, 2, 81 offset axis end function plot seismic data, title clip quantile abs. data , 0.99 return heatmap x, t, data 1 1001, , clim clip, clip , yflip true, c grays, legend none, xlabel "Offset km ", ylabel "Time s ", title title end size shot , size t , size x pshot plot seismic shot, " a Original data" function conjgrad forward Function, adjoint Function, shaping Function, ϵ Float64, d Array, p0 Array, niter Int tol 1.0e 6 "Conjugate gradient algorithm for shaping regularization" p deepcopy p0 x shaping p r forward x . d sp, sx, sr similar p , similar x , similar r g0 gnp zero eltype x for iter in 1 niter gx adjoint r ϵ x gp shaping gx ϵ p gx shaping gp gr forward gx gn gp ⋅ gp show iter, gn if iter 1 sp, sx, sr gp, gx, gr g0 gn else β gn gnp γ gn g0 if β tol || gn g0 tol println "converged β β γ γ " break end sp gp β sp sx gx β sx sr gr β sr end gnp gn α sr ⋅ sr ϵ sp ⋅ sp sx ⋅ sx α gn α p p α sp x x α sx r r α sr end return x end md""" assignment Task 1 Replace the absolute value in the AGC implementation with the envelope and compare the results. """ The envelope is computed with the help of the Hilbert transform hilb mapslices hilbert, shot, dims 1 env sqrt. shot . shot hilb . hilb begin pabs heatmap x, t, abs. shot 1 1001, , yflip true, legend false, clim 0, 0.25 , xlabel "Offset km ", ylabel "Time s ", title " b Absolute Value", cmap grays penv heatmap x, t, env 1 1001, , yflip true, legend false, clim 0, 0.25 , xlabel "Offset km ", ylabel "Time s ", title " c Envelope", cmap grays plot pshot, pabs, penv, layout 1, 3 end md""" Local frequency One practical local attribute is local frequency, which expands on the concept of instantaneous frequency. """ md""" For a real valued time variable signal f t , the complex analytical trace is defined as c t f t i\,h t \ , where h t is the Hilbert transform of f t . We can also represent the complex trace in terms of the envelope A t and the instantaneous phase \phi t , as follows c t A t \,e^ i\,\phi t \ . By definition, the instantaneous frequency is the time derivative of the instantaneous phase \omega t \phi' t \mathit Im \left \frac c' t c t \right \frac f t \,h' t f' t \,h t f^2 t h^2 t \ . """ md""" assignment Task 2 theoretical Derive the equation shown above. """ md""" assignment Task 3 To implement local frequency, replace the point by point division in the definition of the instantaneous frequency with a smooth division. Write a function to calculate local frequency and apply it to the AGC corrected shot gather from Task 1. """ md""" Local orthogonalization When addressing the challenge of separating signal from noise, selecting the right parameters for a clear separation can be difficult. If we lean towards removing as much noise as possible and end up with some signal leaking into the estimated noise, we can improve the separation by matching the estimated signal \mathbf s 0 to the remaining signal in the separated noise \mathbf n 0 . """ md""" Doing it by simple scaling amounts to minimizing \displaystyle \min \gamma \left|\mathbf n 0 \gamma\,\mathbf s 0\right|^2\ . Estimating \gamma by least squares minimization, we can define the new signal \mathbf s and the new noise \mathbf n according to \begin array rcl \mathbf s & & \mathbf s 0 \gamma\,\mathbf s 0 1 \gamma \, \mathbf s 0\ , \\ \mathbf n & & \mathbf n 0 \gamma\,\mathbf s 0\ . \end array """ md""" Notice that \mathbf s ^T\mathbf n 1 \gamma \,\left \mathbf s 0^T\mathbf n 0 \gamma\, \mathbf s 0^T\,\mathbf s 0\right \displaystyle 1 \gamma \,\left \mathbf s 0^T\mathbf n 0 \frac \mathbf s 0^T\mathbf n 0 \mathbf s 0^T\,\mathbf s 0 \, \mathbf s 0^T\,\mathbf s 0\right 0\ . In other words, the new signal and noise estimates will be orthogonal. The method becomes significantly more powerful when replacing scaling by a constant with scaling by a variable vector \mathbf c , defined via smooth division. """ md""" Chen, Y., and S. Fomel, 2015, Random noise attenuation using local signal and noise orthogonalization Geophysics, 80, WD1–WD9. """ md""" Random noise We will first apply local orthogonalization for random noise removal. The data example is 2 D seismic reflection data from the National Petroleum Reserve in Alaska. https wiki.seg.org wiki Alaska\ 2D\ land\ line\ 31 81 https wiki.seg.org wiki Alaska 2D land line 31 81 """ begin url "http s3.amazonaws.com open.source.geoscience open data alaska line31 81 31 81 PR.SGY" download url, "alaska.sgy" end begin seismic segy read "alaska.sgy" alaska Float32. seismic.data 1 1001, end nt, nx size alaska ta range start 0, length nt, step 0.004 time axis plot alaska data, title heatmap 1 nx, ta, data, clim 2000, 2000 , legend none, yflip true, cmap grays, title title, ylabel "Time s " plot alaska alaska, "Seismic Stack from Alaska" mutable struct HelixFilter lag Vector CartesianIndex flt Vector HelixFilter lag,flt new map CartesianIndex,lag ,flt HelixFilter lag HelixFilter lag,zeros Float32, length lag end function helix a HelixFilter, ci CartesianIndices "convert helix lags to 1 D for a given grid" middle of the grid mid CartesianIndex Tuple last ci .÷ 2 helix index of middle hmid LinearIndices ci mid from Cartesian shift to helix shift return LinearIndices ci map x x mid, a.lag . hmid end Base.length a HelixFilter Base.length a.lag function deriv x Vector T order 6 where T Real "derivative filter" n length x nt t, y deepcopy x , similar x h, trace2 for it in order 1 1 for i in 2 n 1 y i t i t i 1 t i 1 2 end y 1 , y n y 2 , y n 1 for i in 1 n t i x i y i it 2 it 1 end end y 1 t 2 t 1 for i in 2 n 1 y i t i 1 t i 1 2 end y n t n t n 1 return y end function pef lag2 n1, n2 find PEF lags in 2D nc floor Int, n1 2 lag Array Tuple Int, Int undef, n1 nc 1 n1 n2 1 i 1 for i2 in 1 n2 n0 i2 1 ? nc 2 1 for i1 in n0 n1 lag i i1 nc 1, i2 1 i 1 end end return lag end function hconvolve x, a HelixFilter, adjoint false cx, nx, na CartesianIndices x , length x , length a lag helix a, cx y deepcopy x accounting for the zero coefficient for ia in 1 na for iy in 1 lag ia nx ix iy lag ia if adjoint correlation y ix x iy a.flt ia else convolution y iy x ix a.flt ia end end end return y end function hrecursive x, a HelixFilter, adjoint false cx, nx, na CartesianIndices x , length x , length a lag helix a, cx y similar x if adjoint for ix in nx 1 1 t x ix for ia in 1 na iy ix lag ia if iy nx t a.flt ia y iy end end y ix t end else for ix in 1 nx t x ix for ia in 1 na iy ix lag ia if iy 1 t a.flt ia y iy end end y ix t end end return y end function pef f, x, a HelixFilter, mask convolution with a prediction error filter nx, na length x , length a lag helix a, CartesianIndices x y zeros eltype x , size x for ia in 1 na for iy in 1 lag ia nx if mask iy skip missing points ix iy lag ia y iy x ix f ia end end end return y end function pef adjoint y, x, a HelixFilter, mask adjoint convolution with a prediction error filter nx, na length x , length a lag helix a, CartesianIndices x f zeros eltype a.flt , na for ia in 1 na for iy in 1 lag ia nx if mask iy skip missing points ix iy lag ia f ia x ix y iy end end end return f end function conjgrad forward Function, adjoint Function, d Array, x0 Array, niter Int "Conjugate gradients for minimizing |forward x d|^2" x deepcopy x0 R forward x . d s, S similar x , similar d gnp zero eltype x for iter in 1 niter g adjoint R G forward g gn g ⋅ g show iter, gn if iter 1 steepest descent s, S g, G else β gn gnp s g β s S G β S end gnp gn α gn S ⋅ S x α s R α S end return x end conjugate gradients with preconditioning function conjgrad forward Function, adjoint Function, precon Function, precon adjoint Function, d Array, x0 Array, ϵ Real, niter Int "Conjugate gradients for minimizing |forward x d|^2 ϵ^2 |regul x |^2" x deepcopy x0 R1 forward precon x d R2 ϵ x nd length d s, S1, S2 similar x , similar R1 , similar R2 gnp zero eltype x for iter in 1 niter g precon adjoint adjoint R1 ϵ R2 block adjoint G1 forward precon g G2 ϵ g gn g ⋅ g if iter 1 steepest descent s, S1, S2 g, G1, G2 else β gn gnp s g β s S1 G1 β S1 S2 G2 β S2 end gnp gn α gn S1 ⋅ S1 S2 ⋅ S2 x α s R1 α S1 R2 α S2 Rn real R1 ⋅ R1 R2 ⋅ R2 length of the residual show iter, Rn end return precon x end function smooth division numerator Array, denominator Array, radius Vector Int niter 100 n length numerator p0 similar numerator fill p0, zero eltype p0 normalization norm denominator ⋅ denominator if norm 0.0 return p0 end norm sqrt n norm weighting function weight x x . denominator . norm return conjgrad weight, weight, x LocalSignalAttributes.smooth x, radius , 1.0, numerator norm, p0, niter end function agc input Array, radius Vector Int niter 100 automatic gain control ione ones size input gain abs. input return smooth division ione, gain, radius, niter niter end gain agc shot, 10, 1000 , niter 500 gained gain . shot begin pg heatmap x, t, gain 1 1001, , yflip true, legend false, xlabel "Offset km ", ylabel "Time s ", title " b Gain" pa plot seismic gained, " c AGC" plot pshot, pg, pa, layout 1, 3 end function find mask known, lag make a filter of all ones a HelixFilter lag a.flt . one Float32 sert ones at unknown locations x ones Float32, size known x known . zero Float32 y hconvolve x, a return y . zero Float32 end nlag pef lag2 3, 1 slag pef lag2 5, 3 function find pef data, lag niter length lag f zeros eltype data , length lag a HelixFilter lag, f mask find mask ones Bool,size data , lag a.flt conjgrad f pef f, data, a, mask , x pef adjoint x, data, a, mask , data, f, niter return a end function signal noise data, signal pef, noise pef ϵ 1.0, niter 50 PEF based signal and noise separation forward x hconvolve x, noise pef, false adjoint x hconvolve x, noise pef, true precon x hrecursive x, signal pef, false precon adjoint x hrecursive x, signal pef, true x0 zeros eltype data , size data signal conjgrad forward, adjoint, precon, precon adjoint, forward data , x0, ϵ, niter return signal, data signal end begin npef find pef alaska, nlag, niter 4 spef find pef alaska, slag, niter 30 end as0, an0 signal noise alaska, spef, npef, ϵ 4 plot alaska as0, "Estimated Signal" plot alaska an0, "Estimated Noise" ns smooth division an0, as0, 100, 10 , niter 200 begin an1 an0 ns . as0 as1 alaska an1 end plot alaska as1, "Improved Signal" plot alaska an1, "Improved Noise" md""" Coherent noise For a case of coherent noise, our initial denoising will involve bandpass filtering. """ spectrum mean abs. rfft gained, 1 , dims 2 fs rfftfreq 2000, 1 0.002 frequency axis plot fs, spectrum, title "Spectrum", xlabel "Frequency Hz ", linewidth 2, label none plot fs, spectrum, title "Spectrum at Low Frequencies", xlabel "Frequency Hz ", linewidth 2, label none, xlim 0,50 function butterworth lowpass Bool, x Vector T , freq, np Int where T Real "Bandpass filtering using Butterworth filters" nx length x arg 2π freq sinf, cosf sin arg , cos arg fact lowpass ? sin arg 2 cos arg 2 fact fact for j in 1 np loop over poles ss sin π 2 j 1 4 np sinf denominator filter d0, d1, d2 fact 1 ss , 2 cosf fact, 1 ss fact forward and backward convolution for range in 1 nx, nx 1 1 x1 x0 y1 y2 zero T for ix in range x2, x1, x0 x1, x0, x ix y0 lowpass ? x0 2 x1 x2 d1 y1 d2 y2 d0 x0 2 x1 x2 d1 y1 d2 y2 d0 y2, y1, x ix y1, y0, y0 end end end end function bandpass x Vector T , f1, f2, np Int, dt where T Real y deepcopy x if f1 none high pass filtering butterworth false, y, f1 dt, np end if f2 none low pass filtering butterworth true, y, f2 dt, np end return y end noise0 mapslices trace bandpass trace, none, 25, 10, 0.002 , gained dims 1 signal0 gained noise0 begin pd plot seismic gained, " a Data" pn0 plot seismic noise0, " b Est. Noise" ps0 plot seismic signal0, " c Est. Signal" plot pd, pn0, ps0, layout 1, 3 end γ smooth division noise0, signal0, 10, 2 , niter 200 begin noise noise0 γ . signal0 signal gained noise end begin pn plot seismic noise, " b Est. Noise" ps plot seismic signal, " c Est. Signal" plot pd, pn, ps, layout 1, 3 end md""" assignment Task 4 Repeat the exercise above, but switch the roles of noise and signal. 1. Use a different cut off frequency `f2` in the call to `bandpass` so that the initial estimated noise is signal free while the initial signal is contaminated with noise. 2. Orthogonalize signal and noise through minimization \displaystyle \min \gamma \left|\mathbf s 0 \gamma\,\mathbf n 0\right|^2\ . 3. Compare the results. """

title: Local Similarity
text: A Pluto.jl notebook v0.20.27 frontmatter chapter 4 section 23 order 23 image "https raw.githubusercontent.com fonsp Pluto.jl 580ab811f13d565cc81ebfa70ed36c84b125f55d demo plutodemo.gif" title "Local Similarity" tags "module4", "track julia", "track material", "Pluto", "PlutoUI" layout "layout.jlhtml" using Markdown using InteractiveUtils begin using PlutoUIExtra TableOfContents end using LinearAlgebra using Plots using FFTW using Interpolations using LaTeXStrings using DynamicAxisWarping, Distances import HTTP let notebook dir DIR toc path joinpath notebook dir, "TOC Notebook.jl" Markdown.parse " ⬅️ Back to Table of Contents . open?path HTTP.escapeuri toc path " end md""" Local similarity One type of data analysis involves identifying correspondences between two or more datasets. This occurs, for instance, when comparing time lapse measurements from monitoring of subsurface fluids. In other cases, separate datasets may represent different parts of the same data, such as neighboring traces in a seismic record. Local similarity is an attribute that measures how well two datasets match in a non stationary manner, accounting for changes in similarity across different locations. """ md""" Fomel, S., 2007. Local seismic attributes. Geophysics, 72 3 , pp.A29 A33. """ md""" Measuring shifts A subclass of the matching problem occurs when the difference is a shift that can change over time. Shifts between different parts of the same dataset, such as neighboring seismic traces, represent slopes of local events and fall into the same category. """ md""" In the scanning and picking approach, different shifts between the two datasets are scanned to measure their similarity. Maximum similarity indicates a good alignment from which one can select a variable shift. This approach has limitations, but it works effectively in practice. """ md""" What to scan? Let d 1 t and d 2 t be the two datasets to compare. We want to find the shift \tau t such that d 1 t \approx d 2 t \tau t . The scanning approach involves testing constant shifts and creating a similarity measure S 1 t,\tau that evaluates the similarity between d 1 t and d 2 t \tau . Subsequently, a variable trend \tau t is selected based on S 1 t,\tau . """ md""" An alternative to shifting in time is stretching in time d 2 t \tau is replaced by d 2 \phi\,t , and the scan computes S 2 t,\phi instead of S 1 t,\tau . We can easily convert the picked stretch and shift into each other. From the equation t \tau t \phi t \,t\ , we find that \tau t \left \phi t 1\right \,t or \phi t 1 \tau t t\ . The benefit of analyzing the scanning stretches \phi rather than shifts \tau is that the former has a straightforward link to changes in velocity. If the time shift between two datasets results from a change in velocity, such that the reflection from depth z appears at time t 1 z v 1 in the first dataset and at time t 2 z v 2 in the second dataset, then \phi corresponds to the ratio v 1 v 2 . When velocities vary with depth, the time becomes an integral measure t 2 \displaystyle \int \frac dz v 2 \int\limits 0 ^ t 1 \frac v 1 \tau v 2 \tau \,d\tau\ . Therefore, the derivative of the stretch function over time d\phi dt offers an estimate of the interval velocity ratio \frac d \phi d t \frac v 2 t v 1 t \ . """ md""" How to measure similarity? How can we determine if the two signals are aligned after warping? One method is to measure the difference directly, possibly raising it to some power q , as follows D t,\tau |d 1 t d 2 t \tau |^q\ . Small values of D t,\tau indicate high similarity. The drawback of this method is its heavy reliance on amplitudes if two signals are similar in shape but scaled differently, they can appear to differ significantly. """ md""" An alternative is the local similarity measure. The local similarity C t,\tau is a non stationary extension of the correlation coefficient. In matrix vector notation, it can be defined as the point by point product of the vectors \mathbf c 1 and \mathbf c 2 , where \begin array rcl \mathbf c 1 & & \left \lambda 1^2\,\mathbf I \mathbf S \,\left \mathbf D 1^2 \lambda 1^2\,\mathbf I \right \right ^ 1 \, \mathbf S \,\mathbf D 1\,\mathbf d 2\ , \\ \mathbf c 2 & & \left \lambda 2^2\,\mathbf I \mathbf S \,\left \mathbf D 2^2 \lambda 2^2\,\mathbf I \right \right ^ 1 \, \mathbf S \,\mathbf D 2\,\mathbf d 1\ . \end array Here, \mathbf d 1 and \mathbf d 2 are vectors representing d 1 t and d 2 t \tau , respectively. \mathbf D 1 and \mathbf D 2 are diagonal matrices constructed from these vectors, while \lambda 1 and \lambda 2 denote the magnitudes of these vectors. \mathbf S is a shaping smoothing operator. Due to normalization, the local similarity is relatively insensitive to differences in magnitude. Its locality is governed by the smoothing radius in \mathbf S and can extend across multiple dimensions. """ function conjgrad forward Function, adjoint Function, shaping Function, ϵ Float64, d Array, p0 Array, niter Int tol 1.0e 6 "Conjugate gradient algorithm for shaping regularization" p deepcopy p0 x shaping p r forward x . d sp, sx, sr similar p , similar x , similar r g0 gnp zero eltype x for iter in 1 niter gx adjoint r ϵ x gp shaping gx ϵ p gx shaping gp gr forward gx gn gp ⋅ gp show iter, gn if iter 1 sp, sx, sr gp, gx, gr g0 gn else β gn gnp γ gn g0 if β tol || gn g0 tol println "converged β β γ γ " break end sp gp β sp sx gx β sx sr gr β sr end gnp gn α sr ⋅ sr ϵ sp ⋅ sp sx ⋅ sx α gn α p p α sp x x α sx r r α sr end return x end function doubint x Vector T where T Real "causal and anticausal integration in place" n length x for range in 1 n, n 1 1 t zero T for i in range t x i x i t end end end function fold t Vector T , nb Integer where T Real "reflecting boundary conditions for smoothing" nt length t n nt 2 nb copy middle x t 1 nb n nb reflections from the right side for j in nb n 2 n nt for i in 1 min n,nt j x n 1 i t j i end for i in 1 min n,nt j n x i t j n i end end reflections from the left side for j in nb 1 2 n 1 for i in 1 min n,j 1 x i t j i end for i in 1 min n,j 1 n x n 1 i t j i n end end return x end function smooth x Vector T , nb Int where T Real "smoothing by triangle filtering with reflecting boundaries" n length x t zeros T,n 2 nb for i in 1 n xi x i nb nb t i xi t i nb 2 xi t i 2 nb xi end doubint t return fold t, nb end function smooth x Array, nb Vector Int "multidimensional smoothing" y deepcopy x loop over dimensions for dim in 1 length nb y mapslices slice smooth slice, nb dim , y dims dim end return y end function smooth division numerator Array, denominator Array, radius Vector Int niter 100 n length numerator p0 similar numerator fill p0, zero eltype p0 normalization norm denominator ⋅ denominator if norm 0.0 return zeros n, eltype ratio end norm sqrt n norm show norm weighting function weight x x . denominator . norm return conjgrad weight, weight, x smooth x, radius , 1.0, numerator norm, p0, niter end function local similarity one, two, radius rat1 smooth division one, two, radius rat2 smooth division two, one, radius return rat1 . rat2 end md""" We will evaluate the similarity scan method using a toy synthetic data example. In this case, a simple layered subsurface model includes a reservoir with a seismic velocity change due to fluids. """ layers 300,500,600,700,800 begin velocity model before and after vel1 1500,1700,2000,2300,2400,2500 vel2 deepcopy vel1 vel2 3 2200 end begin from depth to time time1 cumsum 2 layers . vel1 1 5 time2 cumsum 2 layers . vel2 1 5 end begin depth cumsum vcat layers, 0 depths vcat 0 , reshape hcat depth, depth ', 12 1 10 times1 vcat 0 , reshape hcat time1, time1 ', 10 1 10 vels1 reshape hcat vel1, vel1 ', 12 1 10 vels2 reshape hcat vel2, vel2 ', 12 1 10 end plot depths, vels1 vels2 , label "before" "after" , linestyle solid dash , xlabel "Depth m ", ylabel "Velocity m s ", title "Synthetic Velocity Model" md""" The model exists in depth, but our data will be in time corresponding to when the reflections arrive at the surface. The two way travel time is often used as the vertical coordinate in seismic reflection imaging. """ plot times1, vels1 vels2 , label "before" "after" , linestyle solid dash , xlabel "Time s ", ylabel "Velocity m s ", title "Synthetic Velocity Model" md""" To generate a synthetic seismic image, we convert velocity to reflectivity and convolve it with a seismic wavelet. """ function reflectivity time Vector, vel Vector, nt, dt ref zeros eltype vel , nt for ik in 1 length time it 1 round Int, time ik dt ref it vel ik 1 vel ik vel ik 1 vel ik end return ref end begin ref1 reflectivity time1, Float32. vel1 , 1000, 0.004 ref2 reflectivity time2, Float32. vel2 , 1000, 0.004 end function ricker input Array dt 1.0, f 0.2 convolution with a Ricker wavelet fourier rfft input nt length input freq rfftfreq nt, 1 dt Ricker wavelet freq freq f freq freq . freq . fourier freq exp 1 freq return irfft fourier, nt end begin base ricker ref1, dt 0.004, f 10 monitor ricker ref2, dt 0.004, f 10 diff monitor base end t range start 0, step 0.004, length 1000 begin pb plot t, base, linewidth 2, xaxis false, label "base" pm plot t, monitor, linewidth 2, xaxis false, color green, label "monitor" pd plot t, diff, linewidth 2, xlabel "Time s ", color red, label "difference" plot pb, pm, pd, layout 3, 1 end md""" When conducting seismic surveys before and after fluid changes base and monitor acquisition , we can detect changes in data caused by variations in both seismic velocity and seismic reflectivity at the top and bottom of the reservoir. To distinguish between these effects, it is necessary to align the two datasets, which becomes a purely data analysis task. """ md""" Fomel, S. and Jin, L., 2009. Time lapse image registration using the local similarity attribute. Geophysics, 74 2 , pp.A7 A11. """ function warpscan1 inp, oth, time, ϕ radius 1, 1 n1 length inp ng length ϕ warp zeros eltype inp , n1, ng other similar warp intp cubic spline interpolation time, inp for ig in 1 ng loop over stretch squeeze for i1 in 1 n1 τ time i1 ϕ ig if time 1 τ time n1 warp i1, ig intp τ end other i1, ig oth i1 end end return local similarity warp, other, radius end ϕ range start 0.95, stop 1.05, length 101 scan warpscan1 monitor, base, t, ϕ, radius 50, 1 scan1 scan . 1 .^4 16 sharpen heatmap t, ϕ, scan1', yflip true, legend none, xlabel "Time s ", ylabel "Stretch Squeeze", title "Local Similarity Scan" md""" How to pick? Picking a curve \tau t or \phi t from the similarity measure is the next step of the process. The key idea is to treat the local similarity scan as a velocity distribution, use a paraxial eikonal solver to compute the corresponding travel time, and extract the ray path that follows the minimal travel time trajectory. """ function find minimum ic, nc, jc, c, prob Vector finding local minimum if 1 ic ic 1 fm, f0, fp c, prob ic , prob ic 1 elseif nc ic ic 1 fm, f0, fp prob ic 1 , prob ic , c else fm, f0, fp prob ic 1 , c, prob ic 1 end ic jc a fm fp 2 f0 if a 0.0 no minimum if fm f0 && fm fp return fm, ic 1 end if fp f0 && fp fm return fp, ic 1 end return f0, ic end b 0.5 fm fp a b a if a 1.0 return fp, ic 1 end if a 1.0 return fm, ic 1 end if f0 0.5 b a return f0, ic end f0 0.5 b a return f0, ic a end function dynprog i0, scan an 1.0, gate 3 dynamic programming paraxial eikonal solver n1, n2 size scan pseudo slowness weight exp. scan' transpose T eltype scan dist Array T undef, n2 prev similar dist next similar dist what similar weight prob Array T undef, 2 gate 1 time similar weight for i2 in 1 n2 dist i2 sqrt i2 1 ^2 an^2 end for i2 in 1 n2 w 0.5 weight i2,1 weight i0,1 time i2,1 abs i2 i0 w end for i2 in 1 n2 w 0.5 weight i2,2 weight i0,1 prev i2 dist 1 abs i2 i0 w what i2,2 i0 time i2,2 prev i2 end for i1 in 3 n1 for i2 in 1 n2 w weight i2, i1 ib max i2 gate,0 ie min i2 gate,n2 1 c typemax T infinity ic 0 for i in ib 1 ie 1 w2 0.5 w weight i,i1 1 d dist 1 abs i2 i w2 prev i it i ib if d c c d ic it end prob it d end next i2 , what i2, i1 find minimum ic,ie ib 1,ib,c,prob end prev deepcopy next time ,i1 prev end return next, what end function interpolate f, slice linear interpolation n length slice k floor Int, f if k n 1 return slice n end if k 1 return slice 1 end w f k return slice k 1.0 w slice k 1 w end function dynprog traj next, what extract trajectory from dynamic programming n2, n1 size what T eltype what traj Array T undef, n1 minimum at the bottom c, fc findmin next for i1 in n1 1 1 traj i1 fc fc interpolate fc, what ,i1 end return traj end automatic picking function autopick scan an 1.0, gate 3, o2 0.0, d2 1.0, start o2, radius 1 n1, n2 size scan pick Array eltype scan undef, n1 ampl similar pick i0 min max 1, round Int, 1.0 start o2 d2 , n2 next, what dynprog i0, scan an an, gate gate pick dynprog traj next, what smoothing for i1 in 1 n1 ct pick i1 it floor Int, ct if it n2 ampl i1 scan i1, n2 elseif it 1 ampl i1 scan i1, 1 else ct it ampl i1 scan i1, it 1.0 ct scan i1,it 1 ct end end pick pick . 1 d2 . o2 pick pick . start . ampl pick smooth division pick, ampl, radius . start return pick end md""" Deschamps, T., and L. D. Cohen, 2001, Fast extraction of minimal paths in 3D images and applications to virtual endoscopy Medical Image Analysis, 5, 281 299. Fomel, S., 2009. Velocity analysis using AB semblance. Geophysical Prospecting, 57 3 , pp.311 321. """ pick autopick scan, start 1, radius 50, o2 ϕ 1 , d2 ϕ 2 ϕ 1 begin heatmap t, ϕ, scan1', yflip true, legend none, xlabel "Time s ", ylabel "Stretch Squeeze", title "Local Similarity Scan" plot t, pick, legend none, linewidth 2, color white end md""" Once we select the \phi t trajectory, we can use it to warp the monitor trace, thereby better matching the base trace. """ function warp1 inp, time, ϕ n1 length inp warped zeros eltype inp , n1 intp cubic spline interpolation time, inp for i1 in 1 length inp τ time i1 ϕ i1 if time 1 τ time n1 warped i1 intp τ end end return warped end monitor1 warp1 monitor, t, pick begin pm1 plot t, monitor1, linewidth 2, xaxis false, color green, label "warped monitor" pd1 plot t, monitor1 base, linewidth 2, xlabel "Time s ", color red, label "difference" plot pb, pm1, pd1, layout 3, 1 end md""" After warping, the difference accurately shows the reflectivity changes at both the top and bottom of the reservoir, which can be useful for monitoring. We can also convert the measured stretch \phi t to the ratio of the two interval velocities. """ intp cubic spline interpolation t, t . pick tint intp time1 begin dtime time1 vcat 0 ,time1 1 5 dtint tint vcat 0 ,tint 1 5 rat dtime . dtint estimated velocity ratio trat vel2 . vel1 true velocity ratio end begin rat2 reshape hcat rat, rat ', 10 trat2 reshape hcat trat, trat ', 12 1 10 end plot times1, trat2 rat2 , label "true" "estimated" , linestyle solid dash , xlabel "Time s ", ylabel L"v 2 v 1", title "Velocity Ratio", ylim 0,2 md""" assignment Task 1 The picking algorithm internally computes `time`, which is the traveltime from the source based on a numerical solution of the eikonal equation. Output and plot it together with the picked trajectory to verify that the pick follows the minimal traveltime. """ md""" assignment Task 2 Modify `warpscan1` and `warp1` to scan time shifts \tau instead of \phi . Choose an appropriate range of shifts to scan, apply the modified code, and compare the results. """ md""" Dynamic Time Warping Dynamic Time Warping DTW , originally developed for speech processing, is a variation of the scanning and picking method. It makes the following choices 1. Scanning involves time shifts and uses only integer shifts relative to time sampling. 2. DTW employs basic absolute difference or squared difference to measure similarity. 3. Picking also involves only integer shifts and employs dynamic programming as a discrete form of an eikonal solver. The computation is fast, but the accuracy is limited. """ md""" Hale, D., 2013. Dynamic warping of seismic images. Geophysics, 78 2 , pp.S105 S115. """ apply Dynamic Time Warping cost, i1, i2 fastdtw base, monitor, SqEuclidean , 1000 i1 i2 begin plot showaxis false for k in 1 20 length i1 plot i1 k , i2 k , 0.02, 0 , label none, color black end plot base . 0.02, monitor , label "base" "monitor" , title "Dynamic Time Warping", linewidth 2 end dtwplot base, monitor md""" assignment Task 3 Apply the shift generated by DTW to the monitor trace and compare the results with those from the local similarity scan. """ md""" assignment Task 4 Let's now look at a real data application. The data come from a seismic monitoring experiment in Indonesia, where repeated surveys were used to monitor steam injection. Lumley, D., 2001, Time lapse seismic reservoir monitoring Geophysics, 66, 50 53. Fomel, S. and Jin, L., 2009. Time lapse image registration using the local similarity attribute. Geophysics, 74 2 , pp.A7 A11. """ begin download "https ahay.org data dury slice0.rsf ","base.bin" download "https ahay.org data dury slice5.rsf ","monitor.bin" end begin base2 Array Float32 undef, 301, 72 monitor2 similar base2 read "base.bin", base2 read "monitor.bin", monitor2 end Time lapse difference diff2 monitor2 base2 begin t2 range length 301, step 0.001, start 0 x2 range length 72, step 0.005, start 0 end plot2 data, title heatmap x2, t2, data, cmap grays, title title, clim 3, 3 , legend none, yflip true, xlabel "Distance km ", ylabel "Time s " begin pb2 plot2 base2, "Before Injection" pm2 plot2 monitor2, "After Injection" pd2 plot2 diff2, "Difference" plot pb2, pm2, pd2, layout 1, 3 end md""" Coherent events in the time lapse difference above the reservoir level of 0.15 s are artifacts caused by changes in near surface conditions. We can try to remove them by applying data registration. """ function warpscan2 inp, oth, time, ϕ radius 1, 1, 1 Scanning local similarity for 2D input n1, n2 size inp ng length ϕ warp zeros eltype inp , n1, ng, n2 other similar warp for i2 in 1 n2 intp cubic spline interpolation time, inp ,i2 for ig in 1 ng loop over stretch squeeze for i1 in 1 n1 τ time i1 ϕ ig if time 1 τ time n1 warp i1, ig, i2 intp τ end other i1, ig, i2 oth i1, i2 end end end return local similarity warp, other, radius end ϕ2 range start 0.9, stop 1.1, length 101 scan2 warpscan2 monitor2, base2, t2, ϕ2, radius 20, 1, 3 function autopick2 scan an 1.0, gate 3, o2 0.0, d2 1.0, start o2, radius 1, 1 Automatic picking for 2D data n1, n2, n3 size scan pick Array eltype scan undef, n1, n3 ampl similar pick i0 min max 1, round Int, 1.0 start o2 d2 , n2 for i3 in 1 n3 slice scan , ,i3 next, what dynprog i0, slice an an, gate gate pick , i3 dynprog traj next, what for i1 in 1 n1 ct pick i1, i3 it floor Int, ct if it n2 ampl i1, i3 slice i1, n2 elseif it 1 ampl i1, i3 slice i1, 1 else ct it ampl i1, i3 slice i1, it 1.0 ct slice i1,it 1 ct end end end pick pick . 1 d2 . o2 pick pick . start . ampl pick smooth division pick, ampl, radius . start return pick end md""" Your task Complete the exercise and plot the time lapse difference after registration. """

title: Regularized Nonstationary Regression
text: A Pluto.jl notebook v0.20.27 frontmatter chapter 4 section 24 order 24 image "https raw.githubusercontent.com fonsp Pluto.jl 580ab811f13d565cc81ebfa70ed36c84b125f55d demo plutodemo.gif" title "Regularized Nonstationary Regression" tags "module4", "track julia", "track material", "Pluto", "PlutoUI" layout "layout.jlhtml" using Markdown using InteractiveUtils begin using PlutoUIExtra TableOfContents end using LinearAlgebra, Random using Statistics using Plots using ZipFile import HTTP let notebook dir DIR toc path joinpath notebook dir, "TOC Notebook.jl" Markdown.parse " ⬅️ Back to Table of Contents . open?path HTTP.escapeuri toc path " end md""" Regularized Nonstationary Regression Linear regression is a common example of an overdetermined inverse problem, where an input dataset d \mathbf x is expressed as a combination of given basis functions f k \mathbf x . d \mathbf x \approx \displaystyle \sum k 1 ^ N a k\,f k \mathbf x with coefficients a k determined through least squares minimization of the misfit \displaystyle \min a k \| d \mathbf x \displaystyle \sum k 1 ^ N a k\,f k \mathbf x \|^2\ . """ md""" In practice, linear regression does not always produce satisfactory results because of non stationarity sometimes, dependence varies across different parts of the data. What if we changed the problem by making the coefficients variable? d \mathbf x \approx \displaystyle \sum k 1 ^ N b k \mathbf x \,f k \mathbf x \ . The problem becomes highly underdetermined and lacks a reasonable solution unless we add extra constraints. These constraints might involve shaping regularization. For example, by constraining the smoothness of b k \mathbf x , we can control the non stationarity of regression coefficients. """ md""" Mathematically, the regularized inversion for coefficients \mathbf b \left \begin array cccc b 1 & b 2 & \cdots & b N \end array \right ^T\ , using \mathbf S is a shaping operator, takes the form \widehat \mathbf b \mathbf A ^ 1 \,\widehat \mathbf d \ , where \widehat \mathbf d \left \begin array cccc \mathbf S \left f 1 \mathbf x \,d \mathbf x \right & \mathbf S \left f 2 \mathbf x \,d \mathbf x \right & \cdots & \mathbf S \left f N \mathbf x \,d \mathbf x \right \end array \right ^T\ , the elements of matrix \mathbf A are A ij \mathbf x \lambda^2\,\delta ij \mathbf S \left d i \mathbf x \,d j \mathbf x \lambda^2\,\delta ij \right \ , and \lambda is a scaling coefficient. """ md""" The case of local attributes we discussed earlier corresponds to N 1 . """ md""" Fomel, S., 2009, Adaptive multiple subtraction using regularized nonstationary regression Geophysics, v. 74, V25 V33. """ function regression f Array, b Array n size f d zeros eltype b ,n 1 end 1 nk, nx n end , length d for k in 1 nk, x in 1 nx xk x k 1 nx d x f xk b xk end return d end function regression adjoint f Array, d Array n size f nk, nx n end , length d b Array eltype d undef, n for k in 1 nk, x in 1 nx xk x k 1 nx b xk f xk d x end return b end function dottest forward Function, adjoint Function, m Array, d Array "Dot product test" mod similar m rand mod dat similar d rand dat println " L m ⋅d forward mod ⋅ dat " println "L' d ⋅m adjoint dat ⋅ mod " end f rand 10,5,3 dottest x regression f, x , y regression adjoint f, y , f, f , ,1 function doubint x Vector T where T Real "causal and anticausal integration in place" n length x for range in 1 n, n 1 1 t zero T for i in range t x i x i t end end end function fold t Vector T , nb Integer where T Real "reflecting boundary conditions for smoothing" nt length t n nt 2 nb copy middle x t 1 nb n nb reflections from the right side for j in nb n 2 n nt for i in 1 min n,nt j x n 1 i t j i end for i in 1 min n,nt j n x i t j n i end end reflections from the left side for j in nb 1 2 n 1 for i in 1 min n,j 1 x i t j i end for i in 1 min n,j 1 n x n 1 i t j i n end end return x end function smooth x Vector T , nb Int where T Real "smoothing by triangle filtering with reflecting boundaries" n length x t zeros T,n 2 nb for i in 1 n xi x i nb nb t i xi t i nb 2 xi t i 2 nb xi end doubint t return fold t, nb end function smooth x Array, nb Vector Int "multidimensional smoothing" y deepcopy x loop over dimensions for dim in 1 length nb y mapslices slice smooth slice, nb dim , y dims dim end return y end function conjgrad forward Function, adjoint Function, shaping Function, ϵ Float64, d Array, p0 Array, niter Int tol 1.0e 6 "Conjugate gradient algorithm for shaping regularization" p deepcopy p0 x shaping p r forward x . d sp, sx, sr similar p , similar x , similar r g0 gnp zero eltype x for iter in 1 niter gx adjoint r ϵ x gp shaping gx ϵ p gx shaping gp gr forward gx gn gp ⋅ gp show iter, gn if iter 1 sp, sx, sr gp, gx, gr g0 gn else β gn gnp γ gn g0 if β tol || gn g0 tol println "converged β β γ γ " break end sp gp β sp sx gx β sx sr gr β sr end gnp gn α sr ⋅ sr ϵ sp ⋅ sp sx ⋅ sx α gn α p p α sp x x α sx r r α sr end return x end function regularized regression data Array, basis Array, radius Array niter 100 p0 zeros eltype data , size basis normalization norm basis ⋅ basis norm sqrt length basis norm return conjgrad x regression basis norm, x , y regression adjoint basis norm, y , x smooth x, radius , 1.0, data norm, p0, niter end md""" Line fitting An elementary example of regression is line fitting d x \approx a 1 a 2\,x\ . In this case, the basis functions are f 1 x 1 and f 2 x x . """ x collect 1 50 plane x 0.1 . 3 noisy plane randn 50 0.1 scatter noisy, label "input data" begin f1 ones 50 f2 x fk hcat f1, f2 end ak regularized regression noisy, fk, 1000 begin scatter noisy, label "input data" plot regression fk, ak , linewidth 2, label "fit", title "Stationary Regression" end bk regularized regression noisy, fk, 10 begin scatter noisy, label "input data" plot regression fk, bk , linewidth 2, label "fit", color "green", title "Nonstationary Regression" end curve sqrt. x .^ 2 0.025 . 9 noisy2 curve randn 50 0.1 ak2 regularized regression noisy2, fk, 1000 begin scatter noisy2, label "input data" plot regression fk, ak2 , linewidth 2, label "fit", title "Stationary Regression" end md""" As the plot shows, stationary regression might not be suitable when the input data exhibit nonstationary behavior. One way to address this is to divide the data into smaller windows or patches and assume stationary behavior within each one. """ begin fk1, fk2 fk 1 25, , fk 26 50, akp1 regularized regression noisy2 1 25 , fk1, 1000 akp2 regularized regression noisy2 26 50 , fk2, 1000 end begin scatter noisy2, label "input data" plot 1 25, regression fk1, akp1 , linewidth 2, label none, color "purple", title "Regression in Patches" plot 26 50, regression fk2, akp2 , linewidth 2, label "fit", color "purple", title "Regression in Patches" end md""" Regularized nonstationary regression, using the smoothing radius as the control parameter, offers a more convenient way to manage the fit. """ bk2 regularized regression noisy2, fk, 10 begin scatter noisy2, label "input data" plot regression fk, bk2 , linewidth 2, label "fit", color "green", title "Nonstationary Regression" end md""" assignment Task 1 Make a movie an animated GIF showing the results of line fitting using nonstationary regression with varying smoothness radius. """ md""" Match filtering Match filtering between two datasets is a specific type of regression where the basis functions are shifts of one dataset. The matching process functions as a convolution with a filter. In regularized nonstationary regression, the filter is adaptive and can vary across different parts of the data. """ function shifts1 data Array, ns Int create symmetric 1 D shifts n collect size data n1, n2 n 1 , prod n 2 end shift zeros eltype data , Tuple vcat n, 2 ns 1 for i2 in 1 n2, i1 in 1 n1, j1 in ns ns k1 i1 j1 if 1 k1 n1 k k1 i2 1 n1 j i1 i2 1 n1 j1 ns n1 n2 shift j data k end end return shift end download "https ahay.org data wz data.rsf ","data.bin" begin shot Array Float32 undef, 2000, 81 single precision array read "data.bin", shot shot 1.0e 8 normalize end begin tt range 0, 2, 1001 time axis xx range 2, 2, 81 offset axis end function butterworth lowpass Bool, x Vector T , freq, np Int where T Real "Bandpass filtering using Butterworth filters" nx length x arg 2π freq sinf, cosf sin arg , cos arg fact lowpass ? sin arg 2 cos arg 2 fact fact for j in 1 np loop over poles ss sin π 2 j 1 4 np sinf denominator filter d0, d1, d2 fact 1 ss , 2 cosf fact, 1 ss fact forward and backward convolution for range in 1 nx, nx 1 1 x1 x0 y1 y2 zero T for ix in range x2, x1, x0 x1, x0, x ix y0 lowpass ? x0 2 x1 x2 d1 y1 d2 y2 d0 x0 2 x1 x2 d1 y1 d2 y2 d0 y2, y1, x ix y1, y0, y0 end end end end function bandpass x Vector T , f1, f2, np Int, dt where T Real y deepcopy x if f1 none high pass filtering butterworth false, y, f1 dt, np end if f2 none low pass filtering butterworth true, y, f2 dt, np end return y end function smooth division numerator Array, denominator Array, radius Vector Int niter 100 n length numerator p0 similar numerator fill p0, zero eltype p0 normalization norm denominator ⋅ denominator if norm 0.0 return p0 end norm sqrt n norm weighting function weight x x . denominator . norm return conjgrad weight, weight, x smooth x, radius , 1.0, numerator norm, p0, niter end function agc input Array, radius Vector Int niter 100 automatic gain control ione ones size input gain abs. input return smooth division ione, gain, radius, niter niter end begin gain agc shot, 10, 1000 , niter 500 gained gain . shot end begin noise0 mapslices trace bandpass trace, none, 25, 10, 0.002 , gained dims 1 signal0 gained noise0 end function plot seismic data, title cmap grays clip quantile abs. data , 0.99 return heatmap xx, tt, data 1 1001, , clim clip, clip , yflip true, c cmap, legend none, xlabel "Offset km ", ylabel "Time s ", title title end begin pd plot seismic gained, " a Data" pn0 plot seismic noise0, " b Est. Noise" ps0 plot seismic signal0, " c Est. Signal" plot pd, pn0, ps0, layout 1, 3 end md""" We revisit the earlier example of signal and noise separation, but this time, instead of simple scaling, we will implement adaptive match filtering. """ shifts shifts1 signal0, 10 size shifts match regularized regression noise0, shifts, 25, 2 , niter 400 begin p1 plot seismic match , ,1 , " a First", cmap viridis p2 plot seismic match , ,11 , " b Middle", cmap viridis pm plot seismic sum match, dims 3 , " c Mean", cmap viridis plot p1, p2, pm, layout 1, 3 end begin pred regression shifts, match noise1 noise0 pred signal1 signal0 pred end begin pn1 plot seismic noise1, " b Est. Noise" ps1 plot seismic signal1, " c Est. Signal" plot pd, pn1, ps1, layout 1, 3 end md""" assignment Task 2 Apply regularized match filtering to the time lapse seismic images from the previous assignment, select appropriate parameters, and display the difference between the monitor and base images before and after the match. """ begin download "https ahay.org data dury slice0.rsf ","base.bin" download "https ahay.org data dury slice5.rsf ","monitor.bin" end begin base2 Array Float32 undef, 301, 72 monitor2 similar base2 read "base.bin", base2 read "monitor.bin", monitor2 end begin t2 range length 301, step 0.001, start 0 x2 range length 72, step 0.005, start 0 end plot2 data, title heatmap x2, t2, data, cmap grays, title title, clim 3, 3 , legend none, yflip true, xlabel "Distance km ", ylabel "Time s " begin pb2 plot2 base2, "Before Injection" pm2 plot2 monitor2, "After Injection" plot pb2, pm2, layout 1, 2 end md""" Adaptive prediction error filters Prediction error filters we studied earlier use autoregression, which involves fitting a dataset to itself with causal shifts. By making the filter variable through regularized nonstationary regression, we can adapt it to local changes in the data pattern. """ md""" Liu, Y. and Fomel, S., 2011. Seismic data interpolation beyond aliasing using regularized nonstationary autoregression. Geophysics, 76 5 , pp.V69 V77.
