""" function causal shifts1 inp, lag n1 length inp ns length lag T eltype inp shift Array T undef,n1,ns for is in 1 ns s lag is for i1 in 1 n1 k1 i1 s if 1 k1 n1 shift i1,is inp k1 else shift i1,is zero T end end end return shift end begin n1 400 exponentially decaying sinusiods with different frequencies inp zeros Float32,n1 freq similar inp for j in n1÷15 n1÷5 n1 wave zeros Float32,n1 for x in j 399 y x j 400 wave x exp y 15 sin y 0.95 j freq x 0.95 j 400 end inp wave end end shift1 causal shifts1 inp, 1,2 pef1 regularized regression inp, shift1, 7 PEF residual res1 inp regression shift1, pef1 function stems data, label, color "plot data using stems" plt plot zeros Float32, n1 , label none, color black plot plt, data, line stem, label label, color color, legend outerleft, xlim 0.5, n1 0.5 , border none return plt end begin plot input stems inp,"input", blue plot decon stems res1, "decon", green plot plot input, plot decon, layout 2, 1 end md""" A three point prediction error filter \ 1,a 1,a 2\ perfectly predicts an attenuating sinusoidal signal m x \rho^x\,cos \omega\,x \ , when the filter coefficients are defined as \begin array rcl a 1 & & 2\,\rho\,\cos \omega \ \\ a 2 & & \rho^2\ .\end array The following identity proves this fact \begin array l m x a 1\,m x 1 a 2\,m x 2 \\ \rho^x\,\left \cos \omega\,x 2\,\cos \omega \,\cos \omega\,x \omega \cos \omega\,x 2\,\omega \right 0\ .\end array """ md""" Therefore, we can get an estimate of the local frequency \omega x from the non stationary coefficients b 1 x a 1 x and b 2 x a 2 x , as follows \omega x \arccos\left \frac b 1 x 2\,\sqrt b 2 x \right \ . """ begin b1 pef1 ,1 b2 pef1 ,2 ω acos. b1 . 2 sqrt. b2 end plot freq ω , label "true frequency" "estimated frequency" , linewidth 2, linestyle solid dash md""" Let us proceed to adaptive PEFs in 2D. """ function causal shifts2 inp, lag n1, n2 size inp ns length lag T eltype inp shift Array T undef,n1,n2,ns for is in 1 ns s1, s2 lag is for i2 in 1 n2, i1 in 1 n1 k1, k2 i1 s1, i2 s2 if 1 k1 n1 && 1 k2 n2 shift i1,i2,is inp k1,k2 else shift i1,i2,is zero T end end end return shift end function pef lag2 n1, n2 find PEF lags in 2D nc floor Int, n1 2 lag Array Tuple Int, Int undef, n1 nc 1 n1 n2 1 i 1 for i2 in 1 n2 n0 i2 1 ? nc 2 1 for i1 in n0 n1 lag i i1 nc 1, i2 1 i 1 end end return lag end download data from a public server download "https zenodo.org api records 11099632 files archive", "files.zip" begin unzip the archive file r ZipFile.Reader "files.zip" make a dictionary of files for easy access patterns Dict String, IO for file in r.files name splitext file.name 1 patterns name file end "wood" pattern wood Array Float32 undef, 128, 128 single precision array read patterns "wood" , wood wood maximum wood "herring" pattern herr Array Float32 undef, 128, 128 single precision array read patterns "herr" , herr herr maximum herr "seismic" pattern seis Array Float32 undef, 250, 125 single precision array read patterns "seis" , seis seis maximum seis end grayplot data, title heatmap data, title title, yflip true, axis nothing, legend none, color grays lag pef lag2 10, 10 wood shift causal shifts2 wood, lag wood pef regularized regression wood, wood shift, 10, 10 function forward pef pef, inp, lag nonlinear regression residual n1, n2 size inp ns length lag T eltype inp res similar inp for i2 in 1 n2, i1 in 1 n1 shift zero T for is in 1 ns s1, s2 lag is k1, k2 i1 s1, i2 s2 if 1 k1 n1 && 1 k2 n2 shift inp k1,k2 pef i1,i2,is end end res i1,i2 inp i1,i2 shift end return res end function inverse pef pef, res, lag inverse recursive filtering n1, n2 size res ns length lag T eltype res inp similar res for i2 in 1 n2, i1 in 1 n1 shift zero T for is in 1 ns s1, s2 lag is k1, k2 i1 s1, i2 s2 if 1 k1 n1 && 1 k2 n2 shift inp k1,k2 pef i1,i2,is end end inp i1,i2 res i1,i2 shift end return inp end function test adaptive pef input shift causal shifts2 input, lag pef regularized regression input, shift, 10, 10 residual forward pef pef, input, lag random randn eltype input , size input pattern inverse pef pef, random, lag p1 grayplot input, "input" p2 grayplot residual, "input PEF" p3 grayplot pattern, "random PEF" return plot p1, p2, p3, layout 1, 3 end test adaptive pef wood test adaptive pef herr test adaptive pef seis md""" assignment Task 3 Test the performance of adaptive prediction error filters on other datasets, possibly including your own. """

title: Time-Frequency Analysis
text: A Pluto.jl notebook v0.20.27 frontmatter chapter 4 section 25 order 25 image "https raw.githubusercontent.com fonsp Pluto.jl 580ab811f13d565cc81ebfa70ed36c84b125f55d demo plutodemo.gif" title "Time Frequency Analysis" tags "module4", "track julia", "track material", "Pluto", "PlutoUI" layout "layout.jlhtml" using Markdown using InteractiveUtils begin using PlutoUIExtra TableOfContents end using LinearAlgebra using Plots using SignalAnalysis using SegyIO for reading data in sgy format using Polynomials import HTTP let notebook dir DIR toc path joinpath notebook dir, "TOC Notebook.jl" Markdown.parse " ⬅️ Back to Table of Contents . open?path HTTP.escapeuri toc path " end md""" Time Frequency Analysis Tools for non stationary data analysis are useful for analyzing data simultaneously in the time and frequency domains. The Fourier uncertainty principle states that signals cannot be perfectly resolved simultaneously in time and frequency. Therefore, we can control the resolution of time frequency analysis by controlling its locality. """ md""" Using local attributes Consider a set of sinusoidal signals as Fourier basis functions. One approach to time frequency analysis is to apply local attributes to project the analyzed function onto different functions from the basis set. """ md""" Liu, G., Fomel, S. and Chen, X., 2011. Time frequency analysis of seismic data using local attributes. Geophysics, 76 6 , pp.P23 P34. """ function doubint x Vector "causal and anticausal integration in place" n length x for range in 1 n, n 1 1 t zero eltype x for i in range t x i x i t end end end function fold t Vector, nb Integer "reflecting boundary conditions for smoothing" nt length t n nt 2 nb copy middle x t 1 nb n nb reflections from the right side for j in nb n 2 n nt for i in 1 min n,nt j x n 1 i t j i end for i in 1 min n,nt j n x i t j n i end end reflections from the left side for j in nb 1 2 n 1 for i in 1 min n,j 1 x i t j i end for i in 1 min n,j 1 n x n 1 i t j i n end end return x end function smooth x Vector, nb Int "smoothing by triangle filtering with reflecting boundaries" n length x t zeros eltype x ,n 2 nb for i in 1 n xi x i nb nb t i xi t i nb 2 xi t i 2 nb xi end doubint t return fold t, nb end function smooth x Array, nb Vector Int "multidimensional smoothing" y deepcopy x loop over dimensions for dim in 1 length nb y mapslices slice smooth slice, nb dim , y dims dim end return y end function conjgrad forward Function, adjoint Function, shaping Function, ϵ Float64, d Array, p0 Array, niter Int tol 1.0e 6, verb true "Conjugate gradient algorithm for shaping regularization" p deepcopy p0 x shaping p r forward x . d sp, sx, sr similar p , similar x , similar r g0 gnp zero Float64 for iter in 1 niter gx adjoint r ϵ x gp shaping gx ϵ p gx shaping gp gr forward gx gn real gp ⋅ gp if verb show iter, gn end if iter 1 sp, sx, sr gp, gx, gr g0 gn else β gn gnp γ gn g0 if β tol || gn g0 tol if verb println "converged β β γ γ " end break end sp gp β sp sx gx β sx sr gr β sr end gnp gn α real sr ⋅ sr ϵ sp ⋅ sp sx ⋅ sx α gn α p p α sp x x α sx r r α sr end return x end function smooth division numerator Array, denominator Array, radius Vector Int niter 100, verb true n length numerator p0 similar numerator fill p0, zero eltype p0 normalization norm denominator ⋅ denominator if norm 0.0 return p0 end norm sqrt n norm weighting function weight x x . denominator . norm return conjgrad weight, weight, x smooth x, radius , 1.0, numerator norm, p0, niter verb verb end function timefreq signal Vector, time, freq, radius Time frequency analysis using local attributes s similar signal c similar signal nw length freq tf Array eltype signal undef, length time , nw for iw in 1 nw w 2π freq iw if w 0.0 s . 0.0 c . 0.5 else s sin. w time c cos. w time s smooth division signal, s, radius , verb false end c smooth division signal, c, radius , verb false tf ,iw sqrt. s . s c . c end return tf end begin t range start 0, step 0.008, length 501 f range start 0, stop 60, step 0.25 end begin synthetic signal three sinusioids and two spikes syn1 cos. 2π 10 t cos. 2π 20 t cos. 2π 30 t syn1 250 10 syn1 290 10 end plot signal time, signal, title color 1 plot time, signal, title title, label none, color color, xlabel "Time s ", ylabel "Amplitude" plot signal t, syn1, "Synthetic Signal 1" rad1, rad2 15, 30 plot timefreq time, freq, tf, title cmap auto heatmap time, freq, tf', title "Time Frequency Analysis title ", xlabel "Time s ", ylabel "Frequency Hz ", legend none, cmap cmap md""" We can see how decreasing resolution in time increases resolution in frequency. """ dt 0.004 time sampling begin t2 range start 0, step dt, length 501 f2 range start 0, stop 124, step 0.25 end syn2 cos. 2π 2.5 t2 .^ 2 . 8 . t2 cos. 2π 5 t2 .^ 2 . 25 . t2 plot signal t2, syn2, "Synthetic Signal 2" spectrogram spec tfd signal syn2, 1 dt , Spectrogram nfft 125, window hanning plot spec, title "Spectrogram", legend none md""" assignment Task 1 Make multiple plots or a movie showing how the time frequency analysis result changes with the smoothing radius. """ md""" Application to real data We will apply time frequency analysis to the seismic image from Alaska, which was used in a previous assignment. """ begin url "http s3.amazonaws.com open.source.geoscience open data alaska line31 81 31 81 PR.SGY" download url, "alaska.sgy" end begin seismic segy read "alaska.sgy" alaska Float32. seismic.data 1 1001, end nt, nx size alaska begin ta range start 0, length nt, step dt time axis fa range start 0, stop 60, step 1 frequency axis end plot alaska data, title clim 2000, 2000 heatmap 1 nx, ta, data, legend none, yflip true, cmap grays, clim clim, title title, ylabel "Time s " plot alaska alaska, "Seismic Stack from Alaska" function timefreq data Matrix, time, freq, radius nt, n2 size data nf length freq tf Array eltype data undef, nt, n2, nf for i2 in 1 n2 loop over traces tf ,i2, timefreq data ,i2 , time, freq, radius end return tf end tf15 timefreq syn1, t, f, rad1 plot timefreq t, f, tf15, " radius rad1 " tf30 timefreq syn1, t, f, rad2 plot timefreq t, f, tf30, " radius rad2 " tf2 timefreq syn2, t2, f2, 17 plot timefreq t2, f2, tf2, "" tfa timefreq alaska, ta, fa, 40 heatmap fa, ta, tfa ,250, , yflip true, title "Time Frequency Analysis of One Trace", xlabel "Frequency Hz ", ylabel "Time s " md""" We can notice the effect of seismic attenuation losing high frequencies with time. """ plot alaska tfa , ,41 , "40 Hz", clim 0,1000 plot alaska tfa , ,21 , "20 Hz", clim 0,1000 plot alaska tfa , ,11 , "10 Hz", clim 0,1000 function centroid frequency tf, freq nt, n2, nf size tf mf sum tf, dims 3 lf similar mf lf . 0.0 for k in 1 nf lf , tf , ,k freq k end lf lf . mf return lf , ,1 end mfa centroid frequency tfa, fa heatmap 1 nx, ta, mfa, yflip true, colorbar title "Frequency Hz ", title "Local Frequency", ylabel "Time s " md""" Using nonstationary regression Instead of processing one frequency at a time, we can use the nonstationary regression approach to fit a superposition of all basis functions simultaneously. That means changing the problem from \displaystyle \min c \sum \omega \left\| d t c t,\omega \,\psi t,\omega \right\|^2\ , where d t is the input data, \psi t,\omega is the Fourier basis, and c t,\omega are non stationary coefficients, to \displaystyle \min c \left\| d t \sum \omega c t,\omega \,\psi t,\omega \right\|^2\ , with both problems supplemented with the shaping regularization of the estimated c t,\omega . For stationary signals, both approaches are equivalent thanks to the orthogonality of the Fourier basis and yield c t,\omega independent of t . For nonstationary signals, that is not the case. """ md""" Liu, Y. and Fomel, S., 2013. Seismic data analysis using local time‐frequency decomposition. Geophysical Prospecting, 61 3 , pp.516 525. """ function regression f Array, b n size f d zeros eltype b ,n 1 end 1 nk, nx n end , length d for k in 1 nk, x in 1 nx xk x k 1 nx d x f xk b xk end return d end function regression adjoint f Array, d Array n size f nk, nx n end , length d b Array eltype d undef, n for k in 1 nk, x in 1 nx xk x k 1 nx b xk conj f xk d x end return b end function regularized regression data Array, basis Array, radius Array niter 100 p0 zeros eltype data , size basis normalization norm real basis ⋅ basis norm sqrt length basis norm return conjgrad x regression basis norm, x , y regression adjoint basis norm, y , x smooth x, radius , 1.0, data norm, p0, niter end function ltft signal, time, freq, radius Locat time frequency transform nt length time nw length freq sc Array Complex eltype signal undef, nt, nw tf similar sc for iw in 1 nw w 2π freq iw for it in 1 nt t time it if w 0.0 s 0.0 c 0.5 else s sin w t c cos w t end sc it, iw complex c, s end end basis hcat real sc , imag sc tf2 regularized regression signal, basis, radius tf complex. tf2 ,1 nw , tf2 , nw 1 end return tf, sc end tft2, basis2 ltft syn2, t2, f2, 17 plot timefreq t2, f2, abs. tft2 , "Transform" md""" The advantage of the regression approach is that it is invertible. After decomposing the input signal into frequency components, the original signal can be reconstructed by summing the components. """ plot timefreq t2, f2, real. tft2 . conj. basis2 , "Decomposition", cmap grays inverse transform ltft inv tft, basis sum real. tft . conj. basis , dims 2 plot residual time, signal, title, max plot time, real signal , label none, ylim max,max , title title, xlabel "Time s ", ylabel "Amplitude" plot residual t2, syn2 ltft inv tft2, basis2 , "Transform Loss", maximum syn2 md""" Thanks to invertibility, we can separate different frequency components by partitioning the time frequency plane. """ begin nt2, nf2 length t2 , length f2 mask Array Bool undef, nt2, nf2 for kt in 1 nt2, kf in 1 nf2 tk, fk t2 kt , f2 kf if fk 8 tk^ 2 16.5 mask kt, kf true else mask kt, kf false end end end plot timefreq t2, f2, mask, "Mask" plot timefreq t2, f2, mask . real. tft2 . conj. basis2 , "Segmented First Component", cmap grays begin comp1 ltft inv mask . tft2, basis2 comp2 ltft inv . mask . tft2, basis2 end begin pc1 plot signal t2, comp1, "Component 1" color 1 pc2 plot signal t2, comp2, "Component 2" color 2 plot pc1, pc2, layout 2, 1 end md""" assignment Task 2 Apply the local time frequency transform to analyze a seismic trace from a real dataset. Choose appropriate parameters. Observe the loss of high frequencies at late times. """ download "https ahay.org data attr trace.bin", "trace.bin" begin trace Array Float32 undef, 1501 read "trace.bin", trace end tt range start 0, length 1501, step dt plot signal tt, trace, "Real Seismic Trace" md""" Estimating frequency components by nonstationary autoregression Instead of looping through different frequencies, we can estimate the nonstationary frequency components in the data using autoregression. """ md""" Fomel, S., 2013. Seismic data decomposition into spectral components using regularized nonstationary autoregression. Geophysics, 78 6 , pp.O69 O76. """ md""" The process consists of three steps. Step 1 Nonstationary autoregression to estimate adaptive PEF """ tp range start 0.2, step dt, length 601 synp cos. 2π 2.5 tp .^ 2 . 8 . tp cos. 2π 5 tp .^ 2 . 25 . tp function causal shifts1 inp, lag n1 length inp ns length lag T eltype inp shift Array T undef,n1,ns for is in 1 ns s lag is for i1 in 1 n1 k1 i1 s if 1 k1 n1 shift i1,is inp k1 else shift i1,is zero T end end end return shift end shift causal shifts1 synp, 1,2 md""" It helps to make the data complex using the Hilbert transform so that the prediction error filter becomes complex. """ function hilbert x Vector T order 100 where T Real "digital Hilbert transform" n length x nt t, y deepcopy x , similar x h, trace2 c 1 2 c2 c c for it in order 1 1 y 1 t 1 t 3 2 t 2 t 1 c2 y 2 t 2 t 4 3 t 2 2 t 1 c2 for i in 3 n 2 y i t i t i 2 2 t i t i 2 c2 end y n 1 t n 1 t n 3 3 t n 1 2 t n c2 y n t n t n 2 2 t n 1 t n c2 for i in 1 n t i x i y i 2 it 1 2 it end end y 1 2 t 1 t 2 c for i in 2 n 1 y i t i 1 t i 1 c end y n 2 t n 1 t n c return y end analytical signal signal im hilbert signal asyn2 analytical synp ashift mapslices trace analytical trace , shift dims 1 pef regularized regression asyn2, ashift, 5 res asyn2 regression ashift, pef plot residual t2, res 51 end 50 , "Deconvolution", maximum syn2 md""" Step 2 Estimating local frequencies by finding polynomial zeros """ function find roots pef nt, nf size pef rs similar pef polynomial Array eltype pef undef, 1 nf polynomial 1 one eltype pef for it in 1 nt polynomial 2 end pef it, rs it, sort roots Polynomial polynomial , by angle end return rs end rs find roots pef freq angle. rs 2π dt plot t2, freq 51 end 50, , title "Estimated Frequencies", ylim f2 1 , f2 end , label "Component 1" "Component 2" , xlabel "Time s ", ylabel "Frequency Hz " md""" Step 3 Fitting local frequency components to the data """ pfreq cumsum freq 51 end 50, dims 1 dt . t2 . dt phase frequency begin plot t2, pfreq, title "Estimated Phase Frequencies", ylim 0, 50 , label "Component 1" "Component 2" , xlabel "Time s ", ylabel "Frequency Hz " plot t2, hcat 5 t2 .^ 2 . 25, 2.5 t2 .^ 2 . 8 , linestyle dash, color lightblue orange , label "True Component 1" "True Component 2" end Nonstationary Fourier components comps exp. im 2π pfreq . t2 amps regularized regression asyn2 51 end 50 , comps, 20 plot residual t2, asyn2 51 end 50 regression comps, amps , "Regression Residual", maximum syn2 decomp real amps . comps begin pd1 plot signal t2, decomp ,1 , "Component 1" color 1 pd2 plot signal t2, decomp ,2 , "Component 2" color 2 plot pd1, pd2, layout 2, 1 end md""" Applying to the real data example """ ttshift causal shifts1 trace, 1 5 convert to complex begin atrace analytical trace attshift mapslices trace analytical trace , ttshift dims 1 end Step1 regularized nonstationary autoregression ttpef regularized regression atrace, attshift, 30 niter 300 plot residual tt, atrace regression attshift, ttpef , "Real Trace Deconvolution", maximum abs. trace Step2 root finding ttrs find roots ttpef ttfreq angle. ttrs 2π dt plot tt, ttfreq ,2 5 , title "Estimated Frequencies Real Data", label none, xlabel "Time s ", ylabel "Frequency Hz " pttfreq cumsum ttfreq dims 1 dt . tt . dt phase frequency Nonstationary Fourier components ttcomps exp. im 2π pttfreq . tt Step3 regularized regression for amplitudes ttamps regularized regression atrace, ttcomps, 6 plot residual tt, atrace regression ttcomps, ttamps , "Regression Residual Real Data", maximum abs. trace ttdecomp real ttamps . ttcomps begin ptt1 plot signal tt, ttdecomp ,2 , "Component 1" color 1 ptt2 plot signal tt, ttdecomp ,3 , "Component 2" color 2 plot ptt1, ptt2, layout 2, 1 end begin ptt3 plot signal tt, ttdecomp ,4 , "Component 3" color 3 ptt4 plot signal tt, ttdecomp ,5 , "Component 4" color 4 plot ptt3, ptt4, layout 2, 1 end begin mtt Array Array Float64 undef, 4 mff Array Array Float64 undef, 4 for k in 1 4 msk abs. ttamps ,k 1 . 0.33 mtt k tt msk mff k ttfreq ,k 1 msk end end begin plot mtt 1 , mff 1 , title "Estimated Frequencies Real Data", label "Component 1", color 1, xlabel "Time s ", ylabel "Frequency Hz " plot mtt 2 , mff 2 , label "Component 2", color 2 plot mtt 3 , mff 3 , label "Component 3", color 3 plot mtt 4 , mff 4 , label "Component 4", color 4 end md""" assignment Task 3 Apply time frequency analysis using nonstationary autoregression to the Alaska data to extract low and high frequency components. Modify the programs to support 2 D processing, select appropriate parameters, and display the results. """

title: Adaptive Regularization
text: A Pluto.jl notebook v0.20.27 frontmatter chapter 4 section 26 order 26 image "https raw.githubusercontent.com fonsp Pluto.jl 580ab811f13d565cc81ebfa70ed36c84b125f55d demo plutodemo.gif" title "Adaptive Regularization" tags "module4", "track julia", "track material", "Pluto", "PlutoUI" layout "layout.jlhtml" using Markdown using InteractiveUtils using Plots using FastMarching using LinearAlgebra using Random import HTTP let notebook dir DIR toc path joinpath notebook dir, "TOC Notebook.jl" Markdown.parse " ⬅️ Back to Table of Contents . open?path HTTP.escapeuri toc path " end md""" Adaptive regularization We solved nonstationary problems using stationary regularization, such as smoothing with a fixed radius. This chapter will investigate different ways to adapt regularization to the problem. """ md""" Nonstationary smoothing How to implement non stationary smoothing? The analysis of the Taylor expansion around the zero frequency suggests achieving it by interpolating between two triangles as follows T R Z \displaystyle \frac N 1 ^2 R^2 2\,N 1 \,T N Z \frac R^2 N^2 2\,N 1 \,T N 1 Z \ , where N is the largest integer smaller than R so that N \le R \le N 1 . """ function doubint x Vector "causal and anticausal integration in place" n length x for range in 1 n, n 1 1 t zero eltype x for i in range t x i x i t end end end function fold t Vector, nb Integer "reflecting boundary conditions for smoothing" nt length t n nt 2 nb copy middle x t 1 nb n nb reflections from the right side for j in nb n 2 n nt for i in 1 min n,nt j x n 1 i t j i end for i in 1 min n,nt j n x i t j n i end end reflections from the left side for j in nb 1 2 n 1 for i in 1 min n,j 1 x i t j i end for i in 1 min n,j 1 n x n 1 i t j i n end end return x end function fold adj x Vector, nb Integer "reflecting boundary conditions for smoothing" n length x nt n 2 nb t zeros eltype x , nt copy middle t 1 nb n nb x reflections from the right side for j in nb n 2 n nt for i in 1 min n,nt j t j i x n 1 i end for i in 1 min n,nt j n t j n i x i end end reflections from the left side for j in nb 1 2 n 1 for i in 1 min n,j 1 t j i x i end for i in 1 min n,j 1 n t j i n x n 1 i end end return t end function smoothr x Vector, r Vector T where T Real "smoothing by triangle filtering with reflecting boundaries" n length x y similar x nb ceil Int, maximum r 1 t fold adj x, nb doubint t for i in 1 n ri r i nt floor Int, ri nt1 nt 1 wt nt1 nt1 ri ri nt nt nt nt1 wt1 ri ri nt nt nt1 nt1 nt nt1 y i 2 wt wt1 t i nb t i nb nt1 t i nb nt1 wt1 t i nb nt t i nb nt wt end return y end function stems data, label, color plt plot zeros Float32, 13 , label none, color black clip 1.1 maximum abs. data plot plt, data, line stem, marker circle, label label, color color, legend outerleft, xlim 0.5, 13.5 , ylim clip, clip , border none return plt end begin spike zeros 13 spike 7 1 end begin ps Array Plots.Plot undef, 6 rs range start 3, stop 4, length 6 r similar spike for k in 1 6 r . rs k triangle smoothr spike, r ps k stems triangle, "radius rs k ", k end p1 plot ps 1 , ps 2 , ps 3 , layout 3, 1 p2 plot ps 4 , ps 5 , ps 6 , layout 3, 1 plot p1, p2, layout 1, 2 end function smooth2 x Array, r Array "smoothing in 2D" n1, n2 size x y deepcopy x for i2 in 1 n2 y ,i2 smoothr y ,i2 , r ,i2,1 end for i1 in 1 n1 y i1, smoothr y i1, , r i1, ,2 end return y end md""" Using non stationary smoothing in data reconstruction We will return to the rainfall data interpolation example to demonstrate how to achieve fast data gridding with non stationary smoothing. """ begin download data files download "https ahay.org data rain alldata.rsf ","alldata.bin" download "https ahay.org data rain obsdata.rsf ","obsdata.bin" end begin read data alldata Array Float32 undef, 3, 467 obsdata Array Float32 undef, 3, 100 read "alldata.bin", alldata read "obsdata.bin", obsdata end function compute distance source Array, nx Int, ny Int x0 0, y0 0, dx 1, dy 1 unit velocity velocity ones eltype source , nx, ny xy similar source 1 2, xy 1, source 1, . x0 dx . 1 xy 2, source 2, . y0 dy . 1 return FastMarching.msfm velocity, xy, true, true end begin lat 185 185 latitude lon 127 127 longitude nx, ny length lat , length lon x0, y0 lat 1 , lon 1 end dist compute distance obsdata, nx, ny, x0 x0, y0 y0 heatmap lat, lon, dist', title "Distance", cmap coolwarm function bin sources Array, values Array, nx Int, ny Int x0 0, y0 0, dx 1, dy 1 bin initial data to the grid binned zeros eltype sources , nx, ny for n in 1 size sources,2 x, y sources 1,n , sources 2,n i, j round Int, 1 x x0 dx , round Int, 1 y y0 dy if 1 i nx && 1 j ny binned i,j values n end end return binned end binned bin obsdata, obsdata 3, , nx, ny, x0 x0, y0 y0 function nearest neighbor distance sort grid indices by distance grid CartesianIndices distance sort grid, by i distance i neighbor similar grid d1, d2 CartesianIndex 1,0 , CartesianIndex 0,1 for i in 1 length grid g grid i neighbors filter x x in grid, g, g d1, g d1, g d2, g d2 d, j findmin distance neighbors neighbor i neighbors j end return hcat grid, neighbor end grid nearest neighbor dist function interpolate nearest binned, grid nearest copy binned for i, j in eachrow grid nearest i nearest j end return nearest end rain interpolate nearest binned, grid heatmap lat, lon, rain', title "Rainfall Interpolation", cmap viridis md""" Starting with nearest neighbor interpolation, we will perform smoothing only once, using a smoothing radius equal to the distance to the nearest weather station. """ rad dist . 1 the smallest smoothest radius is 1 smoothed smooth2 rain, cat rad, rad dims 3 heatmap lat, lon, smoothed', title "Smoothed Rainfall Interpolation", cmap viridis function lint regul, coord d 1,1 , o 0,0 "bilinear interpolation" n size regul nd size coord, 2 irreg Array eltype regul undef, nd for id in 1 nd find nearest neighbor x1 1 coord 1,id o 1 d 1 x2 1 coord 2,id o 2 d 2 i1, i2 floor Int, x1 , floor Int, x2 a1, a2 x1 i1, x2 i2 b1, b2 1 a1, 1 a2 if 0 i1 && i1 n 1 && 0 i2 && i2 n 2 irreg id regul i1,i2 b1 b2 regul i1 1,i2 a1 b2 regul i1,i2 1 b1 a2 regul i1 1,i2 1 a1 a2 end end return irreg end begin exact alldata 3, predict lint smoothed, alldata o lat 1 , lon 1 end begin lim 10, 600 cc exact ⋅ predict sqrt exact ⋅ exact sqrt predict ⋅ predict scatter exact, predict, xlabel "True", ylabel "Predicted", title "Smoothed Interpolation, cc Float16 cc ", aspect ratio equal, xlim lim, ylim lim, label none plot lim, lim, label none end md""" Estimating triangle radius Suppose we have two datasets \mathbf d 1 and \mathbf d 2 connected via triangle smoothing. How can we determine the smoothing radius? The relationship is \mathbf T \mathbf r \,\mathbf d 1 \approx \mathbf d 2\ , where the dependence of triangle smoothing \mathbf T on radius \mathbf r is nonlinear. The Gauss Newton approach calls for iterative estimation through linearization \left \mathbf T \mathbf r n \mathbf T ' \mathbf r n \, \mathbf r n 1 \mathbf r n \right \,\mathbf d 1 \approx \mathbf d 2\ , """ md""" Writing the frequency domain representation of the triangle filter T R,\omega \displaystyle \frac 1 R^2 \,\left \frac \sin\left \frac R\,\omega\Delta t 2 \right \sin\left \frac \omega\Delta t 2 \right \right ^2\ , we can differentiate it with respect to R to find the derivative representation, as follows \displaystyle \frac \partial T \partial R R,\omega i\omega \left \frac i \Delta t \sin\left \frac R\,\omega \Delta t 2 \right 2R^2\sin^2\left \frac \omega \Delta t 2 \right \right \frac 2 R \,T R,\omega . """ md""" To return to the time domain, we recognize that the i\omega term represents the derivative and that, for integer R N , the expression in the square brackets can be implemented as a digital filter \displaystyle \frac Z^N Z^ N N^2 1 Z 1 Z^ 1 \ . For non integer R , the term is computed via interpolation. """ md""" S. Greer and S. Fomel, 2018, Matching and merging high resolution and legacy seismic images Geophysics, v. 83, V115 V122. R. Alomar and S. Fomel, 2022. Least squares non stationary triangle smoothing Second International Meeting for Applied Geoscience & Energy, 2847 2851. """ function deriv x Vector T order 6 where T Real "derivative filter" n length x nt t, y deepcopy x , similar x h, trace2 for it in order 1 1 for i in 2 n 1 y i t i t i 1 t i 1 2 end y 1 , y n y 2 , y n 1 for i in 1 n t i x i y i it 2 it 1 end end y 1 t 2 t 1 for i in 2 n 1 y i t i 1 t i 1 2 end y n t n t n 1 return y end function smooth der x Vector, r Vector T where T Real "derivative smoothing by triangle filtering" n length x y similar x nb ceil Int, maximum r 1 t fold adj x, nb doubint t for i in 1 n ri r i nt floor Int, ri nt1 nt 1 wt nt1 ri nt nt wt1 ri nt nt1 nt1 y i t i nb nt t i nb nt wt t i nb nt1 t i nb nt1 wt1 end return deriv y 2 smoothr x,r . r end function butterworth lowpass Bool, x Vector T , freq, np Int where T Real "Bandpass filtering using Butterworth filters" nx length x arg 2π freq sinf, cosf sin arg , cos arg fact lowpass ? sin arg 2 cos arg 2 fact fact for j in 1 np loop over poles ss sin π 2 j 1 4 np sinf denominator filter d0, d1, d2 fact 1 ss , 2 cosf fact, 1 ss fact forward and backward convolution for range in 1 nx, nx 1 1 x1 x0 y1 y2 zero T for ix in range x2, x1, x0 x1, x0, x ix y0 lowpass ? x0 2 x1 x2 d1 y1 d2 y2 d0 x0 2 x1 x2 d1 y1 d2 y2 d0 y2, y1, x ix y1, y0, y0 end end end end begin Random.seed 2025 data1 randn 51 butterworth true, data1, 0.2, 6 n 25 25 radius 3 exp. 0.025 n .^2 . 1 radius0 ones 51 data2 smoothr data1, radius end function conjgrad forward Function, adjoint Function, shaping Function, ϵ Float64, d Array, p0 Array, niter Int tol 1.0e 6, verb true "Conjugate gradient algorithm for shaping regularization" p deepcopy p0 x shaping p r forward x . d sp, sx, sr similar p , similar x , similar r g0 gnp zero Float64 for iter in 1 niter gx adjoint r ϵ x gp shaping gx ϵ p gx shaping gp gr forward gx gn real gp ⋅ gp if verb show iter, gn end if iter 1 sp, sx, sr gp, gx, gr g0 gn else β gn gnp γ gn g0 if β tol || gn g0 tol if verb println "converged β β γ γ " end break end sp gp β sp sx gx β sx sr gr β sr end gnp gn α real sr ⋅ sr ϵ sp ⋅ sp sx ⋅ sx α gn α p p α sp x x α sx r r α sr end return x end function smooth x Vector, nb Int "smoothing by triangle filtering with reflecting boundaries" n length x t zeros eltype x ,n 2 nb for i in 1 n xi x i nb nb t i xi t i nb 2 xi t i 2 nb xi end doubint t return fold t, nb end function smooth x Array, nb Vector Int "multidimensional smoothing" y deepcopy x loop over dimensions for dim in 1 length nb y mapslices slice smooth slice, nb dim , y dims dim end return y end function smooth division numerator Array, denominator Array, radius Vector Int niter 100, verb true n length numerator p0 similar numerator fill p0, zero eltype p0 normalization norm denominator ⋅ denominator if norm 0.0 return p0 end norm sqrt n norm weighting function weight x x . denominator . norm return conjgrad weight, weight, x smooth x, radius , 1.0, numerator norm, p0, niter verb verb end function find radius data1, data2, radius0, rad niter 10 radius deepcopy radius0 for iter in 1 niter Gauss Newton iterations res data2 smoothr data1, radius show iter, res ⋅ res der smooth der data1, radius radius radius smooth division res, der, rad verb false radius radius . 1 . 1.0 end return radius end der smooth der data1, radius0 eradius find radius data1, data2, radius0, 5 niter 40 plot radius eradius , label "true" "estimated" , title "Smoothing Radius Estimation" plot data2 smoothr data1, eradius , label "true" "estimated" , title "Data Match" md""" Sparseness regularization using the seislet transform Promoting sparsity is an effective regularization method. To do so adaptively, we need a sparsifying transform for the presumed model pattern. For a nonstationary signal with smoothly varying frequencies, the seislet transform is an appropriate tool. """ function predict forward z Vector T , t T, i, j where T Complex t2 t for i2 in i i j 1 t2 z i2 end return t2 end function predict backward z Vector T , t T, i, j where T Complex t2 t for i2 in i j 1 1 i t2 z i2 end return t2 end function seislet haar x Vector T , z Vector T inv Bool false where T Complex n length x Assume n is a power of 2 if inv j n ÷ 2 while j 1 for i in 1 2j n j x i predict backward z, x i j , i, j 2 x i j predict forward z, x i , i,j end j ÷ 2 end else j 1 while j n ÷ 2 for i in 1 2j n j x i j predict forward z, x i , i, j x i predict backward z, x i j , i, j 2 end j 2 end end end function order coefficients c Vector, n Int "put wavelet coefficients in the right order" nc length c x Array eltype c undef, n ic 1 x 1 c 1 j nc ÷ 2 while j 1 for i in 1 2j nc j ic 1 x ic c i j end if ic n return x end j ÷ 2 end return x end function reorder coefficients x Vector, nc Int "inverse transformation to order coefficients" n length x c Array eltype x undef, nc ic 1 c 1 x 1 j nc ÷ 2 while j 1 for i in 1 2j nc j if ic n ic 1 c i j x ic else c i j zero eltype x end end j ÷ 2 end return c end function seislet transform Function, x Vector T , z Vector T inv Bool false where T Complex y similar x n length x find the nearest power of two nt 1 while nt n nt 2 end if inv t reorder coefficients x, nt else t vcat x, zeros T, nt n pad with zeros end transform t, z inv inv if inv y t 1 n truncate else y order coefficients t, n end return y end begin t collect 1 128 freq 1 . 0.004 t pfreq cumsum freq z exp. im freq chirp exp. im pfreq end plot real chirp , title "Chirp Signal", label none seis seislet seislet haar , chirp, z plot real seis , title "Seislet Transform", linewidth 2, color red, label none, xlabel "Scale" inv seislet seislet haar , seis, z inv true plot real inv , title "Inverse Seislet Transform", label none

title: Measures of uniformity
text:
A Pluto.jl notebook v0.20.27 frontmatter chapter 4 section 4 order 4 image "https raw.githubusercontent.com fonsp Pluto.jl 580ab811f13d565cc81ebfa70ed36c84b125f55d demo plutodemo.gif" title "Measures of uniformity" tags "module4", "track julia", "track material", "Pluto", "PlutoUI" layout "layout.jlhtml" using Markdown using InteractiveUtils begin using PlutoUIExtra TableOfContents end using Plots, LaTeXStrings using Interpolations using PrettyTables using ZipFile using Images using Statistics import HTTP let notebook dir DIR toc path joinpath notebook dir, "TOC Notebook.jl" Markdown.parse " ⬅️ Back to Table of Contents . open?path HTTP.escapeuri toc path " end md""" Measures of uniformity The previous chapter covered averaging attributes. After choosing how to average our data, the next logical question is How much do the data points differ from the average? Our next global attribute assesses the uniformity of the data distribution. Additionally, we will explore how to measure similarity between two different datasets. """ md""" Variance and semblance If we estimate the average of data values a 1, a 2, \ldots, a n as the mean \alpha \mathbf a  rac 1 N \sum\limits n 1 ^ N a n\ , then the natural estimate of variability is the scaled least squares measure """ md"""  eta \mathbf a  rac 1 N \sum\limits n 1 ^ N a n \alpha ^2 \ . As we discussed previously, the minimization of  eta can lead to the mean value \alpha . """ md""" It is easy to see that  egin array rcl  eta \mathbf a & & \displaystyle  rac 1 N \sum\limits n 1 ^ N a n^2 2\,\alpha\, rac 1 N \sum\limits n 1 ^ N a n \alpha^2 \ & &  rac 1 N \sum\limits n 1 ^ N a n^2 \alpha^2\ .\end array """ md""" If the data values are random, statistically independent, and identically distributed with the mathematical expectation a and the variance \sigma , then the mathematical expectation of  eta is """ md"""  egin array rcl E  eta & & \displaystyle  rac 1 N \sum\limits n 1 ^ N E\left a n^2ight E \alpha^2 \ & & \displaystyle \left \sigma^2 a^2ight \left  rac \sigma^2 N a^2ight \ & & \displaystyle \left 1  rac 1 N ight \,\sigma^2\ .\end array In other words,  eta provides a biased estimate of the data variance \sigma^2 , with the bias diminishing with N . """ md""" Instead of subtracting the two terms in the equation for eta , we could take their ratio, known as semblance . S \mathbf a  rac \displaystyle \left \sum\limits n 1 ^ N a night ^2 \displaystyle N\,\sum\limits n 1 ^ N a n^2 \ . """ md""" Semblance is a dimensionless measure that ranges from 0 to 1. It reaches a maximum of 1 when all data values are identical. If the values are random, identically distributed, statistically independent, and have a zero mean, semblance approaches zero as 1 N . """ md""" Jensen inequality The difference between the two terms on the right hand side of the equation for  eta resembles a more general statistical inequality called the Jensen inequality . """ md""" A function C x defined on the interval a \le x \le b is called convex if, for any two points x 1 and x 2 within the interval and for any \lambda between 0 and 1 , \lambda\,C x 1 1 \lambda \,C x 2 \ge C\left \lambda\,x 1 1 \lambda \,x 2ight \ . In other words, the straight line between points \ x 1,C x 2 \ and \ x 1,C x 2 \ always stays above the graph of the function. """ function plot convex C x x^2 1 t range 1.0, 2.01, step 0.01 a 0.5 b 1.75 m a b 2 tp range a, b, step 0.01 L x C a b x C b x a b a plot tp, C. tp , fillrange L. tp , alpha 0.2, color gray plot t, C. t , color red, linewidth 3 plot m, m , 0, L m , color black plot a, a , 0, C a , color black, linestyle dash plot b, b , 0, C b , color black, linestyle dash scatter m , C m , color red scatter a, m, b , L a , L m , L b , color blue plot ylims 0,5 , xlims 1,2 , legend none, yticks xticks a, m, b , L"x 1", L"\lambda\,x 1 1 \lambda \,x 2", L"x 2" , tickfontsize 16, grid none end plot convex md""" The Jensen inequality extends the concept of convex inequality as follows. It states that, for any convex C x , \displaystyle \sum\limits n 1 ^N \lambda n\,C x n \ge C\left \sum\limits n 1 ^N \lambda n\,x night \ , where the weights \lambda n are such that \lambda n \ge 0 for n 1,2,\ldots,N and \displaystyle \sum\limits n 1 ^N \lambda n 1 . """ function plot jensen C x x^2 1 mean x sum x length x t range 2.01, 2.01, step 0.01 p range 1.7, 2.0, step 0.7 m mean p Cm mean C. p L x C p 1 p 6 x C p 6 x p 1 p 6 p 1 plot p, C. p , fillrange L. p , alpha 0.2, color gray plot t,C. t ,color red,linewidth 3 plot m, m , 0, C m , color black for point in p plot point,point , 0,C point , color black, linestyle dash end scatter m , C m ,color red scatter m , Cm ,color blue scatter p,C. p ,color blue annotate m,C m 0.4, text L"C \sum n\,\lambda n x n ", center, 16, color red annotate m,Cm 0.4, text L"\sum n\,\lambda n C x n ", center, 16, color blue plot ylims 0,5 , xlims 2,2 , legend none, yticks xticks p,map k L"x % k ",1 length p ,tickfontsize 16, grid none end plot jensen md""" To formally prove the Jensen inequality, we can employ the method of mathematical induction. The inequality holds immediately for N 1 and N 2 . Assume it is true for N terms and consider N 1 terms. Among all the weights \lambda n , n 1,2,\ldots,N 1 , at least one must be different from zero. Let this be \lambda k . """ md""" Writing \sum\limits n 1 ^ N 1 \lambda n\,x n \lambda k\,x k 1 \lambda k \,\sum\limits n 
e k  rac \lambda n 1 \lambda k \,x n and using the convexity property, we observe that """ md"""  egin array l C\left \sum\limits n 1 ^ N 1 \lambda n\,x night \ C\left \lambda k\,x k 1 \lambda k \,\sum\limits n 
e k  rac \lambda n 1 \lambda k \,x night \ \le \lambda k\,C x k 1 \lambda k \,C\left \sum\limits n 
e k  rac \lambda n 1 \lambda k \,x night \ .\end array """ md""" Note that \sum\limits n 
e k  rac \lambda n 1 \lambda k  rac 1 1 \lambda k \,\left \sum\limits n 1 ^N \lambda n \lambda kight 1\ . """ md""" Therefore, we can use the induction assumption to verify that C\left \sum\limits n 
e k  rac \lambda n 1 \lambda k \,x night \le \sum\limits n 
e k  rac \lambda n 1 \lambda k \,C x n """ md""" and prove by substitution the inequality for N 1 terms C\left \sum\limits n 1 ^ N 1 \lambda n\,x night \le \sum\limits n 1 ^ N 1 \lambda n\,C x n \ . """ md""" The probabilistic version of the Jensen inequality uses mathematical expectations instead of weighted sums E\left C x n ight \ge C\left E x n ight \ . """ md""" The Jensen inequality is an equality when all x n are equal. This insight leads to a variety of different measures of uniformity for different choices of C x . For example, if the convex function C x is a quadratic C x x^2 , and if all weights are equal w n 1 N , the difference between the two sides of the Jensen inequality is exactly the measure  eta from the earlier equation. """ md""" The ratio of the two sides \displaystyle  rac \displaystyle C\left \sum\limits n 1 ^N \lambda n\,x night \displaystyle \sum\limits n 1 ^N \lambda n\,C x n corresponds, in this case, to the semblance measure S \mathbf x . """ md""" Certainly, the class of convex functions is much broader. For instance, we could consider C x |x|^p for p \ge 1 . Another example is C x \ln x , for positive x and equal weights \lambda n 1 N , which leads to  rac 1 N \,\sum\limits n 1 ^ N \ln x n \le \ln\left  rac 1 N \sum\limits n 1 ^ N \,x night """ md""" or, taking the exponential of both sides, \left \prod\limits n 1 ^ N x night ^ 1 N \le  rac 1 N \sum\limits n 1 ^ N \,x n\ . This formula is a well known statement that the geometric mean is less than the arithmetic mean. """ md""" Yet another option, C x x\,\ln x , leads to the following inequality involving entropy \sum\limits n 1 ^N \lambda n\,x n\,\ln x n \ge \left \sum\limits n 1 ^N \lambda n\,x night \,\ln\left \sum\limits n 1 ^N \lambda n\,x night \ . """ md""" References The Jensen inequality and its implications are examined by Claerbout 1992 , who states, "Jensen inequality is my favorite theory that never quite made it into practice, but there is still hope " The inequality is named after the Danish mathematician Johan Jensen https en.wikipedia.org wiki Johan Jensen mathematician , who published it in 1906. Claerbout, J. F., 1992, Earth Soundings Analysis Processing Versus Inversion Blackwell. """ md""" Measures of similarity Another way to understand uniformity is to assess how similar different datasets are. The standard statistical approach for this is the correlation coefficient """ md""" \gamma \mathbf a ,\mathbf b  rac \displaystyle \sum\limits n 1 ^N a n\,b n \displaystyle \sqrt \sum\limits n 1 ^N a n^2 \,\sqrt \sum\limits n 1 ^N b n^2 \ . """ md""" The correlation coefficient equals the cosine of the angle between two multidimensional vectors. Since it is normalized by the vectors' lengths, \gamma is unaffected by the signals' magnitudes and depends only on their similarity. """ md""" In some applications, we do not care about the sign of the correlation coefficient. In other words, if two signals are similar except for opposite polarity, we still consider them similar. In that case, we can square the correlation coefficient and relate it to a specific least squares problem. """ md""" Consider the following optimization problem find a number \xi that minimizes  rac 1 2 \,\sum\limits n 1 ^N a n \xi\,b n ^2\ for two given sequences a n and b n . """ md""" Analogously to the case of the mean, we are dealing here with a quadratic function of \xi  egin array l \displaystyle  rac 1 2 \,\sum\limits n 1 ^N a n \xi\,b n ^2 \ \displaystyle  rac 1 2 \,\sum\limits n 1 ^N a n^2 \xi\,\sum\limits n 1 ^N a n\,b n  rac \xi^2 2 \,\sum\limits n 1 ^N b n^2\ ,\end array which reaches the minimum at """ md""" \xi \mathbf a ,\mathbf b  rac \displaystyle \sum\limits n 1 ^N a n\,b n \displaystyle \sum\limits n 1 ^N b n^2 \ . """ md""" Comparing with the equation for \gamma , we can see that  egin array rcl \gamma^2 \mathbf a ,\mathbf b & & \ & &  rac \displaystyle \sum\limits n 1 ^N a n\,b n \displaystyle \sum\limits n 1 ^N a n^2 \, rac \displaystyle \sum\limits n 1 ^N a n\,b n \displaystyle \sum\limits n 1 ^N b n^2 \ & & \xi \mathbf b ,\mathbf a \,\xi \mathbf a ,\mathbf b \ .\end array """ md""" Moreover, when comparing \gamma^2 and semblance, we observe that semblance is essentially a squared correlation coefficient between the signal and a constant S \mathbf a \gamma^2 \mathbf a ,\mathbf 1 \ , where \mathbf 1 represents the constant sequence 1,1,\ldots,1 . The magnitude of the constant does not matter in this definition because of the amplitude normalization. """ md""" note Semblance measures data uniformity. It is equivalent to a squared correlation with a constant. """ md""" Varimax The varimax measure is defined as \phi \mathbf a  rac \displaystyle N\,\sum\limits i 1 ^N a n^4 \displaystyle \left \sum\limits n 1 ^ N a n^2ight ^2 \ . """ md""" It is used to measure the focusing of signals and is related to the statistical concept of kurtosis . Compared to our previous definition, we see that varimax is equivalent to the inverse of the semblance of the squared signal \phi \mathbf a  rac 1 S \mathbf a ^2  rac 1 \gamma^2 \mathbf a ^2,\mathbf 1 \ , where \mathbf a ^2 represents the sequence a 1^2,a 2^2,\ldots,a N^2 . """ md""" While semblance peaks when the signal is uniform, varimax reaches its maximum when the signal differs significantly from a constant, such as when it is centered around a specific value. Wiggins, R., 1978, Minimum entropy deconvolution Geoexploration, 16, 21 25. """ md""" Skewness The skewness attribute measures the asymmetry of the data distribution. For zero mean data, it is defined as \kappa \mathbf a  rac \displaystyle \sum\limits n 1 ^N a n^3 \displaystyle \left  rac 1 N \,\sum\limits n 1 ^ N a n^2ight ^ 3 2 \ . """ md""" Using simple algebraic manipulations, we can show that \kappa^2 \mathbf a  rac \displaystyle \left \sum\limits n 1 ^N a n^3ight ^2 \displaystyle \left  rac 1 N \,\sum\limits n 1 ^ N a n^2ight ^3  rac \displaystyle \left \sum\limits n 1 ^N a n^2\cdot a night ^2 \displaystyle \sum\limits n 1 ^N a n^4\,\sum\limits n 1 ^N a n^2 \, rac \displaystyle \sum\limits n 1 ^N a n^4\,\sum\limits n 1 ^N 1^2 \displaystyle \left \sum\limits n 1 ^N a n^2ight ^2 \,N^2 N^2\, rac \gamma^2 \mathbf a ^2,\mathbf a \gamma^2 \mathbf a ^2,\mathbf 1 \ . Thus, skewness is the ratio of the similarity between the given signal and the squared signal to the similarity between the squared signal and a constant. """ md""" AB semblance and weighted semblance If we expect the signal to show a trend, we can calculate a correlation with the trend instead of with a constant. This method is known as the AB semblance and can be defined as S AB \mathbf a ,\mathbf f \displaystyle  rac \left \sum\limits n 1 ^N a n\, 1 b\,f n ight ^2 \sum\limits n 1 ^N 1 b\,f n ^2\,\sum\limits n 1 ^N a n^2 \ , where 1 b\,f n represents the trend in the data with some unknown coefficient b . The appropriate value of b is found by maximizing the AB semblance, which leads to the following definition b \displaystyle rac N\,\sum\limits n 1 ^N a n\,f n \sum\limits n 1 ^N a n\, \sum\limits n 1 ^N f n \sum\limits n 1 ^N a n\, \sum\limits n 1 ^N f n^2 \sum\limits n 1 ^N a n\,f n\, \sum\limits n 1 ^N f n \ . Substituting specifies AB semblance as  egin array rcl 
onumber S AB \mathbf a ,\mathbf f & & \displaystyle  rac N\,\left \sum\limits n 1 ^N a n\,f night ^2 \left \sum\limits n 1 ^N a night ^2\,\sum\limits n 1 ^N f n^2 2\,\sum\limits n 1 ^N a n\, \sum\limits n 1 ^N f n\,\sum\limits n 1 ^N a n\,f n \left N\,\sum\limits n 1 ^N f n^2 \left \sum\limits n 1 ^N f night ^2ight \,\sum\limits n 1 ^N a n^2 \ & & \displaystyle S \mathbf a \displaystyle  rac \left N\,\sum\limits n 1 ^N a n\,f n \sum\limits n 1 ^N a n\,\sum\limits n 1 ^N f night ^2 N\,\left N\,\sum\limits n 1 ^N f n^2 \left \sum\limits n 1 ^N f night ^2ight \,\sum\limits n 1 ^N a n^2 \ .\end array """ md""" The weighted semblance takes a different approach to treating trends in the data. It is defined as S W \mathbf a ,\mathbf f \displaystyle  rac \left \sum\limits n 1 ^N a n\, 1 b\,f n ight ^2 \sum\limits n 1 ^N 1 b\,f n \,\sum\limits n 1 ^N 1 b\,f n \,a n^2 and can be interpreted as the squared correlation coefficient between a n\,\sqrt 1 b\,f n and \sqrt 1 b\,f n . """ md""" Note that if the sequence a n is constant, both AB semblance and weighted semblance evaluate to one. However, they behave differently with respect to the b parameter. In the weighted semblance approach, b is determined by minimizing S W rather than maximizing it. Therefore, this measure penalizes deviations from a constant into a trend more strongly than the conventional semblance. The corresponding value of b is given by b \displaystyle  rac \sum\limits n 1 ^N a n\,\sum\limits n 1 ^N a n^2\,\sum\limits n 1 ^N f n 2\,N\,\sum\limits n 1 ^N a n^2\,\sum\limits n 1 ^N a n\,f n N\,\sum\limits n 1 ^N a n\,\sum\limits n 1 ^N a n^2\,f n \sum\limits n 1 ^N a n\,f n\,\sum\limits n 1 ^N a n^2\,\sum\limits n 1 ^N f n 2\,\sum\limits n 1 ^N a n\,\sum\limits n 1 ^N a n^2\,f n\,\sum\limits n 1 ^N f n N\,\sum\limits n 1 ^N a n\,f n\,\sum\limits n 1 ^N a n^2\,f n \ , which specifies weighted semblance as S W \mathbf a ,\mathbf f \displaystyle  rac 4\,\left N\,\sum\limits n 1 ^N a n\,f n \sum\limits n 1 ^N a n\,\sum\limits n 1 ^N f night \,\left \sum\limits n 1 ^N a n\,\sum\limits n 1 ^N a n^2\,f n \sum\limits n 1 ^N a n^2\,\sum\limits n 1 ^N a n\,f night \left N\,\sum\limits n 1 ^N a n^2\,f n \sum\limits n 1 ^N a n^2\,\sum\limits n 1 ^N f night ^2 \displaystyle S \mathbf a  rac \left \sum\limits n 1 ^N a n\,\sum\limits n 1 ^N a n^2\,\sum\limits n 1 ^N f n 2\,N\,\sum\limits n 1 ^N a n^2\,\sum\limits n 1 ^N a n\,f n N\,\sum\limits n 1 ^N a n\,\sum\limits n 1 ^N a n^2\,f night ^2 N\,\left N\,\sum\limits n 1 ^N a n^2\,f n \sum\limits n 1 ^N a n^2\,\sum\limits n 1 ^N f night ^2\,\sum\limits n 1 ^N a n^2 \ . """ md""" Fomel, S., 2009, Velocity analysis using AB semblance Geophysical Prospecting, 57, 311 321. Luo, S., and D. Hale, 2012, Velocity analysis using weighted semblance Geophys 77, U15 U22. Sarkar, D., R. T. Baumel, and K. L. Larner, 2002, Velocity analysis in the p esence of amplitude variation Geophysics, 67, 1664 1672. Sarkar, D., J. P. Castagna, and W. Lamb, 2001, AVO and velocity analysis Geophysics, 66, 1284 1293. """ md""" Semblance in seismic data analysis Semblance and other measures of uniformity have immediate uses in seismic data analysis. The traditional velocity analysis method involves testing various moveout parameters seismic velocities , applying a moveout correction to make a seismic gather appear flat, and then measuring semblance along the offset direction. """ download a common midpoint gather download "https ahay.org data midpts cmp.rsf ","cmp.bin" md""" We will examine the idea of the semblance scan using a common midpoint gather from an old Gulf of Mexico dataset. Claerbout, J. F., 2000, Basic Earth Imaging https reproducibility.org RSF book bei Stanford Exploration Project. """ gather Array Float32 undef, 1000, 24 single precision array read data read "cmp.bin", gather begin t range 0, step 0.004, length 1000 time axis x range 0.264, step 0.134, length 24 offset axis end plot seismic gather, title heatmap x, t, gather, yflip true, c grays, legend none, title title, clim 5e 06, 5e 06 , xlabel "Offset km ", ylabel "Time s " plot seismic gather, "CMP Gather" md""" In reflection seismology, a common midpoint CMP gather consists of traces for the same midpoint between the source and receiver at different offsets the surface distances between source and receiver . """ md""" The arrival time of reflection events increases with offset, and the following equation approximates the shape of the reflection events 	au \displaystyle \sqrt t^2  rac x^2 v^2 t \ , where 	au is recorded time, t is vertical time, x is offset, and v t is the moveout parameter with the dimensions of velocity. """ md""" Normal moveout NMO processing aims to convert traces from 	au to t to align them across different offsets. """ function nmo cmp Matrix, offset StepRangeLen, time StepRangeLen, velocity Array "Normal moveout" nmo similar cmp blank zero eltype cmp tmax time end inbounds for ix,x in enumerate offset int cubic spline interpolation time, cmp ,ix inbounds for it, t in enumerate time τ sqrt t^2 x^2 velocity it ^2 moveout nmo it,ix τ tmax ? blank int τ end end return nmo end NMO with constant velocities function normal moveout gather Array, vs Vector Float64 , nt Int nmos gather for v in vs velocity fill v, nt constant array push nmos, nmo gather, x, t, velocity end return nmos end try two constant velocities vs 1.5, 2.5 try two constant velocities nmos normal moveout gather, vs, 1000 begin ts "CMP Gather" "NMO vs 1 km s" "NMO vs 2 km s" plts map k plot seismic nmos k , ts k , 1 3 plot plts 1 , plts 2 , plts 3 , layout 1,3 end md""" Trying different constant velocities, we observe that events align differently at various times. To estimate variable v 	au , we will test multiple constant velocities and calculate semblance across offsets after NMO as a measure of flatness. """ function velocity scan cmp Matrix, offset StepRangeLen, time StepRangeLen, vels StepRangeLen nt, nx size cmp nv length vels stack zeros Float32,nt,nv stack2 zeros Float32,nt,nv tmax time end inbounds for ix, x in enumerate offset int cubic spline interpolation time, cmp ,ix inbounds for iv, v in enumerate vels inbounds for it, t in enumerate time τ sqrt t^2 x^2 v^2 if τ tmax break end a int τ stack it,iv a stack2 it,iv a a end end end return stack . stack . stack2 . eps Float32 nx end vels range 1.4, 2.5, 111 scan velocity scan gather, x, t, vels heatmap vels, t, scan, yflip true, title "Semblance Scan", xlabel "Velocity km s ", ylabel "Time s " begin picks 0.0 1.5 0.5 1.5 0.8 1.7 1.0 1.8 1.3 1.9 1.8 2.0 2.0 2.1 2.2 2.1 2.6 2.2 2.9 2.2 3.6 2.3 4 2.4 pretty table HTML, picks column labels "Time s ", "Velocity km s " , alignment c end begin plt scan heatmap vels, t, scan, yflip true, legend none, xlabel "Velocity km s ", ylabel "Time s ", title "Semblance Scan" scatter plt scan, picks ,2 , picks ,1 , yflip true, color white, legend none, ylim 0,t 1000 , xlim vels 1 , vels 111 end linearly interpolate manual picks int linear interpolation picks ,1 , picks ,2 apply normal moveout with variable velocity nmod nmo gather, x, t, int t begin plt nmod plot seismic nmod, "Normal Moveout" plot plts 1 , plt scan, plt nmod, layout 1,3 end md""" The use of semblance in seismic velocity analysis was pioneered by Taner and Koehler 1969 . Yilmaz, 2000 is the classic reference on reflection seismic data analysis, including semblance scans https wiki.seg.org wiki Velocity analysis . Taner, M. T., and F. Koehler, 1969, Velocity spectra Digital computer derivation and applications of velocity functions Geophysics, 34, 85 88. Yilmaz, O., 2000, Seismic data analysis https wiki.seg.org wiki Seismic Data Analysis SEG. """ md""" assignment Task 1 We can use the average mean horizontal semblance to assess the flatness of a seismic gather before and after the normal moveout correction. """ function semblance gather Matrix "average horizontal semblance" stack over second dimension stack sum gather, dims 2 stack2 sum gather . gather, dims 2 semb2 stack . stack . stack2 . eps eltype gather return sum semb2 length gather end begin semblances "Input Gather" semblance nmos 1 "NMO vs 1 km s" semblance nmos 2 "NMO vs 2 km s" semblance nmos 3 "NMO Variable Velocity" semblance nmod pretty table HTML, semblances column labels "Case", "Flatness" , alignment l end md""" With this measure, the flatness of the NMO corrected gather after a variable velocity pick appears much greater. Your task is to increase this number. To do this, you can modify manual picks in the `picks` array and rerun the commands for variable velocity normal moveout. Feel free to try out other improvements. """ md""" http www.spacefacts.de mission patches sts 99 2.png Histogram equalization In this example, we will use a digital elevation model from the Shuttle Radar Topography Mission SRTM . """ md""" SRTM was an international project led by the National Imagery and Mapping Agency now the National Geospatial Intelligence Agency , a part of the U.S. Department of Defense, and NASA, with cooperation from the German Aerospace Center DLR. Its purpose was to develop the most comprehensive high resolution digital topographic database of Earth's surface. SRTM used a specially modified radar system mounted on Endeavour's 11 day mission https www.nasa.gov mission pages shuttle shuttlemissions archives sts 99.html in February 2000. """ md""" https upload.wikimedia.org wikipedia commons 0 0b Payload bay sts 99.jpg https en.wikipedia.org wiki STS 99 https en.wikipedia.org wiki STS 99 """ md""" "SRTM used of a technique called radar interferometry . In radar interferometry, two radar images are taken from slightly different locations. Differences between these images allow for the calculation of surface elevation, or change. To get two radar images taken from different locations the SRTM hardware consisted of one radar antenna in the shuttle payload bay and a second radar antenna attached to the end of a mast that extended 60 meters 200 feet from the shuttle." https eospso.nasa.gov missions shuttle radar topography mission https eospso.nasa.gov missions shuttle radar topography mission """ begin url "https terrain.ardupilot.org SRTM3 North America N30W098.hgt.zip" data HTTP.get url end md""" The SRTM digital elevation models are divided into tiles, each covering one degree of latitude and one degree of longitude, and named after their southwestern corners. The original binary format is `hgt` short for "height" , a 2 byte integer in big endian encoding. We will select one tile that represents the elevation around Austin. """ begin elevation Array Int16 undef, 1201, 1201 zip ZipFile.Reader IOBuffer data.body read zip.files 1 , elevation convert from big endian to little endian by byte swap elevation bswap. elevation fix orientation elevation reverse transpose elevation end begin lon range 97,98,1201 lat range 30,31,1201 end plot elevation map, bartitle heatmap lon, lat, map, color grays, xflip true, colorbar title bartitle, title "Digital Elevation Map of the Austin Area", xlabel "Longitude W ", ylabel "Latitude N " plot elevation elevation, "Elevation m " md""" Each image has a specific distribution of values a histogram . When different values in a histogram are not evenly spread out, the image may have low contrast. One way to enhance contrast is to use histogram equalization . """ md""" To analyze the image's histogram for different shades of gray, let's first convert the image to an 8 bit format. """ begin emax maximum elevation emin minimum elevation grays round. UInt8, 255 elevation. emin emax emin end plot elevation grays, "Shades of Gray" function histogram image hist zeros Int, 256 inbounds for gray in image hist gray 1 1 end return hist end hist histogram grays bar hist 1201^2 , xlim 1, 256 , legend none, xlabel "Shades of Gray", ylabel "Relative Occurence", title "Image Histogram" md""" Let f x,y be the original image. The equalized image will be F x,y . Let h f be the histogram probability distribution of the original image values. Let H F be the histogram of the modified image. The mapping of probabilities indicates H F \,dF h f \,df """ md""" or if we want the modified histogram to be uniform,  rac d F d f C\,h f \ , where C is a constant. """ md""" Solving the equation, we obtain the following mapping F f f 0 C\,\int\limits f 0 ^f h \phi \,d\phi\ , where f 0 is the minimum value of f . """ cumulative sum cumhist cumsum hist bar cumhist 1201^2 , xlim 1, 256 , legend none, xlabel "Shades of Gray", title "Cumulative Histogram" md""" The histogram equalization algorithm involves the following steps 1. From the input image f x,y , calculate its histogram h f . 2. Calculate the cumulative histogram F f . Select an appropriate normalization factor C to ensure that the range of F matches that of f . 3. Map every pixel f x,y to F x,y . """ function equalize image n length image hist histogram image image2 similar image compute cumulative histogram accum 0 for shade in 1 256 accum hist shade hist shade round accum 255 n end remap image inbounds for i 1 n shade image i image2 i UInt8 hist shade 1 end return image2 end grays2 equalize grays plot elevation grays2, "Shades of Gray" hist2 histogram grays2 bar hist2 1201^2 , xlim 1, 256 , legend none, xlabel "Shades of Gray", ylabel "Relative Occurence", title "Equalized Image Histogram", color green cumulative sum cumhist2 cumsum hist2 bar cumhist2 1201^2 , xlim 1, 256 , legend none, xlabel "Shades of Gray", title "Equalized Cumulative Histogram", color green md""" assignment Task 2 The SRTM data are publicly available in higher resolution 30 meters instead of 90 meters from NASA servers but require registration https urs.earthdata.nasa.gov . We will extract information from an image instead of downloading a higher resolution digital model. """ begin image url "https raw.githubusercontent.com sfomel ipython refs heads master N30W098.jpg" image HTTP.get image url .body | IOBuffer | load end convert image to one byte representation grays1 UInt8. rawview channelview image md""" Your task apply histogram equalization to the high resolution image. """ convert one byte representation back to image image1 colorview Gray, normedview grays1 save image as JPEG file save "elevation.jpg", image1 md""" assignment Task 3 Select a different title from the SRTM dataset containing your hometown or an interesting topographical landmark and process it using histogram equalization. This site offers a user friendly interface for navigating tiles https dwtkns.com srtm30m https dwtkns.com srtm30m """ md""" Time power correction Raw seismic reflection data are recorded as shot gathers S t,x , where t indicates time and x indicates the offset horizontal distance from the receiver to the source . Raw data can be challenging to analyze due to the rapid amplitude decay of seismic waves. We can compensate for this decay by multiplying the data by a gain function. """ md""" A commonly used function is the power of time. The gain compensated gather is S p t,x t^p\,S t,x The advantage of the time power gain is its simplicity and the ability to reverse it by multiplying the data by t^ p . What value of p should we use? We will try to develop a method for finding the optimal value of p for a given dataset. """ download a data file download "https ahay.org data wz data.rsf ","data.bin" begin a single precision floating point matrix shot Matrix Float32 undef, 2000, 81 read data read "data.bin", shot normalize shot maximum shot end time power data, time, p data . time .^ p begin time range 0, 4, 2000 offset range 2, 2, 81 end function plot seismic2 data, title clip quantile abs. data , 0.99 return heatmap offset, time, data, clim clip, clip , yflip true, c grays, legend none, xlabel "Offset km ", ylabel "Time s ", title title end p 2 tpow time power shot, time, p begin plot shot plot seismic2 shot, "Original Data" plot tpow plot seismic2 tpow, "Time Power Gain p p " plot plot shot, plot tpow, layout 1, 2 end md""" assignment Task 4 Change the code below to compute the varimax of the shot gather after the time power correction as a function of p . """ function varimax p tpow time power shot, time, p tpow2 tpow . tpow Change below return sum tpow2 end md""" Plot the varimax function for p ranging from 1.5 to 3.5. """ map varimax, range 1.5, 3.5, 10 md""" assignment Task 5 Pick the value of p corresponding to the minimum varimax, apply it for the time power gain, and plot the corrected gather to visually verify the result. """ md""" assignment Bonus Task Implement an algorithm to find the optimal value of p without scanning too many values. You might find the Optim https github.com JuliaNLSolvers Optim.jl package helpful. """

title: Convolution
text: A Pluto.jl notebook v0.20.27 frontmatter chapter 4 section 5 order 5 image "https raw.githubusercontent.com fonsp Pluto.jl 580ab811f13d565cc81ebfa70ed36c84b125f55d demo plutodemo.gif" title "Convolution" tags "module4", "track julia", "track material", "Pluto", "PlutoUI" layout "layout.jlhtml" using Markdown using InteractiveUtils This Pluto notebook uses bind for interactivity. When running this notebook outside of Pluto, the following 'mock version' of bind gives bound variables a default value instead of an error . macro bind def, element format off return quote local iv try Base.loaded modules Base.PkgId Base.UUID "6e696c72 6542 2067 7265 42206c756150" , "AbstractPlutoDingetjes" .Bonds.initial value catch b missing end local el esc element global esc def Core.applicable Base.get, el ? Base.get el iv el el end format on end using Plots using Polynomials using LaTeXStrings using PlutoUI begin using PlutoUIExtra TableOfContents end import HTTP let notebook dir DIR toc path joinpath notebook dir, "TOC Notebook.jl" Markdown.parse " ⬅️ Back to Table of Contents . open?path HTTP.escapeuri toc path " end md""" Convolution We previously defined global data attributes, such as a single average value. In practice, it makes more sense to generate local attributes that vary by location. If the computation of local quantities is linear and stationary, it becomes a convolution . """ md""" From global to local As discussed previously, computation of the mean value is a linear operation equivalent to multiplication by the row matrix \left \begin array cccc \displaystyle \frac 1 N & \displaystyle \frac 1 N & \cdots & \displaystyle \frac 1 N \end array \right \ . """ md""" In practice, it may make more sense to compute a running local mean with a matrix that may look like \left \begin array cccccc 1 3 & 1 3 & 1 3 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 3 & 1 3 & 1 3 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 3 & 1 3 & 1 3 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 3 & 1 3 & 1 3 \\ \end array \right """ md""" or, more generally, a local operator that may look like \left \begin array c x 0 \\ x 1 \\ x 2 \\ x 3 \end array \right \left \begin array cccccc b 0 & b 1 & b 2 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & b 0 & b 1 & b 2 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & b 0 & b 1 & b 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & b 0 & b 1 & b 2 \end array \right \, \left \begin array c y 0 \\ y 1 \\ y 2 \\ y 3 \\ y 4 \\ y 5 \end array \right """ md""" Even more generally and using a shorter notation, x k \sum\limits n 0 ^ N 1 b n\,y k n \ , where b 0, b 1, \ldots, b N correspond to the non zero diagonals of the matrix. Operation in the last equation is known as correlation . """ md""" Its adjoint matrix transpose , expressed as \left \begin array c y 0 \\ y 1 \\ y 2 \\ y 3 \\ y 4 \\ y 5 \end array \right \left \begin array cccc b 0 & 0 & 0 & 0\\ b 1 & b 0 & 0 & 0 \\ b 2 & b 1 & b 0 & 0 \\ 0 & b 2 & b 1 & b 0 \\ 0 & 0 & b 2 & b 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & b 2 \end array \right \, \left \begin array c x 0 \\ x 1 \\ x 2 \\ x 3 \end array \right """ md""" or y k \sum\limits n 0 ^ N 1 b n\,x k n \ , is known as convolution . Convolution and correlation play a vital role in digital data analysis. """ md""" Convolution is a class of operators that are linear and stationary acting similarly on different parts of the data . If the input to convolution is an impulse a vector with all zeros except one , the output will contain the values b 0, b 1, \ldots, b N 1 , which repeat with a shift in every column. These values represent the coefficients of the convolution filter . """ function convolution x Array, b Array nx, nb length x , length b y zeros eltype x , nx nb 1 for ib in 1 nb, ix in 1 nx y ix ib 1 x ix b ib end return y end function correlation y Array, b Array ny, nb length y , length b nx ny 1 nb x zeros eltype y , nx for ib in 1 nb, ix in 1 nx x ix y ix ib 1 b ib end return x end md""" Note that we can write the convolution equation equivalently as \left \begin array c y 0 \\ y 1 \\ y 2 \\ y 3 \\ y 4 \\ y 5 \end array \right \left \begin array ccc x 0 & 0 & 0 \\ x 1 & x 0 & 0 \\ x 2 & x 1 & x 0 \\ x 3 & x 2 & x 1 \\ 0 & x 3 & x 2 \\ 0 & 0 & x 3 \end array \right \, \left \begin array c b 0 \\ b 1 \\ b 2 \end array \right \ , """ md""" in which case it will have a different adjoint, namely \left \begin array c b 0 \\ b 1 \\ b 2 \end array \right \left \begin array cccccc x 0 & x 1 & x 2 & x 3 & x 4 & 0 \\ 0 & x 0 & x 1 & x 2 & x 3 & x 4 \\ 0 & 0 & x 0 & x 1 & x 2 & x 3 \end array \right \, \left \begin array c y 0 \\ y 1 \\ y 2 \\ y 3 \\ y 4 \\ y 5 \end array \right \ . """ md""" There is no fundamental difference between the input \mathbf x and the filter \mathbf b , but we typically assume the filter is short and the input is arbitrarily long. """ md""" In many applications, it is desirable for the input and output to have the same size. We can truncate the convolution matrix to make it square to achieve that. One possible truncation is \left \begin array c y 0 \\ y 1 \\ y 2 \\ y 3 \end array \right \left \begin array cccc b 1 & b 0 & 0 & 0 \\ b 2 & b 1 & b 0 & 0 \\ 0 & b 2 & b 1 & b 0 \\ 0 & 0 & b 2 & b 1 \end array \right \, \left \begin array c x 0 \\ x 1 \\ x 2 \\ x 3 \end array \right """ function convolve x Array, b Array, adjoint false nx, nb length x , length b y zeros eltype x , nx for ib in 1 nb, iy in ib nx if adjoint correlation y iy 1 ib x iy b ib else convolution y iy x iy 1 ib b ib end end return y end begin input zeros Float32,30 for k,a in Dict 6 1,15 0.5,16 1,17 0.5,25 1 input k a end end filt map x 1 x 4, 0 3 conv convolve input, filt, false corr convolve input, filt, true function stems data, label, color plt plot zeros Float32, 30 , label none, color black plot plt, data, line stem, marker circle, label label, color color, legend outerleft, xlim 0, 30.5 , ylim 1.7, 1.7 , border none return plt end plot stems input, "input", blue , stems filt, "filter", green , stems conv, "convolution", red , stems corr, "correlation", red , layout 4, 1 md""" The cost of convolution is proportional to the product of the input's size and the filter's size. As long as the filter size is fixed and small, convolution is an efficient O N operation. """ md""" Z transform notation A convenient way to express convolution and correlation operators is the Z transform notation. If we represent both the input and the filter as polynomials, X Z x 0 x 1\,Z x 2\,Z^2 \cdots and B Z b 0 b 1\,Z b 2\,Z^2 \cdots , the output of convolution corresponds to the polynomial product """ md""" \begin array rcl Y Z & & X Z \,B Z \\ & & x 0\,b 0 \, x 0\,b 1 x 1\,b 0 \,Z \\ & & \, x 0\,b 2 x 1\,b 1 x 2\,b 0 \,Z^2 \cdots \\ & & y 0 y 1\,Z y 2\,Z^2 \cdots\end array """ md""" The variable Z represents a shift operator that moves the data one sample forward. The reverse movement is division by Z or multiplication by 1 Z . Thus, a correlation with filter B Z can be expressed as multiplication by B 1 Z . """ md""" Recursive filtering In addition to polynomial multiplication, we can consider polynomial division, the exact inverse of convolution. Consider, for example, a two point filter B Z 1 Z 2 . The inverse of multiplication by B Z is division by B Z , or multiplication by an infinitely long filter """ md""" \frac 1 1 Z 2 1 \frac Z 2 \frac Z^2 4 \frac Z^3 8 \cdots In digital signal processing, filters like that are known as IIR Infinite Impulse Response rather than FIR Finite Impulse Response filters, which use polynomial multiplication. """ md""" To implement polynomial division, let us assume b 0 1 and consider the sequence of convolution operations. \begin array rcl y 0 & & x 0\ , \\ y 1 & & x 1 b 1\,x 0\ , \\ y 2 & & x 2 b 1\,x 1 b 2\,x 0\ , \\ \cdots & & \cdots \\ y k & & x k \sum n 1 ^ N 1 b n\,x k n \ .\end array """ md""" We can reverse this sequence as follows \begin array rcl x 0 & & y 0\ , \\ x 1 & & y 1 b 1\,x 0\ , \\ x 2 & & y 2 b 1\,x 1 b 2\,x 0\ , \\ \cdots & & \cdots \\ x k & & y k \sum n 1 ^ N 1 b n\,x k n \ .\end array """ md""" In this manner, the output is computed recursively using both the input and the previous output. """ function recursive x Array, a Array, adjoint false nx, na length x , length a y similar x if adjoint inbounds for ix in nx 1 1 t x ix assume a 1 1 for ia in 2 min na, nx ix 1 t a ia y ix ia 1 end y ix t end else inbounds for ix in 1 nx t x ix assume a 1 1 for ia in 2 min na, ix t a ia y ix ia 1 end y ix t end end return y end md""" To understand why the adjoint's recursion goes backward, note that polynomial division corresponds to inverting a lower triangular matrix that may look like \left \begin array cccc 1 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ b 1 & 1 & 0 & 0 \\ b 2 & b 1 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & b 2 & b 1 & 1 \\ \end array \right \ . """ md""" The adjoint inverts an upper triangular matrix \left \begin array cccc 1 & b 1 & b 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & b 1 & b 2 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & b 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end array \right and correspond to recursion running backward from the last row to the first row. """ deconv recursive conv, filt, false decorr recursive corr, filt, true plot stems conv, "convolution", red , stems deconv, "deconvolution", blue , stems corr, "correlation", red , stems decorr, "decorrelation", blue , layout 4,1 begin a single spike spike zeros Float32,30 spike 1 1 impulse responses fir convolve spike, filt, false iir recursive spike, filt filt 1 , false print out println "FIR ",fir println "IIR ",iir end plot stems fir,"FIR", blue , stems iir,"IIR", green , layout 2,1 md""" Minimum phase filters For B Z 1 Z 2 , the polynomial obtained from the division 1 B Z is infinite yet convergent. Its coefficients decrease rapidly, which guarantees a stable recursion. """ md""" On the other hand, the polynomial series like \frac 1 1 2\,Z 1 2\,Z 4\,Z^2 8\,Z^3 \cdots is divergent and would produce an unstable recursion. """ md""" Note, however, that if we wanted to divide by B Z 1 2\,Z , we could do it by multiplying the numerator and denominator by B 1 Z , \frac 1 B Z \frac B 1 Z B 1 Z \,B Z \frac 2 Z 1 2 Z 5 2\,Z and then decomposing the denominator differently to obtain two stable divisions """ md""" \begin array rcl \displaystyle \frac 1 B Z & & \displaystyle \frac 2 Z 1 4\,\left 1 Z 2\right \,\left 1 1 2Z \right \\ & & \displaystyle \frac 2 Z 1 4 \\ & & \displaystyle \left 1 \frac Z 2 \frac Z^2 4 \frac Z^3 8 \cdots\right \\ & & \displaystyle \left 1 \frac 1 2\,Z \frac 1 4\,Z^2 \frac 1 8\,Z^3 \cdots\right \ .\end array """ md""" What makes a filter stable for division? A two point filter 1 Z Z 0 is stable if |Z 0| \ge 1 . By the fundamental theorem of algebra, any N point filter B Z b 0 b 1\,Z \cdots b N 1 \,Z^ N 1 can be decomposed into N 1 elementary filters. B Z b 0\,\left 1 \frac Z Z 1 \right \,\left 1 \frac Z Z 2 \right \,\cdots\,\left 1 \frac Z Z N 1 \right \ , where the roots Z 1,Z 2,\ldots,Z N 1 are real or complex. If every root Z n of B Z satisfies |Z n| \ge 1 or, in other words, lies outside of the unit circle in the complex plane, the inverse of B Z will be stable. """ md""" When we form the correlation filter B 1 Z , its roots are the reciprocals of the roots of B Z . For every root Z n in B Z , there is a corresponding root 1 Z n in B 1 Z . In the autocorrelation A Z B Z \,B 1 Z , all roots come in pairs Z n and 1 Z n , one inside and one outside the unit circle or both exactly on the circle . If, from each pair, we select the root outside the unit circle, we can form a decomposition A Z S Z \,S 1 Z with stable S Z and then perform a stable division \displaystyle \frac 1 B Z \frac B 1 Z B 1 Z \,B Z \frac B 1 Z S Z \,S 1 Z \ . """ F Polynomial filt, Z R roots F function plot roots R, var plt scatter real R ,imag R , color red, label "filter roots" plot plt, cos, sin, 0, 2π, label "unit circle", linewidth 3, color black, aspect ratio equal, xlim 2,2 , ylim 2,2 , title L"Roots of % var Z ", xlabel "real", ylabel "imaginary" plt2 scatter real R ,imag R , color red, label none scatter plt2, real 1 . R ,imag 1 . R , color orange, label none plot plt2, cos, sin, 0, 2π, label none, linewidth 3, color black, aspect ratio equal, xlim 2,2 , ylim 2,2 , title L"Roots of % var 1 Z \,% var Z ", xlabel "real", ylabel "imaginary" return plot plt, plt2, layout 1, 2 end plot roots R, "F" begin R2 R R2 2 1 R 2 R2 3 1 R 3 scale abs R2 2 abs R2 3 ^2 F2 fromroots R2, var Z end plot roots R2, "G" begin filt2 coeffs F2 normalize to make filt2 1 1 filt2 filt2 1 end begin a single spike spike2 zeros Float32,30 spike2 15 1 impulse responses fir2 convolve spike2, filt2, false iir2 recursive spike2, filt2 print out println "FIR ",fir2 15 end println "IIR ",iir2 15 end end conv2 convolve input, filt2, false B Z deconv2 recursive recursive convolve conv2, filt2, true , B 1 Z filt, false , 1 S Z filt, true scale 1 S 1 Z plot stems input, "input ", blue , stems filt2 2.6, "filter ", green , stems conv2 2.6, "convolution ", red , stems deconv2, "deconvolution", blue , layout 4,1 md""" Among all filters with the same autocorrelation, the stable filter S Z , with all roots outside the unit circle, is known as the minimum phase filter. It is the filter whose energy is most concentrated at the start of the signal. """ md""" Consider two filters S Z l s\,Z \,F Z and L Z s l\,Z \,F Z where l s . The two filters have the same roots except for one root either Z 1 l s outside the unit circle or Z 1 s l inside the unit circle . The autocorrelation of the two signals is S Z \,S 1 Z L Z \,L 1 Z l^2 s^2 l\,s\,Z l\,s Z \,F Z \,F 1 Z \ . """ md""" Expanding the coefficients S Z l\,f 0 l\,f 1 s\,f 0 \,Z \cdots s\,f N 1 \,Z^N\ , L Z s\,f 0 s\,f 1 l\,f 0 \,Z \cdots l\,f N 1 \,Z^N\ . """ md""" Taking the difference of squared coefficients, we find that \begin array rcl s 0^2 l 0^2 & & l^2 s^2 \,f 0^2 \\ s 1^2 l 1^2 & & l^2 s^2 \, f 1^2 f 0^2 \\ \cdots & & \cdots \\ s n^2 l n^2 & & l^2 s^2 \, f n^2 f n 1 ^2 \\ \cdots & & \cdots \\ s N^2 l N^2 & & l^2 s^2 \,f N 1 ^2\end array """ md""" The cumulative energy difference remains non negative \sum\limits k 0 ^ n s k^2 \sum\limits k 0 ^ n l k^2 l^2 s^2 \,f n^2 \ge 0 for any n N . This proves the minimum phase property of stable signals moving a root outside the unit circle shifts the signal's energy closer to the beginning of the signal. """ S Polynomial 1, 1 R 1 , Z fromroots R 2 3 , var Z L Polynomial 1 R 1 , 1 , Z fromroots R 2 3 , var Z roots S roots L begin filters and their autocorrelations s, l real coeffs S , real coeffs L As convolution s,reverse s Al convolution l,reverse l end plot stems s 1.8, "minimum phase", blue , stems As 7.4,"autocorrelation", blue , stems l 1.8, "mixed phase", green , stems Al 7.4,"autocorrelation", green , layout 4,1 plot 0 3, cumsum s .^2 cumsum l .^2 , linewidth 3, marker diamond, xlabel "coefficient number", ylabel "cumulative energy", label L"\sum k 0 ^ n s k^2" L"\sum k 0 ^ n l k^2" md""" Looking for polynomial roots is not the best way to decompose autocorrelations. The next section presents an alternative approach. """ md""" assignment Task 1 Let us create a filter by generating a polynomial with random complex roots. """ import Random begin Random.seed 2025 for reproducibility r randn ComplexF64, 10 r vcat r, conj r add conjugate roots end md""" To create a polynomial with real coefficients, we supplement each root with its conjugate pair. """ plot roots r, "P" md""" We can see that P Z is a mixed phase filter because some roots are inside the unit circle, while others are outside. """ P fromroots r, var Z begin p coeffs P p sqrt sum p . p length p normalize energy end stems p 2, "mixed phase filter", red md""" Your task create two new filters with the same autocorrelation. 1. The minimum phase filter by replacing all roots r k inside the unit circle with 1 r k . 2. The maximum phase filter by replacing all roots r k outside the unit circle with 1 r k . Plot both filters. """ md""" assignment Task 2 Plot the cumulative energy of the three filters minimum phase, mixed phase, and maximum phase . """ md""" Wilson Burg method of spectral factorization To use recursive filtering polynomial division effectively, we need a method to extract a minimum phase stable inverse filter from the autocorrelation of a given filter.
