""" md""" Implicit diffusion In 1 D, using the simplest three point discretization of the second derivative, the operator \mathbf D corresponds to the filter D Z \displaystyle \frac 1 \Delta x ^2 \left Z^ 1 2 Z\right \ . and we can consider inversion as polynomial division. """ md""" Factoring the symmetric filter in the denominator, smoothing via implicit diffusion becomes S Z \displaystyle \frac 1 1 \alpha\,D Z \frac 1 \beta ^2 1 \beta\,Z \, 1 \beta\,Z^ 1 with suitably chosen \beta . """ function idiffuse x Vector T , β where T Real "Smoothing by one step of implicit diffusion" n length x y deepcopy x yi y 1 for i in 2 n 1 y i yi 1 β y i β yi end y n yi y n 1 β β yi 1 β for i in n 1 1 1 y i yi 1 β y i β yi end return y end md""" https i0.wp.com www.mines.edu geophysics wp content uploads sites 30 2017 02 dave hale.jpg "I write a lot of software, but here is my favorite ten line program" https github.com softwareunderground 52things blob master chapters Hale.md https github.com softwareunderground 52things blob master chapters Hale.md """ md""" The recursive implicit diffusion filter has an infinite impulse response that decays exponentially. Repeated applications of the filter converge to a Gaussian. """ md""" assignment Task 3 How can we relate the parameter \beta in the implicit diffusion filter to the parameter N in the triangle filter? """ md""" Note that Fourier response of implicit diffusion and its Taylor expansion are assuming \Delta t 1 \begin array rcl S \omega & & \displaystyle \frac 1 \beta ^2 1 2\beta\,\cos\omega \beta^2 \frac 1 \displaystyle 1 \frac 4\beta 1 \beta ^2 \,\sin^2\left \frac \omega 2 \right \\ & & \displaystyle 1 \frac \beta 1 \beta ^2 \,\omega^2 O\left \omega^4\right \end array """ md""" The Fourier response of triangle filtering and its Taylor expansion are \begin array rcl T \omega & & \displaystyle \frac 1 \cos N\omega N^2\, 1 \cos \omega \frac \sin^2\left \frac N\omega 2 \right N^2\,\sin^2\left \frac \omega 2 \right \\ & & \displaystyle 1 \frac N^2 1 \,\omega^2 12 O\left \omega^4\right \end array Compare the two Taylor expansions to find the relationship between N and \beta and express it as a function. """ β nb 0.5 MODIFY ME md""" Explicit diffusion The key to making explicit diffusion stable is to make the time step \Delta t variable. Taking another look at the box filter B N Z \frac \left 1 Z^ N \right N\, 1 Z \frac 1 N \,\left 1 Z \cdots Z^ N 1 \right \ , we can observe that the roots of the box filter B N Z are distributed along the unit circle in the complex plane """ md""" 1 Z^N \displaystyle \prod n 0 ^ N 1 \left 1 \frac Z Z n \right \ , where Z n \exp\left i\,\frac 2\pi\,n N \right \ . """ md""" Setting the radius of the box N to an odd number, for simplicity, and grouping the roots into conjugate pairs Z n and \overline Z n , we can represent this filter as \begin array rcl B N Z & & \displaystyle \displaystyle \frac 1 N \,\prod n 1 ^ N 1 \left 1 \frac Z Z n \right \\ & & \displaystyle \frac 1 N \,\prod n 1 ^ N 1 2 \left 1 \frac Z Z n \right \,\left 1 \frac Z \overline Z n \right \\ & & \displaystyle \frac 1 N \,\prod n 1 ^ N 1 2 \left 1 2\,\gamma n\,Z Z^2\right \ ,\end array """ md""" where \gamma n \cos \left \frac 2\pi\,n N \right \ . Thus, we represented the box filter as a cascade of three point elementary filters. Continuing the cascade duplicates the filter's roots, yielding an analogous representation of the triangle filter T N Z \frac 1 N^2 \,\prod n 1 ^ N 1 \left Z 1 Z 2\,\gamma n\right \ . """ md""" How does it relate to diffusion? Smoothing with multiple steps of explicit diffusion becomes a cascade of filters \begin array rcl E Z & & \displaystyle \prod n 1 ^ N \left 1 \alpha\,D Z \right \\ & & \displaystyle \prod n 1 ^ N \left 1 \frac \alpha \Delta x ^2 \,\left Z 1 Z 2\right \right \ .\end array """ md""" Comparing it to the factorization of triangle smoothing, we see a fundamental similarity, with one key difference instead of keeping \alpha constant, triangle smoothing varies \alpha with the cascade index n . This change ensures the stability of the filtering process. """ md""" Correspondingly, we can define triangle smoothing as a cascade of three point filters T N Z \displaystyle \prod n 1 ^ N 1 \left 1 \alpha n\,\left Z 1 Z 2\right \right \ , where \alpha n \displaystyle \frac 1 2\, 1 \gamma n \displaystyle \frac 1 4\,\sin^2 \left \frac \pi\,n N \right \ . """ function ediffuse x Vector T , nb Int where T Real "triangle smoothing by explicit diffusion" n length x z, y similar x , copy x inbounds for ib in 1 nb 1 α 1 4 sin π ib nb ^2 reflecting boundary conditions z 1 1 2 α y 1 α y 1 y 2 for i in 2 n 1 z i 1 2 α y i α y i 1 y i 1 end z n 1 2 α y n α y n 1 y n y, z z, y end return y end x rand 1000 btime ediffuse x,5 md""" assignment Task 4 Determine the smallest size of the smoothing radius `nb` that makes triangle smoothing implemented with recursive filtering `smooth` faster than explicit diffusion `ediffuse`. """ function stems data, label, color plt plot zeros Float32, 25 , label none, color black plot plt, data, line stem, marker circle, label label, color color, legend outerleft, markersize 3, xlim 0.5, 25.5 , ylim 1.1 minimum data 0.05,1.1 maximum data 0.05 ,border none return plt end function plot triangle spike ptk Array Plots.Plot undef, 5 pfk similar ptk tri deepcopy spike for k in 1 5 tri triangle tri, 5 ptk k stems tri, k, 0.2, " k" fspike abs. rfft tri pfk k stems vcat reverse fspike 2 end , fspike , k, 1, none end return ptk, pfk end begin ptk, pfk plot triangle spk pt plot ptk 1 , ptk 2 , ptk 3 , ptk 4 , ptk 5 , layout 5, 1 pf plot pfk 1 , pfk 2 , pfk 3 , pfk 4 , pfk 5 , layout 5, 1 plot pt, pf, layout 1, 2 , plot grid title " Time Frequency" end function plot idiffuse spike ptk Array Plots.Plot undef,5 pfk similar ptk spk deepcopy spike for k in 1 5 spk idiffuse spk, 0.5 ptk k stems spk, k, 0.35, " k" fspike abs. rfft spk pfk k stems vcat reverse fspike 2 end , fspike , k, 1.01, none end return ptk, pfk end begin pdt, pdf plot idiffuse spk pit plot pdt 1 , pdt 2 , pdt 3 , pdt 4 , pdt 5 , layout 5, 1 pif plot pdf 1 , pdf 2 , pdf 3 , pdf 4 , pdf 5 , layout 5, 1 plot pit, pif, layout 1, 2 , plot grid title " Time Frequency" end function plot ediffuse x Vector T , nb Int where T Real n length x z, y similar x , copy x plots Array Plots.Plot undef, nb plots 1 stems y, none, 1 for ib in 1 nb 1 α 1 4 sin π ib nb ^2 reflecting boundary conditions z 1 1 2 α y 1 α y 1 y 2 for i in 2 n 1 z i 1 2 α y i α y i 1 y i 1 end z n 1 2 α y n α y n 1 y n y, z z, y plots ib 1 stems y, " ib", ib 1 end return plots end begin spike3 zeros Float32, 25 spike3 13 1 end begin pek plot ediffuse spike3, 7 pe1 plot pek 1 , pek 2 , pek 3 , pek 4 , layout 4, 1 pe2 plot pek 5 , pek 6 , pek 7 , layout 4, 1 plot pe1, pe2, layout 1, 2 end md""" S. Fomel, 2022, Shaping regularization by fast explicit diffusion Second International Meeting for Applied Geoscience & Energy, 3156 3160. """ md""" Data application For the last task, we return to the time slice from a seismic volume. """ begin download "https ahay.org data hall horizon.asc","horizon.asc" xyz readdlm "horizon.asc" read data from a text file end begin n1, n2 196, 291 iline xyz 1 n1 end, 1 xline xyz 1 n1, 2 horizon reshape xyz , 3 , n1, n2 end heatmap iline, xline, horizon, xlabel L" x m ", ylabel L" y m ", title "Seismic Horizon" slice horizon 98, begin plot iline, slice, linewidth 2, ylim 55,85 , clim 55,85 , xlabel L" x m ", title "Horizontal Slice", line z slice, label none annotate 35500,73,"Channel" annotate 36750,79,"Channel" end md""" Hall, M., 2014. Smoothing surfaces and attributes. The Leading Edge, 33 2 , pp.128 129. """ md""" assignment Task 5 To emphasize the channel structure, it helps to remove minor fluctuations from the image. To do this, apply two dimensional smoothing in both x and y . You can select the appropriate smoothing program and its parameters. """ md""" assignment Bonus Task Our triangle smoothing programs assume that the smoothing radius `nb` is an integer. To increase precision, we can extend the method to accommodate a real valued radius. The analysis of the Taylor expansion around zero frequency suggests implementing this by interpolating between two triangles as follows T R Z \displaystyle \frac N 1 ^2 R^2 2\,N 1 \,T N Z \frac R^2 N^2 2\,N 1 \,T N 1 Z \ , where N is the largest integer smaller than R so that N \le R N 1 . Write a function to implement triangle smoothing with a real valued radius. """ function smooth x Vector T , R T where T Real "smoothing by triangle filtering with reflecting boundaries" nb floor Int, R largest integer smaller than R MODIFY BELOW return smooth x,nb end function plot trianglebc k1 n length k1 pk Array Plots.Plot undef, 3 hk Array Float32 undef, 3 blank zeros Float32, 40 for k in 1 3 spike deepcopy blank spike k1 k 1 spike smooth spike, 12 smax maximum spike plt plot blank, label none, color black plot plt, spike, line stem, label none, color blue, xlim 0.5, 40.5 , ylim 0.05, smax 0.05 , marker circle, border none hk k smax pk k plt end return pk, hk end begin pk, hk plot trianglebc 17,9,1 plot pk 1 , pk 2 , pk 3 , layout grid 3, 1, heights hk sum hk , margin 0.001 Plots.mm end function plot triangle2 spike pk Array Plots.Plot undef,3 spike2 deepcopy spike for k in 1 3 spike2 mapslices col smooth col, 200 , spike2 dims 1 spike2 mapslices row smooth row, 100 , spike2 dims 2 surf surface spike2, legend none, title "smooth k" cont contour spike2, legend none pk k plot surf, cont, layout 2, 1 , axis nothing end return pk end begin pk2 plot triangle2 spike2 plot pk2 1 , pk2 2 , pk2 3 , layout 1, 3 end assert smooth x,5 ≈ ediffuse x,5 btime smooth x,5 md""" Test your function by plotting impulse responses and applying the program to data examples from previous tasks. """

title: Bandpass Filtering
text:
A Pluto.jl notebook v0.20.27 frontmatter chapter 4 section 10 order 10 image "https raw.githubusercontent.com fonsp Pluto.jl 580ab811f13d565cc81ebfa70ed36c84b125f55d demo plutodemo.gif" title "Bandpass Filtering" tags "module4", "track julia", "track material", "Pluto", "PlutoUI" layout "layout.jlhtml" using Markdown using InteractiveUtils begin using PlutoUIExtra TableOfContents end using Plots, SpecialFunctions, LaTeXStrings using FFTW using Statistics import HTTP let notebook dir DIR toc path joinpath notebook dir, "TOC Notebook.jl" Markdown.parse " ⬅️ Back to Table of Contents . open?path HTTP.escapeuri toc path " end md""" Bandpass Filtering The smoothing operation we discussed earlier aims to suppress high frequencies. Bandpass filtering focuses on keeping all frequencies within a specific range while removing those outside it. How can we create an effective filter for this purpose? """ md""" Why bandpass? Bandpass filtering is needed in various situations. Consider, for example, the derivative filter. In the continuous domain, the derivative corresponds to multiplication by i\omega in the Fourier domain. In the discrete case, allowing the frequency response to increase linearly with \omega can cause problems because it produces a non periodic spectrum with a discontinuity at the maximum frequency known as the Nyquist frequency , leading to artifacts oscillations in the time domain. """ md""" A digital derivative filter must decay back to zero at the Nyquist frequency while possibly maintaining linear behavior at lower frequencies. These requirements call for low pass filtering. Specific signals may also contain unwanted low frequency components that require high pass filtering. The general bandpass filter combines low pass and high pass filtering by selecting a particular band of frequencies. """ md""" Frequency windowing The simplest form of bandpass filtering is a box window in the frequency domain. However, as we know from previous discussion, a box in one domain corresponds to an oscillating, slowly decaying function in the other domain. Indeed, consider a low pass box filter with the maximum frequency \omega 0 B \omega \left\  egin array rcl 1 & \quad\mbox for & |\omega| \le \omega 0 \ 0 & \quad\mbox for & |\omega| \omega 0\end array ight.\ . """ md""" Its time response is  egin array rcl b t & & \displaystyle  rac 1 2\pi \int\limits \omega 0 ^ \omega 0 e^ i\omega\,t \,d\omega  rac 1 \pi\,t \, rac e^ i\omega 0\,t e^ i\omega 0\,t 2i \ & & \displaystyle  rac \sin \omega 0\,t \pi t  rac \omega 0 \pi \,\mbox sinc \omega 0\,t \ .\end array """ md""" What if we tried to speed up the decrease in the time domain by multiplying the box filter's impulse response with a rapidly decreasing windowing function? For example, we could multiply b t by a Gaussian in the time domain w t b t \,g t b t \,e^  rac t^2 4\,t 0^2 \ , where t 0 is the parameter that controls the Gaussian width. """ md""" Multiplying two functions in the time domain is equivalent to convolving their Fourier transforms in the frequency domain. To prove it formally,  egin array rcl W \omega & & \displaystyle \int\limits \infty ^ \infty b t \,g t \,e^ i\omega\,t \,dt \ & & \displaystyle  rac 1 2\pi \int\limits \infty ^ \infty B 
u \int\limits \infty ^ \infty g t e^ i \omega 
u \,t \,dt\,d
u \ & & \displaystyle  rac 1 2\pi \int\limits \infty ^ \infty B 
u \,G \omega 
u \,d
u\ .\end array """ md""" Using the fact that the Fourier transform of a Gaussian is a Gaussian,  egin array rcl W \omega & & \displaystyle  rac 2\,t 0\,\sqrt \pi 2\pi \int\limits \omega 0 ^ \omega 0 e^ \omega 
u ^2\,t0^2 \,d
u \ & & \displaystyle  rac 1 \sqrt \pi \,\int\limits \omega 0 \omega \,t 0 ^ \omega \omega 0 \,t 0 e^ \xi^2 \,d\xi \ & & \displaystyle  rac \mbox erf \left \omega 0 \omega \,t 0ight \mbox erf \left \omega 0 \omega \,t 0ight 2 \ ,\end array """ md""" where \mbox erf is the error function defined as \mbox erf x \displaystyle  rac 2 \sqrt \pi \int\limits 0 ^ x e^ \xi^2 \,d\xi\ . The convolution with a Gaussian that creates the erf window is similar to the process of diffusion applied to the original square box. The more the box in the frequency domain diffuses, the shorter its impulse response in the time domain. """ sinc x sin x x gauss x, a exp x^2 4 a^2 function pt a, arrow false plt plot x sinc x gauss x,a , 25,25,linewidth 2, fill true, fillalpha 0.1 plot plt, x gauss x,a , 25,25, linewidth 2, linestyle dash, legend none if arrow plot plt, 0,2 a , 0.35,0.35 , arrow closed, color black, linewidth 2, legend none annotate plt, a, 0.45, L"2\,t 0" end return plt end box x, a erf a x 1 erf a x 1 2 function pf a, arrow false plt plot x box x,a , 2,2, color red, linewidth 2, fill true, fillalpha 0.1, legend none if arrow plot plt, 0,1 , 0.5,0.5 , arrow closed, color black, linewidth 2, legend none annotate plt, 0.5, 0.6, L"\omega 0" end return plt end plot pt 8, true , pf 8, true , pt 4 , pf 4 , layout 2, 2 , plot grid title " Time Frequency" md""" To switch from a low pass filter to a high pass filter, we can use 1 W \omega instead of W \omega . Therefore, a bandpass window with a band between \omega 1 and \omega 2 is  egin array rcl W 2 \omega & & \left 1 \displaystyle  rac \mbox erf \left \omega 1 \omega \,t 0ight \mbox erf \left \omega 1 \omega \,t 0ight 2 ight \, \ & & \displaystyle \left  rac \mbox erf \left \omega 2 \omega \,t 0ight \mbox erf \left \omega 2 \omega \,t 0ight 2 ight \ .\end array In the continuous world, a Gaussian decays quickly but never truly ends. If we want the impulse response to be strictly finite, we can replace the Gaussian in the definition of the erf function with its B spline approximation. """ md""" assignment Task 1 Let us implement bandpass filtering by windowing in the frequency domain. """ function bandpass erf x Vector T , fmin, fmax, t0, dt where T Real nt length x freq rfftfreq nt, 1 dt a 2π t0 Fx rfft x for i,f in enumerate freq if fmin none high pass filtering Fx i 1 erf a fmin f erf a fmin f 2 end if fmax none low pass filtering Fx i erf a fmax f erf a fmax f 2 end end return irfft Fx, nt end md""" For a quick test, we can generate a synthetic signal made up of several sinusoids. """ begin dt 0.004 nt 1000 t range start 0, step dt, length nt f rfftfreq nt, 1 dt end x sin. 2π 5 t 0.5 sin. 2π 10 t 2 sin. 2π 15 t begin p1 plot t, x, title "Input Signal", xlabel "Time s ", label none p2 plot f, abs. rfft x , xlim 0,20 , title "Spectrum", xlabel "Frequency Hz ", color red,label none plot p1, p2, layout 2, 1 end md""" Your task apply `bandpass erf` with appropriate parameters to the input signal so that the output contains a single sinusoid with a frequency of 10 Hz. """ md""" Butterworth filters A construction called Butterworth filtering chooses a different frequency domain function, which also behaves like a smoothed box. The function is B N \omega \displaystyle  rac 1 \displaystyle 1 \left  rac \omega \omega 0 ight ^ 2N \ . The function B N \omega is flat at the zero frequency, where it takes the value of 1, and at infinity, it takes the value of 0. The integer number N controls the transition behavior. """ md""" Butterworth, S., 1930, On the theory of filter amplifiers Wireless Engineer, 7, 536 541. """ butter x, k 1 1 x^k function plot function func, name ks 2, 4, 8, 16, 32 plot x func x,k for k in ks ,0,π, label permutedims L"N % k" for k in ks , linewidth 2, xticks 0 3, ""^3 , title " name function" plot 1, 1 , 0, 1 , color black, linestyle dash, label none annotate 1, 0.07, L"\omega 0", bottom margin 2Plots.mm end plot function butter, "Butterworth" md""" The function B N \omega has 2N poles in the complex plane distributed around the circle with the radius \omega 0 . The poles come in conjugate pairs. Therefore, we can write  egin array rcl B N \omega & & \prod\limits k 0 ^ N 1  rac 1 \displaystyle \left  rac \omega \omega k 1ight \,\left  rac \omega  ar \omega k 1ight \ & & \prod\limits k 0 ^ N 1  rac 1 \displaystyle \left  rac \omega^2 \omega 0^2 2\,\cos 	heta k \, rac \omega \omega 0 1ight \ ,\end array """ md""" where \omega k \omega 0\,e^ i\, \pi 2 \pi\,k N is the k th complex root of the equation 1 \omega \omega 0 ^ 2N 0 , and 	heta k \pi 2 \pi\,k N is its phase angle in the complex plane. """ md""" The pole based representation brings us closer to implementing Butterworth filtering in the time domain. However, we need one more step to convert the expressions involving \omega into a form suitable for digital filtering. """ md""" Recall that i\omega corresponds to the derivative operator. How can a derivative be approximated in the digital world? Let y t be the derivative of x t . Let us consider the identity x t \Delta t x t \int\limits t ^ t \Delta t y 	au \,d	au and approximate the integral by the trapezoid rule x t \Delta t \approx \displaystyle x t  rac \Delta t 2 \,\left y t y t \Delta t ight \ . """ md""" This approximation takes us to the digital world, where x t and y t are discretized with \Delta t sampling x n 1 \approx \displaystyle x n  rac \Delta t 2 \, y n y n 1 or y n \approx \displaystyle  rac 2 \Delta t \, x n 1 x n y n 1 \ . """ md""" In the Z transform notation, this iteration corresponds to the following anticausal filtering Y Z \approx \displaystyle  rac 2 \Delta t \, rac 1 Z 1 1 Z 1 \,X Z \ . Therefore, a suitable digital approximation of the derivative is i\omega \approx \displaystyle  rac 2 \Delta t \, rac 1 Z 1 1 Z 1 \ . """ md""" Recalling the dependence between \omega and Z , Z e^ i\,\omega\,\Delta t \ , we can also express the derivative approximation as i\omega \approx  rac 2 \Delta t \, rac Z^ 1 2 Z^ 1 2 Z^ 1 2 Z^ 1 2 i\, rac 2 \Delta t \,	an\left  rac \omega\,\Delta t 2 ight \ . The last approximation is known as the bilinear transform . It transforms the continuous frequency \omega living in the infinite domain on the real axis to its digital analog living in a periodic domain from \pi \Delta t to \pi \Delta t . """ omega x 2 atan x 2 begin plot x x, 4,6, linewidth 2, linestyle dash, label none hline π π , linestyle dash, color black, labels none plot omega, 4,6, linewidth 3, label L"\widehat \omega 2\,\arctan \omega 2 ", legend right, legendfontsize 12, tickfontsize 12, xlabel L"\omega", xticks π,0,π , L" \pi",L"0",L"\pi" , yticks π,0,π , L" \pi",L"0",L"\pi" end md""" note The bilinear transform is an approximation that maps frequencies from the real axis to the unit circle in the complex plane. It aids in constructing discrete versions of filters designed in the continuous domain. """ butter2 x,k 1 1 tan x 2 tan 1 2 ^k begin plot function butter2, "Digital Butterworth" annotate π, 0.07, L"\pi" end md""" By replacing \omega and \omega 0 in the definition of the Butterworth function with their bilinear approximations, we can map the Butterworth function from the infinitely continuous domain into the periodic domain and turn it into the digital filter B N Z \displaystyle \prod\limits k 0 ^ N 1 C N Z \,C N 1 Z \ , """ md""" where  egin array rcl C N Z & &  rac 1 \displaystyle \left  rac 1 	an  ar \omega k\,\Delta t 2 \,\left  rac Z 1 Z 1 ight 1ight \ & & \displaystyle \left  rac Z 1 \displaystyle  rac Z 1 	an  ar \omega k\,\Delta t 2 Z 1 ight \ .\end array Thus B N Z turns into a cascade of recursive filters, suitable for implementation in the discretized time domain. """ function butterworth lowpass Bool, x Vector T , freq, np Int where T Real "Bandpass filtering using Butterworth filters" nx length x arg 2π freq sinf, cosf sin arg , cos arg fact lowpass ? sin arg 2 cos arg 2 fact fact for j in 1 np loop over poles ss sin π 2 j 1 4 np sinf denominator filter d0, d1, d2 fact 1 ss , 2 cosf fact, 1 ss fact forward and backward convolution for range in 1 nx, nx 1 1 x1 x0 y1 y2 zero T for ix in range x2, x1, x0 x1, x0, x ix y0 lowpass ? x0 2 x1 x2 d1 y1 d2 y2 d0 x0 2 x1 x2 d1 y1 d2 y2 d0 y2, y1, x ix y1, y0, y0 end end end end function stems data, label, color plt plot zeros Float32, 41 , label none, color black plot plt, data, line stem, ylim 0.15, 1.01 , label none, xlim 0.5, 41.5 , color color, border none plot plt, data, ylim 0.15, 1.01 , label label, color color, legend outerleft, xlim 0.5, 41.5 , border none return plt end begin spike zeros Float32, 41 spike 21 one Float32 end begin ptk Array Plots.Plot undef,5 pfk similar ptk for k in 1 5 freq k 10 filtered deepcopy spike butterworth true, filtered, freq, 3 ptk k stems filtered, L"\omega 0 % 2 freq \pi", k ft abs. rfft filtered pfk k stems vcat reverse ft 2 end , ft , none, k end end begin plt plot ptk 1 , ptk 2 , ptk 3 , ptk 4 , ptk 5 , layout 5, 1 plf plot pfk 1 , pfk 2 , pfk 3 , pfk 4 , pfk 5 , layout 5, 1 plot plt, plf, layout 1, 2 , plot grid title " Time Frequency" end for k in 1 5 filtered deepcopy spike butterworth true, filtered, 0.25, k ptk k stems 2 filtered, L"N % 2 k ", k ft abs. rfft filtered pfk k stems vcat reverse ft 2 end , ft , none, k end begin plt2 plot ptk 1 , ptk 2 , ptk 3 , ptk 4 , ptk 5 , layout 5, 1 plf2 plot pfk 1 , pfk 2 , pfk 3 , pfk 4 , pfk 5 , layout 5, 1 plot plt2, plf2, layout 1, 2 , plot grid title " Time Frequency" end md""" assignment Task 2 Let us implement bandpass filtering using the Butterworth method. """ function bandpass x Vector T , fmin, fmax, np Int, dt where T Real y deepcopy x if fmax none high pass filtering butterworth false, y, fmax dt, np end ADD low pass filtering return y end md""" Modify the code above to complete the program. Test it with the synthetic data from Task 1. """ md""" assignment Task 3 Let us apply bandpass filtering to seismic data from a previous example. """ download a data file download "https ahay.org data wz data.rsf ","data.bin" begin read data as a single precision floating point matrix shot Matrix Float32 undef, 2000, 81 read "data.bin", shot end time power data, time, α data . time .^ α begin ts range 0, 4, 2000 time axis xs range 2, 2, 81 offset axis fs rfftfreq 2000, 2000 4 frequency axis end function plot seismic data, title clip quantile abs. data , 0.99 return heatmap xs, ts, data, clim clip, clip , yflip true, c grays, legend none, xlabel "Offset km ", ylabel "Time s ", title title end p 2 tpow time power shot, ts, p begin plot shot plot seismic shot, "Original Data" plot tpow plot seismic tpow, L"Time Power Gain p % p " plot plot shot, plot tpow, layout 1, 2 end spec mean abs. rfft tpow, 1 , dims 2 plot fs, spec, title "Spectrum", xlabel "Frequency Hz ", linewidth 2, label none plot fs, spec, title "Spectrum at Low Frequencies", xlabel "Frequency Hz ", linewidth 2, label none, xlim 0,50 md""" The noise surface waves has a distinctly lower frequency than the signal reflected waves . Apply high pass filtering with a 20 Hz cutoff to remove the noise and display the result. Note that the filtering should be applied trace by trace. """ md""" Dip filtering A common task in multidimensional applications is to remove data components with either low or high slopes. Since the slope is easily defined in the two dimensional Fourier domain, we can reduce the problem to band pass filtering. """ md""" For example, if the input is the seismic data in time and space u t,\mathbf x , the slope can be defined in multidimensional Fourier domain U \omega,\mathbf k as p  rac |\mathbf k | \omega \ , where \omega is temporal frequency, and \mathbf k is wavenumber. """ md""" For each frequency, dip filtering for the range from p min to p max , amounts to bandpass filtering \omega\,p min \le |\mathbf k | \le \omega\,p max and can be implemented either in the wavenumber domain or in the original space domain using the techniques from previous sections. Hale, D., and J. F. Claerbout, 1983, Butterworth dip filters Geophysics, 48, 1033 1038. """ md""" assignment Task 4 Let us implement dip filtering. """ function dipfilter x Matrix T , pmin, pmax, np Int, dt where T Real nt size x,1 freq rfftfreq nt, 1 dt Fx rfft x, 1 FFT time frequency Fr, Fi real Fx , imag Fx for i, f in enumerate freq if pmin none high pass filtering fmin pmin f dt bandpass real and imaginary parts separately butterworth false, Fr i, , fmin, np butterworth false, Fi i, , fmin, np end ADD low pass filtering end Fx complex. Fr, Fi return irfft Fx, nt, 1 inverse FFT frequency time end md""" Modify the code above to complete the program. """ md""" Derivative filters To develop a systematic way to design FIR finite impulse response derivative filters, we can utilize the Taylor expansion of the inverse sine arcsine function \arcsin x x  rac 1 2 \, rac x^3 3  rac 1 \cdot 3 2 \cdot 4 \, rac x^5 5  rac 1 \cdot 3 \cdot 5 2 \cdot 4 \cdot 6 \, rac x^7 7 \cdots """ md""" Next, we can notice that the exact derivative operator has the frequency response i\,\omega and substitute x \sin \omega\,\Delta t \displaystyle  rac 1 Z Z 2\,i into the Taylor expansion. """ md""" The expansion becomes  egin array rcl i\,\omega & & i\,\arcsin x \ 
onumber & & \displaystyle  rac 1 2 \, 1 Z Z  rac 1 2^3 \, rac 1 2 \, rac 1 Z Z ^3 3 \ & & \displaystyle  rac 1 2^5 \, rac 1 \cdot 3 2 \cdot 4 \, rac 1 Z Z ^5 5 \cdots\end array and can be truncated to define FIR filters for approximating the derivative operator. """ deriv3 x x 1 x^2 6 deriv5 x x 1 x^2 6 3 x^4 40 plot identity sin deriv3 ∘ sin deriv5 ∘ sin , 0, π 2, linewidth 2, label "exact" "order 1" "order 2" "order 3" , linestyle dash solid solid solid , xlabel "Frequency", title "Imaginary Part of the Derivative Filter" function deriv x Vector T , order where T Real "derivative filter" n length x nt t, y copy x , similar x h, trace2 for it in order 1 1 for i in 2 n 1 y i t i t i 1 t i 1 2 end y 1 , y n y 2 , y n 1 for i in 1 n t i x i y i it 2 it 1 end end y 1 t 2 t 1 for i in 2 n 1 y i t i 1 t i 1 2 end y n t n t n 1 return y end function stems2 data, label, color plt plot zeros Float32, 31 , label none, color black plot plt, data, line stem, marker circle, label label, color color, legend outerleft, xlim 0.5, 30.5 , ylim 1.05, 1.05 , border none return plt end begin spike2 zeros Float32, 31 spike2 16 1 end begin ptk2 Array Plots.Plot undef,3 for k in 1 3 order 1,2,20 k dspike deriv spike2, order ptk2 k stems2 dspike, "order order", k end end plot ptk2 1 , ptk2 2 , ptk2 3 , layout 3, 1 begin dτ 0.001 τ range start 0, step dτ, length 2^12 1 s sin. π τ example function c π cos. π τ analytical derivative end plot c 1 128 end deriv s 1 128 end , 2 dτ 128 , linestyle solid dash , label "exact derivative" "approximate derivative" function deriv error dt dts Vector Float64 undef,8 err similar dts step 1 ds dt for n in 1 8 dts n ds sn s 1 step end cn c 1 step end error cn deriv sn, 2 ds err n sqrt. sum error . error length error ds 2 step 2 end return dts, err end dts, err deriv error dτ plot dts, err, label none, linewidth 3, markershape diamond, xscale log2, yscale log2, xlabel "step size", ylabel "least squares error", title "Accuracy of Derivative Filters" md""" Hilbert transform and analytical signals In the continuous world, the Hilbert transform of a function u t is defined as h t \displaystyle  rac 1 \pi \, \int \infty ^ \infty  rac u 	au t 	au \,\mathrm d 	au\ , where the integral exists as a principal value. """ md""" The Hilbert transform of \cos t is \sin t . In signal analysis, this is used to define the complex valued analytical signal a t u t i\,h t \ . The magnitude of a t corresponds to the envelope of u t , and the derivative of its phase corresponds to the instantaneous frequency. Both are important attributes for analyzing signals. Taner, M.T., Koehler, F. and Sheriff, R.E., 1979. Complex seismic trace analysis. Geophysics, 44 6 , pp.1041 1063. """ md""" The Hilbert transform is a convolution, which corresponds in the Fourier domain to multiplication H \omega i\,\mbox sgn \omega \,U \omega \ , where \mbox sgn denotes the sign function. Because of the discontinuity of the sign function, it needs to be approximated to implement the digital Hilbert transform as a compact filter. """ md""" The construction follows the idea of using Taylor series. In this case, we can look at the Taylor expansion  egin array rcl \displaystyle  rac 1 \sqrt x & & \displaystyle  rac 1 \sqrt x 0 \left 1  rac 1 2 \,\left 1  rac x x 0 ight  rac 1 \cdot 3 2 \cdot 4 \, \left 1  rac x x 0 ight ^2 ight. \ & & \displaystyle \left.  rac 1 \cdot 3 \cdot 5 2 \cdot 4 \cdot 6 \,\left 1  rac x x 0 ight ^4 \cdots ight \end array and notice that \mbox sgn x \mbox sgn \sin x \displaystyle  rac \sin x \sqrt \sin^2 x \ . """ function hilbert x Vector T order 100 where T Real "digital Hilbert transform" n length x nt t, y deepcopy x , similar x h, trace2 c 1 2 c2 c c for it in order 1 1 y 1 t 1 t 3 2 t 2 t 1 c2 y 2 t 2 t 4 3 t 2 2 t 1 c2 for i in 3 n 2 y i t i t i 2 2 t i t i 2 c2 end y n 1 t n 1 t n 3 3 t n 1 2 t n c2 y n t n t n 2 2 t n 1 t n c2 for i in 1 n t i x i y i 2 it 1 2 it end end y 1 2 t 1 t 2 c for i in 2 n 1 y i t i 1 t i 1 c end y n 2 t n 1 t n c return y end md""" Pei, S. C., and P. H. Wang, 2001, Closed form design of maximally flat FIR Hilbert transformers, differentiators, and fractional delayers by power series expansion IEEE Trans. on Circuits and Systems, v. 48, No. 4, 389 398. """ a seismic trace download "https ahay.org data attr trace.bin","trace.bin" begin read trace trace Array Float32 undef, 1501 single precision array read "trace.bin", trace end time sampling tt range start 0, length 1501, step 0.004 plot tt, trace, title "Seismic Trace", label none, color black, xlabel "Time s ", ylabel "Amplitude" begin htrace hilbert trace envelope sqrt. trace . trace htrace . htrace end begin plot tt, trace, 0 htrace . minimum htrace , color lightgray, label "seismic trace" plot tt, 0 trace . maximum trace , htrace, color lightblue, label "Hilbert transform" plot tt, trace, htrace, xlabel "Time s ", label "analytical signal", zlabel "Hilbert Amplitude", linewidth 2, color blue end begin plot tt, trace, label none, xlabel "Time s ", ylabel "Amplitude", color black plot tt, envelope, label "envelope" plot tt, envelope, label none end md""" assignment Task 5 Compute the envelope of `tpow` from Task 3 and display the result. Note that the filtering should apply trace by trace. """ md""" assignment Bonus Task Repeat the exercise from the previous assignment to find the optimal value of the time power p , but replace varimax with the inverse semblance of the envelope. """
